We then call this method, putting a block on the same line after the call, and after any arguments to the method. You can think of the association of a block with a method as a kind of argument passing. This works on one level, but it isn‚Äôt really the whole story. The block is effectively an entire other method that can be invoked or passed forward as an argument to another method. For example:

intro/block_example.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãcall_block‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Start of method"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãyield‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãyield‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"End of method"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã call_block { puts ‚Äã"In the block"‚Äã }

Produces:

‚Äã Start of method

‚Äã In the block

‚Äã In the block

‚Äã End of method

In this example, the code in the block (puts "In the block") is executed twice, once for each call to yield passing control to the block.

You can provide arguments to yield, and they‚Äôll be passed to the block. Within the block, you list the names of the parameters to receive these arguments between vertical bars (|params...|). The following example shows a method calling its associated block twice, passing the block two arguments each time:

intro/block_example2.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãwho_says_what‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãyield‚Äã(‚Äã"Dave"‚Äã, ‚Äã"hello"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãyield‚Äã(‚Äã"Andy"‚Äã, ‚Äã"goodbye"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã who_says_what { |person, phrase| puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã says ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãphrase‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

Produces:

‚Äã Dave says hello

‚Äã Andy says goodbye

You can use code blocks to package code to implement a later callback. Code blocks can be used to pass around chunks of code. They are used throughout the Ruby standard library to allow methods to perform an action on successive elements from a collection such as an array. The act of doing something similar to all objects in a collection is called enumeration in Ruby; other languages call this iteration.

‚Äã animals = [‚Äã"ant"‚Äã, ‚Äã"bee"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"dog"‚Äã] ‚Äã# create an array‚Äã

‚Äã animals.‚Äãeach‚Äã { |animal| puts animal } ‚Äã# iterate over the contents‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã ant

‚Äã bee

‚Äã cat

‚Äã dog

Many of the looping constructs that are built into languages such as Java and JavaScript are method calls in Ruby, with the methods invoking an associated block zero or more times:

‚Äã [‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"dog"‚Äã, ‚Äã"horse"‚Äã].‚Äãeach‚Äã { |name| print name, ‚Äã" "‚Äã }

‚Äã 5.‚Äãtimes‚Äã { print ‚Äã"*"‚Äã }

‚Äã 3.‚Äãupto‚Äã(6) { |i| print i }

‚Äã (‚Äã"a"‚Äã..‚Äã"e"‚Äã).‚Äãeach‚Äã { |char| print char }

‚Äã (‚Äã"a"‚Äã..‚Äã"e"‚Äã).‚Äãeach‚Äã { print _1 }

Produces:

‚Äã cat dog horse *****3456abcdeabcde

In the first line, we ask an array to call the block once for each of its elements. Next, the object 5 calls a block five times, printing * each time. Rather than use for loops, the third example shows that in Ruby we can ask the number 3 to call a block, passing in successive values until it reaches 6. Finally, we use Ruby‚Äôs literal syntax for ranges of values to have the range of characters from a to e invoke a block using the method each. We show that example twice: once using Ruby‚Äôs normal block parameter syntax and once using Ruby‚Äôs shortcut for block parameters, which we‚Äôll see in ‚ÄãBlocks‚Äã.





Reading and ‚ÄòRiting


Ruby comes with a comprehensive library to manage input and output (I/O). But, in most of the examples in this book, we‚Äôll stick to a few simple methods. We‚Äôve already come across methods that write output: puts writes its arguments with a newline after each; p also writes its arguments but will produce more debuggable output. Both can be used to write to any I/O object, but, by default, they write to the standard output stream.

You can read input into your program in many ways. Probably the most traditional one is to use the method gets‚Äîshort for ‚Äúget string‚Äù‚Äîwhich returns the next line from your program‚Äôs standard input stream:

‚Äã line = gets

‚Äã print line

Because gets returns nil when it reaches the end of input, you can use its return value in a loop condition. Notice that in the following code the condition to the while is an assignment. We store whatever gets returns into the variable line and then test to see whether that returned value was nil or false before continuing:

‚Äã ‚Äãwhile‚Äã (line = gets)

‚Äã print line

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

In Chapter 11, ‚ÄãBasic Input and Output‚Äã, we‚Äôll talk more about how to read and write from a file or other data source.





Command-Line Arguments


When you run a Ruby program from the command line, you can pass in arguments. These are accessible from your Ruby code in two different ways.

First, the global array ARGV contains each of the arguments passed to the running program. Create a file called cmd_line.rb that contains the following:

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"You gave ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãARGV.‚Äãsize‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã arguments"‚Äã

‚Äã p ARGV

When we run it with arguments, we can see that they get passed in:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãcmd_line.rb‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãant‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãbee‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãcat‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãdog‚Äã

‚Äã You gave 4 arguments

‚Äã ["ant", "bee", "cat", "dog"]

Often, the arguments to a program are the names of files that you want to process. In this case, you can use a second technique: the variable ARGF is a special kind of I/O object that acts like all the contents of all the files whose names are passed on the command line (or standard input if you don‚Äôt pass any filenames). We‚Äôll look at that some more in ‚ÄãARGF‚Äã.





Commenting Ruby


Ruby has two ways of adding comments to source code, one of which you‚Äôll use, and the other you‚Äôll almost certainly not use. The common one is the # symbol‚Äîanything after that symbol until the end of the line is a comment and is ignored by the interpreter. If the next line continues the comment, it needs its own # symbol.

Ruby also has a rarely used multiline comment, where the first line starts with =begin and everything is a comment until the code reaches =end. Both the =begin and =end must be at the very beginning of the line, they cannot be indented.

While we did just say that Ruby ignores comments, Ruby uses a small number of ‚Äúmagic comments‚Äù for configuration options on a per-file basis. These comments have the form of # directive: value and must appear in the file before the first line of the actual Ruby code.

The most commonly used magic comment is # frozen_string_literal: true. If this directive is true, then every string literal that doesn‚Äôt have an interpolation inside it‚Äôll automatically be frozen, as though freeze was called on it.

You might also see an # encoding: VALUE directive, which specifies the encoding for string and regular expression literals inside that particular file. Ruby also has a # warn_indent: BOOLEAN flag that will throw code warnings if a file‚Äôs indentation is mismatched. There‚Äôs an experimental directive called # sharable_constant_value: which affects how values are shared using the Ractor multithreading tools.





What‚Äôs Next


We finished our lightning-fast tour of some of the basic features of Ruby. We took a look at objects, methods, strings, containers, and regular expressions. We saw some simple control structures and looked at some rather nifty iterators. We hope this chapter has given you enough ammunition to be able to attack the rest of this book.

It‚Äôs time to move on and move up‚Äîup to a higher level. Next, we‚Äôll be looking at classes and objects, things that are at the same time both the highest-level constructs in Ruby and the essential underpinnings of the entire language.



Copyright ¬© 2024, The Pragmatic Bookshelf.





Chapter 3



Classes, Objects, and Variables




From the examples we‚Äôve shown so far, you may be wondering about our earlier assertion that Ruby is an object-oriented language. Well, here is where we justify that claim. We‚Äôre going to be looking at how you create classes and objects in Ruby and at some of the ways that Ruby is more flexible than other object-oriented languages.

In ‚ÄãRuby Is an Object-Oriented Language‚Äã, we state that everything we manipulate in Ruby is an object. And every object in Ruby was instantiated either directly or indirectly from a class. In this chapter, we‚Äôll look in more depth at creating and manipulating those classes.





Defining Classes


Let‚Äôs give ourselves a simple problem to solve. Suppose we‚Äôre running a secondhand bookstore. Every week, we do stock control. A gang of clerks uses portable bar-code scanners to record every book on our shelves. Each scanner generates a comma-separated value (CSV) file containing one row for each book scanned. The row contains (among other things) the book‚Äôs ISBN and price. An extract from one of these files looks something like this: tut_classes/stock_stats/data.csv ‚Äã "Date","ISBN","Price"

‚Äã "2013-04-12","978-1-9343561-0-4",39.45

‚Äã "2013-04-13","978-1-9343561-6-6",45.67

‚Äã "2013-04-14","978-1-9343560-7-4",36.95



Our job is to take all the CSV files and work out how many of each title we have, as well as the total list price of the books in stock.

Whenever you‚Äôre designing an Object-Oriented system, a good first step is to identify the domain concepts you‚Äôre dealing with. Typically the domain concepts‚Äîwhich could represent a physical object, a process, or some other kind of entity‚Äîbecome classes in your final program, and then individual examples of those concepts are instances of these classes.

It seems pretty clear that we‚Äôll need something to represent each data reading captured by the scanners. Each instance of this class will represent a particular row of data, and the collection of all of these objects will represent all the data we‚Äôve captured.

Let‚Äôs call this class BookInStock. (Remember, class names start with an uppercase letter, and method names normally start with a lowercase letter.) Here‚Äôs how we declare that class in Ruby using the keyword class:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

As we saw in the previous chapter, we can create new instances of this class using the method ‚Äònew‚Äò:

‚Äã a_book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã another_book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã

After this code runs, we‚Äôd have two distinct objects, both instances of class BookInStock. But there‚Äôs nothing to distinguish one instance from the other, aside from the fact that they have different internal object IDs. Worse, these objects don‚Äôt yet hold any of the information we need them to hold.

The best way to fix this is to provide the class with an initialize method. This method lets us set the state of each object as it‚Äôs constructed. We store this state in instance variables inside the object. (Remember instance variables? They‚Äôre the ones that start with an @ sign.) Because each object in Ruby has its own distinct set of instance variables, each object can have its own unique state.

Here‚Äôs our updated class definition:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

The initialize method is special in Ruby programs. When you call BookInStock.new to create a new object, Ruby allocates some memory to hold an uninitialized object and then calls that object‚Äôs initialize method, passing through all arguments that were passed to new. This gives you a chance to write code that sets up your object‚Äôs state.

For class BookInStock, the initialize method takes two parameters. These parameters act like local variables within the method, so they follow the local variable naming convention of starting with a lowercase letter. But, as local variables, they‚Äôd just evaporate once the initialize method returns, so we need to transfer them into instance variables. This is common behavior in an initialize method‚Äîthe intent is to have our object set up and usable by the time initialize returns.

This method also illustrates something that often trips up newcomers to Ruby. Notice how we say @isbn = isbn. It‚Äôs easy to imagine that the two variables here, @isbn and isbn, are somehow related. It looks like they have the same name, but they don‚Äôt. The former is an instance variable, and the ‚Äúat‚Äù sign is actually part of its name.

Finally, this code illustrates a basic piece of validation. The Float method takes its argument and converts it to a floating-point number, terminating the program with an error if that conversion fails. Later in the book, we‚Äôll see other, more resilient, ways to handle these exceptional situations. (We know that we shouldn‚Äôt be holding prices in inexact old floats. Ruby has classes that hold fixed-point values exactly, but we want to look at classes, not arithmetic, in this section.) What we‚Äôre doing here is saying that we want to accept any object for the price parameter as long as that parameter can be converted to a float. We can pass in a float, an integer, or even a string containing the representation of a float, and it‚Äôll work. Let‚Äôs try this now. We‚Äôll create three objects, each with a different initial state. The p method prints out an internal representation of an object. Using it, we can see that in each case our parameters got transferred into the object‚Äôs state, ending up as instance variables: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_1.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã b1 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 3)

‚Äã p b1

‚Äã

‚Äã b2 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn2"‚Äã, 3.14)

‚Äã p b2

‚Äã

‚Äã b3 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn3"‚Äã, ‚Äã"5.67"‚Äã)

‚Äã p b3



Produces:

‚Äã #<BookInStock:0x0000000102f99720 @isbn="isbn1", @price=3.0>

‚Äã #<BookInStock:0x0000000102f99180 @isbn="isbn2", @price=3.14>

‚Äã #<BookInStock:0x0000000102f98fa0 @isbn="isbn3", @price=5.67>

Why did we use the p method to write out our objects, rather than puts? Well, let‚Äôs repeat the code using puts: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_1a.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã b1 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 3)

‚Äã puts b1

‚Äã

‚Äã b2 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn2"‚Äã, 3.14)

‚Äã puts b2

‚Äã

‚Äã b3 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn3"‚Äã, ‚Äã"5.67"‚Äã)

‚Äã puts b3



Produces:

‚Äã #<BookInStock:0x0000000104739628>

‚Äã #<BookInStock:0x0000000104739150>

‚Äã #<BookInStock:0x0000000104738fe8>

Remember, puts writes strings to your program‚Äôs standard output. When you pass it an object based on a class you wrote, it doesn‚Äôt know what to do with the object yet, so it uses a simple expedient: it writes the name of the object‚Äôs class, followed by a colon and the object‚Äôs unique object identifier, which is a hexadecimal number. It puts the whole lot inside #<...>.

Our experience tells us that during development we‚Äôll be printing out the contents of a BookInStock object many times, and the default formatting leaves something to be desired. Fortunately, Ruby has a standard message, to_s, that it sends to any object it wants to render as a string. The default behavior of to_s, defined in the Object class, is the ClassName, then the colon, and then the object ID behavior we just described. So, when we pass one of our BookInStock objects to puts, the puts method calls to_s in that object to get its string representation.

If we want a different behavior, we can override the default implementation of to_s to give us a better rendering of our objects (we‚Äôll talk more about how this works in Chapter 6, ‚ÄãSharing Functionality: Inheritance, Modules, and Mixins‚Äã): tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_2.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"ISBN: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@isbn‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã, price: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@price‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã b1 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 3)

‚Äã puts b1

‚Äã b2 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn2"‚Äã, 3.14)

‚Äã puts b2

‚Äã b3 = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn3"‚Äã, ‚Äã"5.67"‚Äã)

‚Äã puts b3



Produces:

‚Äã ISBN: isbn1, price: 3.0

‚Äã ISBN: isbn2, price: 3.14

‚Äã ISBN: isbn3, price: 5.67

The p method actually has a different method it calls on objects, and that method is named inspect. The difference is that inspect is designed to produce a representation that‚Äôs useful to a developer when debugging, and to_s is supposed to produce a human-readable one for more general output.

Something‚Äôs going on here that‚Äôs both trivial and profound. See how the values we set into the instance variables @isbn and @price in the initialize method are subsequently available in the to_s method? That shows how instance variables work‚Äîthey‚Äôre stored with each object and available to all the instance methods of those objects.





Objects and Attributes


The BookInStock objects we‚Äôve created so far have an internal state (the ISBN and price). That state is private to those objects‚Äîno other object can access an object‚Äôs instance variables. In general, this is a Good Thing. It means that the object is solely responsible for maintaining its own consistency. (We feel obligated to note here that there‚Äôs no such thing as perfect privacy in Ruby, and you shouldn‚Äôt depend on Ruby‚Äôs language privacy for security purposes.) A totally secretive object is pretty useless‚Äîyou can create it, but then you can‚Äôt do anything with it. You‚Äôll normally define methods that let you access and manipulate the state of an object, allowing the outside world to interact with the object. These externally visible facets of an object are called its attributes.

For our BookInStock objects, the first thing we may need is the ability to find out the ISBN and price (so we can count each distinct book and perform price calculations). One way of doing that is to write accessor methods: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_3.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãisbn‚Äã

‚Äã @isbn

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprice‚Äã

‚Äã @price

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ..‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 12.34)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"ISBN = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãisbn‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã ISBN = isbn1

‚Äã Price = 12.34

Here we‚Äôve defined two accessor methods to return the values of the two instance variables. The method isbn, for example, returns the value of the instance variable @isbn because the last (and only) thing executed in the method is the expression that evaluates the @isbn variable. Later, in ‚ÄãMethod Bodies‚Äã, we‚Äôll look at a shorter syntax for declaring one-line methods.

As far as other objects are concerned, there‚Äôs no difference between calling these attribute accessor methods or calling any other method. This is great because it means that the internal implementation of the object can change without the other objects needing to be aware of the change.

Because writing accessor methods is such a common idiom, Ruby provides convenient shortcuts.

The method attr_reader creates these attribute reader methods for you: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_4.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã attr_reader ‚Äã:isbn‚Äã, ‚Äã:price‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ..‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 12.34)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"ISBN = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãisbn‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã ISBN = isbn1

‚Äã Price = 12.34

This is the first time we‚Äôve used symbols in this chapter. As we discussed in ‚ÄãSymbols‚Äã, symbols are a convenient way of referencing a name. In this code, you can think of :isbn as meaning the name isbn and of plain isbn as meaning the value of the variable. In this example, we named the accessor methods isbn and price. The corresponding instance variables are @isbn and @price. These accessor methods are identical to the ones we wrote by hand earlier‚Äîthey‚Äôll return the value of the instance variable whose name matches the name of the accessor method. These methods only allow you to read the attribute, but not to change it.

There‚Äôs a common misconception that the attr_reader declaration actually declares instance variables. It doesn‚Äôt. It creates the accessor methods, but the variables themselves don‚Äôt need to be declared. An instance variable pops into existence when you assign a value to it, and any instance value that hasn‚Äôt been assigned a value returns nil when accessed. Ruby completely decouples instance variables and accessor methods, as we‚Äôll see in ‚ÄãAttributes Are Just Methods without Arguments‚Äã.





Writing to Attributes


Sometimes you need to be able to set an attribute from outside the object. For example, let‚Äôs assume that we have to discount the price of some titles after reading in the raw scan data.

In other languages like C# and Java that restrict access to instance variables, you‚Äôd do this with setter functions:

‚Äã ‚Äã// Java code‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã JavaBookInStock {

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdouble‚Äã _price;

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äãdouble‚Äã ‚ÄãgetPrice‚Äã() {

‚Äã ‚Äãreturn‚Äã _price;

‚Äã }

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äãvoid‚Äã ‚ÄãsetPrice‚Äã(‚Äãdouble‚Äã newPrice) {

‚Äã _price = newPrice;

‚Äã }

‚Äã }

‚Äã b = ‚Äãnew‚Äã JavaBookInStock(....);

‚Äã b.setPrice(calculate_discount(b.getPrice()));

In Ruby, the attributes of an object can be accessed via the getter method, and that access looks the same as any other method. We saw this earlier with phrases such as book.isbn. So, it seems natural that setting an attribute‚Äôs value looks like a normal variable assignment such as book.isbn = "new isbn". You enable that assignment by creating a Ruby method whose name ends with an equals sign. A method so named can be used as the target of assignments: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_5.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã attr_reader ‚Äã:isbn‚Äã, ‚Äã:price‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprice‚Äã=(new_price)

‚Äã @price = new_price

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 33.80)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"ISBN = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãisbn‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã book.‚Äãprice‚Äã = book.‚Äãprice‚Äã * 0.75 ‚Äã# discount price‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"New price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã ISBN = isbn1

‚Äã Price = 33.8

‚Äã New price = 25.349999999999998

The assignment book.price = book.price * 0.75 invokes the method price= in the book object, passing it the discounted price as an argument. If you create a method whose name ends with an equals sign, that name can appear on the left side of an assignment. (The Ruby parser will ignore whitespace between the end of the name and the equals sign, which is how book.price = gets parsed to the method named price=.) You can even treat the setter method like a regular method invocation if you want‚Äîbook.price = 1.50 is identical to the somewhat odder-looking book.price=(1.50).

Again, Ruby provides a shortcut for creating these simple attribute-setting methods. If you want a write-only accessor, you can use the form attr_writer, but that‚Äôs fairly rare. You‚Äôre far more likely to want both a reader and a writer for a given attribute, so you‚Äôll use the handy-dandy attr_accessor method: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_6.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã attr_reader ‚Äã:isbn‚Äã

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:price‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 33.80)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"ISBN = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãisbn‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã book.‚Äãprice‚Äã = book.‚Äãprice‚Äã * 0.75 ‚Äã# discount price‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"New price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã ISBN = isbn1

‚Äã Price = 33.8

‚Äã New price = 25.349999999999998

In this example, the line attr_accessor :price creates both the getter method that allows you to write puts book.price and the setter method that allows you to write book.price = book.price * 0.75





Attributes Are Just Methods without Arguments


These attribute-accessing methods don‚Äôt have to be just mere wrappers around an object‚Äôs instance variables. For example, you may want to access the price as an exact number of cents rather than as a floating-point number of dollars.

tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_7.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã attr_reader ‚Äã:isbn‚Äã

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:price‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã

‚Äã (price * 100).‚Äãround‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 33.80)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price in cents = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Price = 33.8

‚Äã Price in cents = 3380

We multiply the floating-point price by 100 to get the price in cents and then use the round method to convert it to an integer. Why? Because floating-point numbers don‚Äôt always have an exact internal representation. When we multiply 33.8 by 100, we get 3379.99999999999954525265. The Integer method would truncate this to 3379. Calling round ensures we get the best integer representation. This is a good example of why you want to use BigDecimal, not Float, in financial calculations. See Chapter 26, ‚ÄãLibrary Reference: Core Data Types‚Äã, for more on BigDecimal.

We can take this even further and create a writing method parallel to the reader method, mapping the value to the instance variable internally: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock_8.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã attr_reader ‚Äã:isbn‚Äã

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:price‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã

‚Äã (price * 100).‚Äãround‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã=(cents)

‚Äã @price = cents / 100.0

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã book = BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"isbn1"‚Äã, 33.80)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price in cents = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã book.‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã = 1234

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Price in cents = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãbook.‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã Price = 33.8

‚Äã Price in cents = 3380

‚Äã Price = 12.34

‚Äã Price in cents = 1234

Here we‚Äôve used attribute methods to create a virtual instance variable. To the outside world, price_in_cents seems to be an attribute like any other. Internally, though, it has no corresponding instance variable.

This is more than a curiosity. In his landmark book, Object-Oriented Software Construction, Bertrand Meyer calls this the Uniform Access Principle. By hiding the difference between instance variables and calculated values, you‚Äôre shielding the rest of the world from the implementation of your class. You‚Äôre free to change how things work in the future without impacting the millions of lines of code that use your class‚Äîfor example, you could switch from a float to a BigDecimal and the users of this class would never need to know. This is a big win.





Attributes, Instance Variables, and Methods


The previous section‚Äôs description of attributes may leave you thinking that they‚Äôre nothing more than methods‚Äîwhy‚Äôd we need to invent a fancy name for them? In a way, that‚Äôs absolutely right. An attribute is just a method. Sometimes an attribute simply returns the value of an instance variable. Sometimes an attribute returns the result of a calculation. And sometimes those funky methods with equals signs at the end of their names are used to update the state of an object. So, the question is, where do attributes stop and regular methods begin? What makes something an attribute and not just a plain old method? Ultimately, that‚Äôs one of those ‚Äúhow many angels can fit on the head of a pin‚Äù questions. Here‚Äôs a personal take.

When you design a class, you decide what internal state it has and also how that state is to appear on the outside to users of your class. The internal state is held in instance variables. The external state is exposed through methods we‚Äôre calling attributes. And the other actions your class can perform are just regular methods. It isn‚Äôt a crucially important distinction, but by calling the external state of an object its attributes, you‚Äôre helping clue people in on how they should view the class you‚Äôve written.





Classes Working with Other Classes


Our original challenge was to read in data from multiple CSV files and produce various simple reports. So far, all we have is BookInStock, a class that represents the data for one book.

During object-oriented design, you identify external things and make them classes in your code. But there‚Äôs another source of classes in your designs‚Äîthe classes that correspond to things inside your code itself. For example, we know that the program we‚Äôre writing will need to consolidate and summarize CSV data feeds. But that‚Äôs a passive statement. Let‚Äôs turn it into a design by asking ourselves what does the summarizing and consolidating. And the answer (in our case) is a CSV reader. Let‚Äôs make it into a class as follows:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã CsvReader

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãread_in_csv_data‚Äã(csv_file_name)

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtotal_value_in_stock‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãnumber_of_each_isbn‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

We‚Äôd call it using something like this:

‚Äã reader = CsvReader.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã reader.‚Äãread_in_csv_data‚Äã(‚Äã"file1.csv"‚Äã)

‚Äã reader.‚Äãread_in_csv_data‚Äã(‚Äã"file2.csv"‚Äã)

‚Äã : : :

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Total value in stock = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãreader.‚Äãtotal_value_in_stock‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

We need to be able to handle multiple CSV files, so our reader object needs to accumulate the values from each CSV file it is fed. We‚Äôll do that by keeping an array of values in an instance variable. And how shall we represent each book‚Äôs data? Well, we just finished writing the BookInStock class, so that problem is solved. The only other question is how we parse data in a CSV file. Fortunately, Ruby comes with a good CSV library, which we‚Äôll cover in detail in Chapter 29, ‚ÄãLibrary Reference: Input, Output, Files, and Formats‚Äã. Given a CSV file with a header line, we can iterate over the remaining rows and extract values by name: tut_classes/stock_stats/csv_reader.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã CsvReader

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã

‚Äã @books_in_stock = []

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãread_in_csv_data‚Äã(csv_file_name)

‚Äã CSV.‚Äãforeach‚Äã(csv_file_name, ‚Äãheaders: ‚Äã‚Äãtrue‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |row|

‚Äã @books_in_stock << BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(row[‚Äã"ISBN"‚Äã], row[‚Äã"Price"‚Äã])

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Because you‚Äôre probably wondering what‚Äôs going on, let‚Äôs dissect that read_in_csv_data method. On the first line, we tell the CSV library to open the file with the given name. The headers: true option tells the library to do two things. One is to parse the first line of the file as the names of the columns. The other is to parse each row into a hash with the column names as the keys and the row values as the values.

The library then reads the rest of the file, passing each row in turn to the block (the code between do and end). Inside the block, we extract the data from the ISBN and Price columns and use that data to create a new BookInStock object. We then append that object to an instance variable called @books_in_stock (the << operator does different things in Ruby, in this case, it means ‚Äúappend to an array‚Äù). And where does that variable come from? It‚Äôs an array that we created in the initialize method.

Again, this is the pattern you want to aim for. Your initialize method sets up an environment for your object, leaving it in a usable state. Other methods then use that state.

If you encounter an error along the lines of ‚Äú‚ÄòFloat‚Äô: can‚Äôt convert nil into Float (TypeError)‚Äù when you run this code, you likely have extra spaces at the end of the header line in your CSV data file. The CSV library is pretty strict about the formats it accepts.

Let‚Äôs turn this from a code fragment into a working program. We‚Äôre going to organize our source into three files. The first, book_in_stock.rb, will contain the definition of the class BookInStock. The second, csv_reader.rb, is the source for the CsvReader class. Finally, a third file, stock_stats.rb, is the main driver program. We‚Äôll start with book_in_stock.rb: tut_classes/stock_stats/book_in_stock.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã BookInStock

‚Äã attr_reader ‚Äã:isbn‚Äã, ‚Äã:price‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(isbn, price)

‚Äã @isbn = isbn

‚Äã @price = Float(price)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã

‚Äã (price * 100).‚Äãround‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



We‚Äôre keeping the price_in_cents method so we can do money arithmetic without accumulating floating-point errors.

Here‚Äôs the csv_reader.rb file. The CsvReader class has two external dependencies, which are the standard CSV library and the BookInStock class that‚Äôs in the file book_in_stock.rb. Ruby has a couple of helper methods that let us load external files.

tut_classes/stock_stats/csv_reader.rb

‚Äã require ‚Äã"csv"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"book_in_stock"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã CsvReader

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã

‚Äã @books_in_stock = []

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãread_in_csv_data‚Äã(csv_file_name)

‚Äã CSV.‚Äãforeach‚Äã(csv_file_name, ‚Äãheaders: ‚Äã‚Äãtrue‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |row|

‚Äã @books_in_stock << BookInStock.‚Äãnew‚Äã(row[‚Äã"ISBN"‚Äã], row[‚Äã"Price"‚Äã])

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtotal_value_in_stock‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# later we'll see easier ways to sum a collection‚Äã

‚Äã sum = 0.0

‚Äã @books_in_stock.‚Äãeach‚Äã { |book| sum += book.‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã }

‚Äã sum / 100.0

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãnumber_of_each_isbn‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

In this file, we use the require method to load in the Ruby CSV library from the Ruby standard library. We also use require_relative to load in the book_in_stock file we wrote. We use require_relative for this because the location of the file we‚Äôre loading is easiest to define relative to the file we‚Äôre loading it from‚Äîthey‚Äôre both in the same directory.

We‚Äôre using price_in_cents to compute the total value.

And finally, here‚Äôs our main program, in the file stock_stats.rb: tut_classes/stock_stats/stock_stats.rb ‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"csv_reader"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã reader = CsvReader.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ARGV.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |csv_file_name|

‚Äã $stderr.‚Äãputs‚Äã ‚Äã"Processing ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcsv_file_name‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã reader.‚Äãread_in_csv_data‚Äã(csv_file_name)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Total value = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãreader.‚Äãtotal_value_in_stock‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Again, this file uses require_relative to bring in the library it needs (in this case, the csv_reader.rb file). It uses the ARGV variable to access the program‚Äôs command-line arguments, loading CSV data for each file specified on the command line.

We can run this program using the CSV data that we used in the ‚Äãcode‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãstock_stats.rb‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãdata.csv‚Äã

‚Äã Processing data.csv

‚Äã Total value = 122.07

Do we need three source files for this? Not necessarily. But as your programs grow (and almost all programs grow over time), you‚Äôll find that large files start to get cumbersome. You‚Äôll also find it harder to write automated tests against the code if it‚Äôs in a monolithic chunk. Finally, you won‚Äôt be able to reuse classes if they‚Äôre all bundled into the final program. As a result, it‚Äôs fairly common to only have one Ruby class per individual file.

Let‚Äôs get back to our discussion of classes.





Specifying Access Control


When designing the interface for a class, it‚Äôs important to consider how much of your class you‚Äôll expose to the outside world. Allow too much access into your class, and you risk increasing the amount that different classes depend on each other‚Äôs internal implementation, which is called coupling. Users of your class will be tempted to rely on details of your class‚Äôs implementation rather than on its logical interface. The good news is that the only easy way to change an object‚Äôs state in Ruby is by calling one of its methods. If you control access to the methods, you control access to the object. A good rule of thumb is never to expose methods that could leave an object in an invalid state.

Ruby gives you three levels of access control:

Public methods can be called by anyone‚Äîno access control is enforced. Methods are public by default (except for initialize, which is always private).



Protected methods can be invoked only by objects of the defining class and its subclasses. Access is kept within the family. We‚Äôll talk more about subclasses in Chapter 6, ‚ÄãSharing Functionality: Inheritance, Modules, and Mixins‚Äã.



Private methods cannot be called with an explicit receiver‚Äîthe receiver is always the current object, also known as self. This means that private methods can be called only in the context of the current object, and with self as the explicit receiver or with the implicit receiver. You can‚Äôt invoke another object‚Äôs private methods with normal dot syntax. (But there are ways around this using metaprogramming tools that we‚Äôll discuss in Chapter 22, ‚ÄãThe Ruby Object Model and Metaprogramming‚Äã)





The difference between ‚Äúprotected‚Äù and ‚Äúprivate‚Äù is fairly subtle and is different in Ruby than in other common object-oriented languages. If a method is protected, it may be called by any instance of the defining class or its subclasses. If a method is private, it may be called only within the context of the calling object‚Äîit‚Äôs never possible to access another object‚Äôs private methods directly, even if the object is of the same class as the caller. In practice, it‚Äôs somewhat rare to see ‚Äúprotected‚Äù in use.

Access control in Ruby is determined dynamically, as the program runs, not statically when the program is compiled or interpreted. You‚Äôll get an access violation only when the code attempts to execute the restricted method.

You specify access levels to methods within the class or module definitions using one or more of the three access methods: public, protected, and private. You can use each function in three different ways.

If called with no arguments, the three functions set the default access control of subsequently defined methods. This is probably familiar behavior if you‚Äôre a C# or Java programmer, where you‚Äôd use keywords such as public to achieve the same effect. Although this usage looks like a keyword, in Ruby, the access control is actually a method.

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MyClass

‚Äã ‚Äã# default is "public"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod1‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# This method is public‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprotected‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# subsequent methods will be "protected"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod2‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# This method is protected‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# subsequent methods will be private"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod3‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# This method is private‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# subsequent methods will be "public"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod4‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# this method is public‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Since the default access for methods is public, it‚Äôs rare to use public explicitly to denote access control.

As a matter of style, the methods after the call to an access method like public are typically not indented‚Äîyou aren‚Äôt defining a block, only the access status of subsequent methods.

Alternatively, you can set access levels of named methods by listing them as arguments to the access control functions:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MyClass

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod1‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod2‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ... and so on‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äã:method1‚Äã, ‚Äã:method4‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprotected‚Äã ‚Äã:method2‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äã:method3‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

This mechanism is somewhat rare in practice, but it does enable the third way to declare access in Ruby.

We‚Äôve mentioned that most statements in Ruby return a value. In particular, defining a method with def returns a value‚Äîthe name of the new method as a symbol. As a result, you can declare access directly preceding a method definition.

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MyClass

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod1‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# This method is public‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprotected‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod2‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# This method is protected‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod3‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# This method is private‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod4‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# This method is public‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

What‚Äôs happening here is that the def method2 statement is returning the value :method2, which is immediately passed as an argument to protected, resulting in protected :method2, and making that method, and only that method, protected. Access declared this way doesn‚Äôt propagate down the file, it only applies to the method that the access modifier directly precedes.

We prefer this last form because it‚Äôs much more explicit about the access level of each method. That said, the first form is older, and currently more common in code you‚Äôre likely to see.

It‚Äôs time for some examples. Perhaps we‚Äôre modeling an accounting system where every debit has a corresponding credit. Because we want to ensure that no one can break this rule, we‚Äôll make the methods that do the debits and credits private, and we‚Äôll define our external interface in terms of transactions.

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Account

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:balance‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(balance)

‚Äã @balance = balance

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Transaction

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(account_a, account_b)

‚Äã @account_a = account_a

‚Äã @account_b = account_b

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtransfer‚Äã(amount)

‚Äã debit(@account_a, amount)

‚Äã credit(@account_b, amount)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãdebit‚Äã(account, amount)

‚Äã account.‚Äãbalance‚Äã -= amount

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãcredit‚Äã(account, amount)

‚Äã account.‚Äãbalance‚Äã += amount

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã savings = Account.‚Äãnew‚Äã(100)

‚Äã checking = Account.‚Äãnew‚Äã(200)

‚Äã

‚Äã transaction = Transaction.‚Äãnew‚Äã(checking, savings)

‚Äã transaction.‚Äãtransfer‚Äã(50)

Protected access is used when objects need to access the internal state of other objects of the same class. For example, we may want to allow two individual Account objects to compare their balances directly but to hide those balances from the rest of the world (perhaps because we present them in a different form):

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Account

‚Äã ‚Äãprotected‚Äã attr_reader ‚Äã:balance‚Äã ‚Äã# accessor method 'balance' but make it protected‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãgreater_balance_than?‚Äã(other)

‚Äã @balance > other.‚Äãbalance‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Because balance is protected, it‚Äôs available only within Account objects.





Variables


Now that we‚Äôve gone through the trouble of creating all these objects, let‚Äôs make sure we don‚Äôt lose them. Variables are used to keep track of objects; each variable holds a reference to an object. Let‚Äôs confirm this with some code:

‚Äã person = ‚Äã"Tim"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"The object in 'person' is a ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson.‚Äãclass‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"The object has an id of ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson.‚Äãobject_id‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"and a value of '‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã'"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã The object in 'person' is a String

‚Äã The object has an id of 60

‚Äã and a value of 'Tim'

On the first line, Ruby creates a new string object with the value Tim. A reference to this object is placed in the local variable person. A quick check shows that the variable has indeed taken on the personality of a string, with a class, an object ID, and a value.

So, is a variable an object? In Ruby, the answer is ‚Äúno.‚Äù A variable is simply a reference to an object. Objects float around in a big pool somewhere (the operating system‚Äôs heap, most of the time) and are pointed to by variables. Let‚Äôs make the example slightly more complicated:

‚Äã person1 = ‚Äã"Tim"‚Äã

‚Äã person2 = person1

‚Äã person1[0] = ‚Äã'J'‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"person1 is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson1‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"person2 is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson2‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã person1 is Jim

‚Äã person2 is Jim

What happened here? We changed the first character of person1 (Ruby strings are mutable, unlike Java‚Äôs), but both person1 and person2 changed from Tim to Jim.

It all comes back to the fact that variables hold references to objects, not the objects themselves. Assigning person1 to person2 doesn‚Äôt create any new objects; it simply copies person1‚Äôs object reference to person2 so that both person1 and person2 refer to the same object, as shown in the illustration.



Assignment aliases objects, potentially giving you multiple variables that reference the same object.

Can‚Äôt this cause problems in your code? It can, but not as often as you‚Äôd think (objects in Java, for example, work exactly the same way). In the previous example, for instance, you could avoid aliasing by using the dup method of String, which creates a new string object with identical contents:

‚Äã person1 = ‚Äã"Tim"‚Äã

‚Äã person2 = person1.‚Äãdup‚Äã

‚Äã person1[0] = ‚Äã"J"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"person1 is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson1‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"person2 is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãperson2‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã person1 is Jim

‚Äã person2 is Tim

You can also prevent anyone from changing a particular object by freezing it. Attempt to alter a frozen object, and Ruby will raise a RuntimeError exception:

‚Äã person1 = ‚Äã"Tim"‚Äã

‚Äã person2 = person1

‚Äã person1.‚Äãfreeze‚Äã ‚Äã# prevent modifications to the object‚Äã

‚Äã person2[0] = ‚Äã"J"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã from prog.rb:4:in `<main>'

‚Äã prog.rb:4:in `[]=': can't modify frozen String: "Tim" (FrozenError)

Numbers and symbols are always frozen in Ruby, so those values are always immutable.





Reopening Classes


While we‚Äôre talking about classes in Ruby, we feel like we should at least mention one of the most unique features of Ruby‚Äôs class structure: the ability to reopen a class definition and add new methods or variables to it at any time, even classes that are part of the third-party tools or the standard library.

In other words, if you have something like this in Ruby:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Book

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:title‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# and a bunch of other stuff‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Later, you can do this:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Book

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãuppercase_title‚Äã

‚Äã title.‚Äãupcase‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

If you declare class Book and a Book class already exists, Ruby won‚Äôt give an error, and the new definitions in the second declaration will be added to the existing class. This is true even if the existing class is part of Ruby itself. This process of reopening classes to add or change methods is colloquially known as monkey-patching.

Typically, you‚Äôd only extend a class like this if the original class isn‚Äôt part of your code, but it‚Äôs reasonably common in Ruby to use this method to add utility functions to core classes or the standard library. Ruby on Rails, for example, does this a lot.

To give an example, Ruby on Rails defines a method called squish, which clears excessive whitespace in a string, so if you have this:

‚Äã ‚Äã"This string has whitespace"‚Äã

It becomes "This string has whitespace." By monkey-patching, Rails can define the method like this:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã String

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãsquish‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# implementation‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

And then call it using str.squish like any other method.

The alternative, which many other languages use, is to define a utility class or classes and define a class method on them, which looks like this:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã StringUtilities

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãsquish‚Äã(str)

‚Äã ‚Äã# implementation‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Which you would then call with StringUtilities.squish(str).

This example shows the advantage of allowing classes to reopen‚Äîthe ability to add and easily use utility methods is convenient. It‚Äôs nice to not have to know which methods are defined by Rails and which of the many possible utility classes a method might be in.

That said, this is something to be done with caution‚Äîmany teams don‚Äôt allow it in their own code without a clear reason. And you should be wary of using monkey-patching to change the behavior of existing methods, rather than adding new methods as we did here. Monkey-patching can make the behavior of code unpredictable. It can be hard to tell where behavior is defined, and these changes are global, meaning that if two files define the same method, the last defined one will win, leading to potentially hard-to-find bugs.

Later in Chapter 22, ‚ÄãThe Ruby Object Model and Metaprogramming‚Äã, we‚Äôll talk about refinements, a Ruby feature that gives you the benefit of reopening classes, but also limits the scope of your changes.





What‚Äôs Next


There‚Äôs more to say about classes and objects in Ruby. We still have to look at class methods and concepts such as mixins and inheritance. We‚Äôll do that in Chapter 6, ‚ÄãSharing Functionality: Inheritance, Modules, and Mixins‚Äã. But, for now, know that everything you manipulate in Ruby is an object and that objects start their lives as instances of classes. And one of the most common things we do with objects is to create collections of them. But that‚Äôs the subject of our next chapter.



Copyright ¬© 2024, The Pragmatic Bookshelf.





Chapter 4



Collections, Blocks, and Iterators




Most real programs have to manage collections of data: the people in a course, the songs in your playlist, the books in the store, and so on. Ruby comes with two classes that are commonly used to handle these collections: arrays and hashes. A Ruby array is an ordered collection of data. A Ruby hash is a key/value pair, equivalent to a Python dictionary, a Java Map, or a JavaScript object. Mastery of these two classes, and their large interfaces, is an important part of being an effective Ruby programmer.

But it isn‚Äôt only these two classes that give Ruby its power when dealing with collections. Ruby also has a block syntax that lets you encapsulate chunks of code. When paired with collections, these blocks can build powerful iterator constructs. In this chapter, we‚Äôll look at the two collection classes as well as the blocks and iterators.





Arrays


The class Array holds a collection of object references. Each object reference occupies a position in the array, identified by an integer index. You can create arrays by using literals or by explicitly creating an Array object. A literal array is a comma-delimited list of objects between square brackets:

‚Äã a = [3.14159, ‚Äã"pie"‚Äã, 99]

‚Äã a.‚Äãclass‚Äã ‚Äã# => Array‚Äã

‚Äã a.‚Äãlength‚Äã ‚Äã# => 3‚Äã

‚Äã a[0] ‚Äã# => 3.14159‚Äã

‚Äã a[1] ‚Äã# => "pie"‚Äã

‚Äã a[2] ‚Äã# => 99‚Äã

‚Äã a[3] ‚Äã# => nil‚Äã

You can create an empty array with either [] or by directly calling Array.new:

‚Äã b = Array.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã b.‚Äãclass‚Äã ‚Äã# => Array‚Äã

‚Äã b.‚Äãlength‚Äã ‚Äã# => 0‚Äã

‚Äã b[0] = ‚Äã"second"‚Äã

‚Äã b[1] = ‚Äã"array"‚Äã

‚Äã b ‚Äã# => ["second", "array"]‚Äã

As the example shows, array indices start at zero. Index an array with a non-negative integer, and it returns the object at that position, or it returns nil if nothing is there. Index an array with a negative integer, and it counts from the end, with -1 being the last element of the array.

‚Äã a = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]

‚Äã a[-1] ‚Äã# => 9‚Äã

‚Äã a[-2] ‚Äã# => 7‚Äã

‚Äã a[-99] ‚Äã# => nil‚Äã

The following diagram shows array access in a different way:



Arrays are accessed using the [] operator. As with most Ruby operators, this operator is implemented as a method, specifically, an instance method of class Array. The last two lines of this example are equivalent:

‚Äã a = [3.14159, ‚Äã"pie"‚Äã, 99]

‚Äã a[0] ‚Äã# => 3.14159‚Äã

‚Äã a.‚Äã[‚Äã](0) ‚Äã# => 3.14159‚Äã

The last line of code treats [] as a normal method. In practice, you wouldn‚Äôt write code like the last line, we just wanted to show how flexible Ruby is.

You can index arrays with a pair of numbers, [start, count]. This returns a new array consisting of references to count the number of objects starting at position start:

‚Äã a = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]

‚Äã a[1, 3] ‚Äã# => [3, 5, 7]‚Äã

‚Äã a[3, 1] ‚Äã# => [7]‚Äã

‚Äã a[-3, 2] ‚Äã# => [5, 7]‚Äã

You can also index arrays using ranges, in which the start and end positions are separated by two or three dots. The two-dot form includes the end position; the three-dot form doesn‚Äôt. We‚Äôll talk more about ranges in Chapter 7, ‚ÄãBasic Types: Numbers, Strings, and Ranges‚Äã.

‚Äã a = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]

‚Äã a[1..3] ‚Äã# => [3, 5, 7]‚Äã

‚Äã a[1...3] ‚Äã# => [3, 5]‚Äã

‚Äã a[3..3] ‚Äã# => [7]‚Äã

‚Äã a[-3..-1] ‚Äã# => [5, 7, 9]‚Äã

The [] operator has a corresponding []= operator, which lets you set elements in the array. If used with a single integer index, the element at that position is replaced by whatever is on the right side of the assignment. Any gaps that result will be filled with nil:

‚Äã a = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9] ‚Äã#=> [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[1] = ‚Äã'bat'‚Äã ‚Äã#=> [1, "bat", 5, 7, 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[-3] = ‚Äã'cat'‚Äã ‚Äã#=> [1, "bat", "cat", 7, 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[3] = [9, 8] ‚Äã#=> [1, "bat", "cat", [9, 8], 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[6] = 99 ‚Äã#=> [1, "bat", "cat", [9, 8], 9, nil, 99]‚Äã

Again, []= is a regular method, and you could write it as a.[]=(index, new_value).

If the index to []= is two numbers (a start and a length) or a range, then those elements in the original array are replaced by whatever is on the right side of the assignment. If the length of the selected elements on the left is zero, the right side is inserted into the array before the start position; no elements are removed. If the right side is itself an array, its elements are used in the replacement. The array size is automatically adjusted if the index selects a different number of elements than are available on the right side of the assignment.

‚Äã a = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9] ‚Äã#=> [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[2, 2] = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã ‚Äã#=> [1, 3, "cat", 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[2, 0] = ‚Äã"dog"‚Äã ‚Äã#=> [1, 3, "dog", "cat", 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[1, 1] = [9, 8, 7] ‚Äã#=> [1, 9, 8, 7, "dog", "cat", 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[0..3] = [] ‚Äã#=> ["dog", "cat", 9]‚Äã

‚Äã a[5..6] = 99, 98 ‚Äã#=> ["dog", "cat", 9, nil, nil, 99, 98]‚Äã

In the line a[2, 2] = "cat", the subarray starting at index 2 and of length 2, which is [5, 7], is replaced by cat. In the next line, the subarray [2, 0] is of length 0, so dog is inserted at index 2. Then the subarray represented by [1, 1], which is [3], is replaced by [9, 8, 7] being inserted in the array. Notice that the entire right-side array isn‚Äôt inserted as one element, rather each element in the right-hand side is inserted individually. The last two lines are similar, but they use ranges instead of a start and a length.

It‚Äôs common to create arrays of short words, but that can be a pain, what with all the quotes and commas. Fortunately, Ruby has a shortcut, %w, which does just what we want:

Instead of this:

‚Äã a = [‚Äã"ant"‚Äã, ‚Äã"bee"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"dog"‚Äã, ‚Äã"elk"‚Äã]

‚Äã a[0] ‚Äã# => "ant"‚Äã

‚Äã a[3] ‚Äã# => "dog"‚Äã

You can use %w followed by a delimiter and then by space-separated individual words.

‚Äã a = ‚Äã%w[ant bee cat dog elk]‚Äã

‚Äã a[0] ‚Äã# => "ant"‚Äã

‚Äã a[3] ‚Äã# => "dog"‚Äã

You can use any character after %w as the delimiter. If it‚Äôs something with a pair, like a bracket or parenthesis, then the array will continue until the other side of the pair. If you don‚Äôt use a pair, the array will continue until it reaches the same character again.

If you want an array of symbols instead of strings, Ruby has a similar %i shortcut:

‚Äã a = ‚Äã%i[ant bee cat dog elk]‚Äã

‚Äã a[0] ‚Äã# => :ant‚Äã

‚Äã a[3] ‚Äã# => :dog‚Äã

Arrays have a large number of other useful methods. Using them, you can treat arrays as stacks, sets, queues, dequeues, and first-in-first-out (FIFO) queues. (Ruby also has a dedicated Set class, which we‚Äôll cover in Chapter 28, ‚ÄãLibrary Reference: Enumerators and Containers‚Äã.)

For example, push and pop add and remove elements from the end of an array, so you can use the array as a stack:

‚Äã stack = []

‚Äã stack.‚Äãpush‚Äã ‚Äã"red"‚Äã

‚Äã stack.‚Äãpush‚Äã ‚Äã"green"‚Äã

‚Äã stack.‚Äãpush‚Äã ‚Äã"blue"‚Äã

‚Äã stack ‚Äã# => ["red", "green", "blue"]‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã stack.‚Äãpop‚Äã ‚Äã# => "blue"‚Äã

‚Äã stack.‚Äãpop‚Äã ‚Äã# => "green"‚Äã

‚Äã stack.‚Äãpop‚Äã ‚Äã# => "red"‚Äã

‚Äã stack ‚Äã# => []‚Äã

Similarly, unshift and shift add and remove elements from the beginning of an array. Combine shift and push, and you have a first-in-first-out (FIFO) queue.

‚Äã queue = []

‚Äã queue.‚Äãpush‚Äã ‚Äã"red"‚Äã

‚Äã queue.‚Äãpush‚Äã ‚Äã"green"‚Äã

‚Äã queue.‚Äãshift‚Äã ‚Äã# => "red"‚Äã

‚Äã queue.‚Äãshift‚Äã ‚Äã# => "green"‚Äã

The first and last methods return (but don‚Äôt remove) the n entries at the head or end of an array. If you don‚Äôt pass an argument, the default number is one.

‚Äã array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]

‚Äã array.‚Äãfirst‚Äã ‚Äã# => 1‚Äã

‚Äã array.‚Äãfirst‚Äã(4) ‚Äã# => [1, 2, 3, 4]‚Äã

‚Äã array.‚Äãlast‚Äã ‚Äã# => 7‚Äã

‚Äã array.‚Äãlast‚Äã(4) ‚Äã# => [4, 5, 6, 7]‚Äã

We‚Äôll look at more array methods later on in ‚ÄãArray‚Äã.





Hashes


Hashes (sometimes known as associative arrays, maps, or dictionaries) are similar to arrays in that they are indexed collections of object references. But, while you index arrays with integers, you index a hash with objects of any type, most often symbols and strings but also regular expressions or anything else in Ruby. When you store a value in a hash, you actually supply two objects: the index, which is called the key, and the value, or entry, to be stored with that key. You can subsequently retrieve the entry by indexing the hash with the same key value that you used to store it.

Why Are They Called Hashes?



The data structure that Ruby calls a Hash‚Äîwhere an arbitrary key is an index to an arbitrary value‚Äîhas different names in different programming languages. The most generic term is probably key-value store. You‚Äôll also see them called dictionaries (because the values are looked up based on the keys), maps (because the individual keys are mapped to individual values), or associative arrays (because they associate keys with values).

The name ‚ÄúHash‚Äù (or the related Java term ‚ÄúHashMap‚Äù) is named after an implementation detail. The keys are stored in memory based on a function that returns a (hopefully) unique value for each object. Because the location of each key can be found without referring to the entire object, lookup is fast. The function that returns the unique value is called a hashing function and so the data structure is called a Hash.



The example that follows uses hash literals (a list of key/value pairs between braces), and then it uses square bracket syntax to access the value at each key for both retrieving and setting the value:

‚Äã h = {‚Äã"dog"‚Äã => ‚Äã"canine"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã => ‚Äã"feline"‚Äã, ‚Äã"bear"‚Äã => ‚Äã"ursine"‚Äã}

‚Äã

‚Äã h.‚Äãlength‚Äã ‚Äã# => 3‚Äã

‚Äã h[‚Äã"dog"‚Äã] ‚Äã# => "canine"‚Äã

‚Äã h[‚Äã"cow"‚Äã] = ‚Äã"bovine"‚Äã

‚Äã h[12] = ‚Äã"dodecine"‚Äã

‚Äã h[‚Äã"cat"‚Äã] = 99

‚Äã h ‚Äã# => {"dog"=>"canine", "cat"=>99, "bear"=>"ursine", "cow"=>"bovine",‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# .. 12=>"dodecine"}‚Äã

In the previous example, the hash keys were strings, and the hash literal used => to separate the keys from the values. (The => is sometimes called a hashrocket.) If the keys are symbols, then you can use a shortcut. You can still use => to separate symbol keys from values:

‚Äã h = {‚Äã:dog‚Äã => ‚Äã"canine"‚Äã, ‚Äã:cat‚Äã => ‚Äã"feline"‚Äã, ‚Äã:bear‚Äã => ‚Äã"ursine"‚Äã}

You can also write the literal by moving the colon to the end of the symbol and dropping the =>.

‚Äã h = { ‚Äãdog: ‚Äã‚Äã"canine"‚Äã, ‚Äãcat: ‚Äã‚Äã"feline"‚Äã, ‚Äãbear: ‚Äã‚Äã"ursine"‚Äã}

Because the value of a symbol doesn‚Äôt change, symbols are often used as hash keys, and so this shortcut is very common.

You can use an even shorter shortcut. Often when creating a new hash, you‚Äôre using existing data stored in variables that share the same name as the key that the variable will be indexed under in the hash. Something like this:

‚Äã firstname = ‚Äã"Fred"‚Äã

‚Äã lastname = ‚Äã"Flintstone"‚Äã

‚Äã user = {‚Äãfirstname: ‚Äãfirstname, ‚Äãlastname: ‚Äãlastname}

‚Äã puts user

Produces:

‚Äã {:firstname=>"Fred", :lastname=>"Flintstone"}

You don‚Äôt need to duplicate the key and the value if they have the same name:

‚Äã firstname = ‚Äã"Fred"‚Äã

‚Äã lastname = ‚Äã"Flintstone"‚Äã

‚Äã user = {firstname:, lastname:}

‚Äã puts user

Produces:

‚Äã {:firstname=>"Fred", :lastname=>"Flintstone"}

Ruby will infer that the value should come from a variable with the same name as the key. If you try to use a key shortcut and no such local variable exists, Ruby will throw an error.

Compared with arrays, hashes have one significant advantage: they can use any object as an index. And you‚Äôll find something that might be surprising: Ruby remembers the order in which you add items to a hash. When you subsequently iterate over the entries, Ruby will return them in that order.

You‚Äôll find that hashes are one of the most commonly used data structures in Ruby. Later, Chapter 5, ‚ÄãMore about Methods‚Äã, lists more of the methods implemented by class Hash.





Digging


Often data isn‚Äôt simply a single hash or array but comes in a complex package that combines hashes and arrays. Accessing data in a complicated structure can be a pain, but Ruby provides a shortcut with the dig method.

The dig method, which is defined for Array, Hash, and Struct, allows you to ‚Äúdig‚Äù through a complicated data structure in a single command.

‚Äã data = {

‚Äã ‚Äãmcu: ‚Äã[

‚Äã {‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"Iron Man"‚Äã, ‚Äãyear: ‚Äã2010, ‚Äãactors: ‚Äã[‚Äã"Robert Downey Jr."‚Äã, ‚Äã"Gwyneth Paltrow"‚Äã]}

‚Äã ],

‚Äã ‚Äãstarwars: ‚Äã[

‚Äã {‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"A New Hope"‚Äã, ‚Äãyear: ‚Äã1977, ‚Äãactors: ‚Äã[‚Äã"Mark Hamill"‚Äã, ‚Äã"Carrie Fisher"‚Äã]}

‚Äã ]

‚Äã }

‚Äã data[‚Äã:mcu‚Äã][0][‚Äã:actors‚Äã][1] ‚Äã# => "Gwyneth Paltrow"‚Äã

‚Äã data.‚Äãdig‚Äã(‚Äã:mcu‚Äã, 0, ‚Äã:actors‚Äã, 1) ‚Äã# => "Gwyneth Paltrow"‚Äã

The biggest advantage of using dig is that if an element isn‚Äôt in the data structure, the method returns nil and doesn‚Äôt raise an exception.





Word Frequency: Using Hashes and Arrays


Let‚Äôs round out this discussion of hashes and arrays with a program that calculates the number of times each word occurs in some text. (So, for example, in this sentence, the word the occurs two times.)

The problem breaks down into two parts. First, given some text as a string, return a list of words. That sounds like an array. Then, build a count for each distinct word. That sounds like a use for a hash‚Äîwe can index it with the word and use the corresponding entry to keep a count.

Let‚Äôs start with the method that splits a string into words:

tut_containers/word_freq/words_from_string.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãwords_from_string‚Äã(string)

‚Äã string.‚Äãdowncase‚Äã.‚Äãscan‚Äã(‚Äã/[\w']+/‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

This method uses two useful string methods: downcase, which returns a lowercase version of a string, and scan, which returns an array of substrings that match a given pattern. In this case, the pattern is [\w‚Äô]+, which matches sequences containing ‚Äúword characters‚Äù and single quotes.

We can play with this method. Notice how the result is an array, and notice that the words are in lowercase and the punctuation is gone:

‚Äã p words_from_string(‚Äã"I like Ruby, it is (usually) optimized for programmer happiness"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã ["i", "like", "ruby", "it", "is", "usually", "optimized", "for", "programmer",

‚Äã "happiness"]

Our next task is to calculate word frequencies. To do this, we‚Äôll create a hash object indexed by the words in our list. Each entry in this hash stores the number of times that word occurred. Let‚Äôs say we‚Äôve already read part of the list, and we‚Äôve seen the word the already. Then we‚Äôd have a hash that contained this data:

‚Äã {..., ‚Äã"the"‚Äã => 1, ...}

If the variable next_word contains the word the, then incrementing the count is as simple as setting the hash to increment the value at that key:

‚Äã counts[next_word] += 1

We‚Äôd then end up with a hash containing the following:

‚Äã {..., ‚Äã"the"‚Äã => 2, ...}

Our only problem is what to do when we encounter a word for the first time. If we try to increment the entry for that word, there won‚Äôt be one, so our program will fail. This problem has several solutions. One is to check whether the entry exists before doing the increment:

‚Äã ‚Äãif‚Äã counts.‚Äãkey?‚Äã(next_word)

‚Äã counts[next_word] += 1

‚Äã ‚Äãelse‚Äã

‚Äã counts[next_word] = 1

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

But there‚Äôs a tidier way. If we create a hash object using Hash.new(0), the parameter, 0 in this case, will be used as the hash‚Äôs default value‚Äîit‚Äôll be the value returned if you look up a key that isn‚Äôt yet in the hash. Using that, we can write our count_frequency method:

tut_containers/word_freq/count_frequency.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãcount_frequency‚Äã(word_list)

‚Äã counts = Hash.‚Äãnew‚Äã(0)

‚Äã word_list.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |word|

‚Äã counts[word] += 1

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã counts

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã p count_frequency([‚Äã"sparky"‚Äã, ‚Äã"the"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"on"‚Äã, ‚Äã"the"‚Äã, ‚Äã"mat"‚Äã])

Produces:

‚Äã {"sparky"=>1, "the"=>2, "cat"=>1, "sat"=>1, "on"=>1, "mat"=>1}

We haven‚Äôt talked about loops or blocks yet, but each takes a block argument and executes the code inside the block once for each element in the array, in this case, checking the hash for each word and incrementing the count associated with that word.

We have one little job left. The hash containing the word frequencies is ordered based on the first time it sees each word. It would be better to display the results based on the frequencies of the words. We can do that using the hash‚Äôs sort_by method. When you use sort_by, you give it a block that tells the sort what to use when making comparisons. In our case, we‚Äôll use the count. The result of the sort is an array containing a set of two-element arrays, with each subarray corresponding to a key/entry pair in the original hash. This makes our whole program look like this:

tut_containers/word_freq/ugly_word_count.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"words_from_string"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"count_frequency"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã raw_text = ‚Äã"The problem breaks down into two parts. First, given some text‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãas a string, return a list of words. That sounds like an array. Then, build‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãa count for each distinct word. That sounds like a use for a hash---we can‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãindex it with the word and use the corresponding entry to keep a count."‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã word_list = words_from_string(raw_text)

‚Äã counts = count_frequency(word_list)

‚Äã sorted = counts.‚Äãsort_by‚Äã { |word, count| count }

‚Äã top_five = sorted.‚Äãlast‚Äã(5)

‚Äã

‚Äã top_five.‚Äãreverse_each‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |word, count|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãword‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcount‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã a: 6

‚Äã the: 3

‚Äã that: 2

‚Äã sounds: 2

‚Äã like: 2

Note that the sorted array is low to high, so we use last to take the last five elements of the array, meaning the one with the highest count, and then we use reverse_each to iterate them highest to lowest.

At this point, a quick test may be in order to validate our code. These tests are going to be valuable in a moment because we‚Äôre going to change that code into a more commonly used Ruby and we want to make sure the behavior doesn‚Äôt change.

To do this, we‚Äôre going to use a testing framework called Minitest that comes with the standard Ruby distributions. We won‚Äôt describe it fully yet (we‚Äôll do that in Chapter 13, ‚ÄãTesting Ruby Code‚Äã). For now, we‚Äôll say that the class MiniTest::Test brings in testing functionality, including the method assert_equal, which checks that its two parameters are equal and complains bitterly if they aren‚Äôt. We‚Äôll use assertions to test our two methods, one method at a time. (That‚Äôs one reason why we wrote them as separate methods‚Äîit makes them testable in isolation.)

Here are some tests for the word_from_string method:

tut_containers/word_freq/test_words_from_string.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"words_from_string"‚Äã

‚Äã require ‚Äã"minitest/autorun"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestWordsFromString < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_empty_string‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal([], words_from_string(‚Äã""‚Äã))

‚Äã assert_equal([], words_from_string(‚Äã" "‚Äã))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_single_word‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal([‚Äã"cat"‚Äã], words_from_string(‚Äã"cat"‚Äã))

‚Äã assert_equal([‚Äã"cat"‚Äã], words_from_string(‚Äã" cat "‚Äã))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_many_words‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal(

‚Äã [‚Äã"the"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"on"‚Äã, ‚Äã"the"‚Äã, ‚Äã"mat"‚Äã],

‚Äã words_from_string(‚Äã"the cat sat on the mat"‚Äã)

‚Äã )

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_ignores_punctuation‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal(

‚Äã [‚Äã"the"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat's"‚Äã, ‚Äã"mat"‚Äã],

‚Äã words_from_string(‚Äã"<the!> cat's, -mat-"‚Äã)

‚Äã )

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Run options: --seed 39197

‚Äã # Running:

‚Äã

‚Äã ....

‚Äã Finished in 0.000420s, 9523.8109 runs/s, 14285.7164 assertions/s.

‚Äã

‚Äã 4 runs, 6 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

The test starts by requiring the source file containing our words_from_string method, along with the unit test framework itself. It then defines a test class. Within that class, any methods whose names start with test are automatically run by the testing framework. The results show that four test methods ran, successfully executing six assertions.

We can also test that our count of word frequency works:

tut_containers/word_freq/test_count_frequency.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"count_frequency"‚Äã

‚Äã require ‚Äã"minitest/autorun"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestCountFrequency < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_empty_list‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal({}, count_frequency([]))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_single_word‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal({‚Äã"cat"‚Äã => 1}, count_frequency([‚Äã"cat"‚Äã]))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_two_different_words‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal({‚Äã"cat"‚Äã => 1, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã => 1}, count_frequency([‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã]))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_two_words_with_adjacent_repeat‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal({‚Äã"cat"‚Äã => 2, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã => 1}, count_frequency([‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã]))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_two_words_with_non_adjacent_repeat‚Äã

‚Äã assert_equal({‚Äã"cat"‚Äã => 2, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã => 1}, count_frequency([‚Äã"cat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"sat"‚Äã, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã]))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Run options: --seed 56174

‚Äã # Running:

‚Äã

‚Äã .....

‚Äã Finished in 0.000503s, 9940.3581 runs/s, 9940.3581 assertions/s.

‚Äã

‚Äã 5 runs, 5 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

In previous editions of the book, we stopped here. But, since then, the Ruby Standard Library has evolved, and the Array class now has a tally method that does exactly what our count_frequency method does. We can use tally instead:

tut_containers/word_freq/better_word_count.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"words_from_string"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã raw_text = ‚Äã"The problem breaks down into two parts. First, given some text‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãas a string, return a list of words. That sounds like an array. Then, build‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãa count for each distinct word. That sounds like a use for a hash---we can‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãindex it with the word and use the corresponding entry to keep a count."‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã word_list = words_from_string(raw_text)

¬ª counts = word_list.‚Äãtally‚Äã

‚Äã sorted = counts.‚Äãsort_by‚Äã { |word, count| count }

‚Äã top_five = sorted.‚Äãlast‚Äã(5)

‚Äã

‚Äã top_five.‚Äãreverse_each‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |word, count|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãword‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcount‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã a: 6

‚Äã the: 3

‚Äã that: 2

‚Äã sounds: 2

‚Äã like: 2

And we get the same answer.





Blocks and Enumeration


In our program that wrote out the results of our word frequency analysis, we had the following loop:

‚Äã top_five.‚Äãreverse_each‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |word, count|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãword‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcount‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

The method reverse_each is an example of an iterator‚Äîa general term for a method that invokes a block of code repeatedly. Ruby also uses the term enumerator for such a method.

The most general iterator in Ruby is each, which takes a block and invokes the block once for each element in the collection. In this case, we‚Äôre using reverse_each, a shortcut method that invokes the block once for each element of the list, but in reverse order.

Enumerator methods can have different behaviors beyond just executing the block of code. A Ruby programmer might use a different enumerator method called map to write the code more compactly. For example:

‚Äã puts top_five.‚Äãreverse‚Äã.‚Äãmap‚Äã { |word, count| ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãword‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcount‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

The map applies its block to each element of the array in turn, returning a new array made up of the result of each invocation of the block.

Now the whole example looks like this:

tut_containers/word_freq/best_word_count.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"words_from_string"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã raw_text = ‚Äã"The problem breaks down into two parts. First, given some text‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãas a string, return a list of words. That sounds like an array. Then, build‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãa count for each distinct word. That sounds like a use for a hash---we can‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãindex it with the word and use the corresponding entry to keep a count."‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã word_list = words_from_string(raw_text)

‚Äã counts = word_list.‚Äãtally‚Äã

‚Äã sorted = counts.‚Äãsort_by‚Äã { |word, count| count }

‚Äã top_five = sorted.‚Äãlast‚Äã(5)

‚Äã puts top_five.‚Äãreverse‚Äã.‚Äãmap‚Äã { |word, count| ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãword‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcount‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

Produces:

‚Äã a: 6

‚Äã the: 3

‚Äã that: 2

‚Äã sounds: 2

‚Äã like: 2

The map method is now taking each element of our top_five array and converting it to a new array made of the strings that come as the result of executing the block.

Because each local variable is only used as the receiver of the next message, you could chain all the values together and get something like this:

tut_containers/word_freq/bester_word_count.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"words_from_string"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã raw_text = ‚Äã"The problem breaks down into two parts. First, given some text‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãas a string, return a list of words. That sounds like an array. Then, build‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãa count for each distinct word. That sounds like a use for a hash---we can‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãindex it with the word and use the corresponding entry to keep a count."‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts words_from_string(raw_text)

‚Äã .‚Äãtally‚Äã

‚Äã .‚Äãsort_by‚Äã { |word, count| count }

‚Äã .‚Äãlast‚Äã(5)

‚Äã .‚Äãreverse‚Äã

‚Äã .‚Äãmap‚Äã { |word, count| ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãword‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcount‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

Produces:

‚Äã a: 6

‚Äã the: 3

‚Äã that: 2

‚Äã sounds: 2

‚Äã like: 2

In this example, each message returns a new collection of data that‚Äôs processed by the next message until we finally return the list of strings that‚Äôs sent to puts.

You may wonder how to debug that long chain of methods if something isn‚Äôt working and you want to determine what each individual step is. Ruby provides a method called tap that‚Äôs designed to allow you to ‚Äútap into‚Äù this kind of method pipeline. All tap does is take a block, pass the receiver into the block, and then return the original receiver of the method. (From the perspective of the method pipeline, this does nothing‚Äîthe receiver calls tap, and then the same object is returned to receive the next method in the chain.) So tap is a no-op, except that it does invoke a block.

That block could have a side effect, such as printing a value to the console for debugging purposes:

tut_containers/word_freq/bester_word_count_with_tap.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"words_from_string"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã raw_text = ‚Äã"The problem breaks down into two parts. First, given some text‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãas a string, return a list of words. That sounds like an array. Then, build‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãa count for each distinct word. That sounds like a use for a hash---we can‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãindex it with the word and use the corresponding entry to keep a count."‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts words_from_string(raw_text)

‚Äã .‚Äãtally‚Äã

‚Äã .‚Äãsort_by‚Äã { |word, count| count }

‚Äã .‚Äãtap‚Äã { |result| puts ‚Äã"sorted tally: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãresult‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã\n\n‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

‚Äã .‚Äãlast‚Äã(5)

‚Äã .‚Äãtap‚Äã { |result| puts ‚Äã"only the last five: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãresult‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã\n\n‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

‚Äã .‚Äãreverse‚Äã

‚Äã .‚Äãtap‚Äã { |result| puts ‚Äã"reversed: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãresult‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã\n\n‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

‚Äã .‚Äãmap‚Äã { |word, count| ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãword‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãcount‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã }

Produces:

‚Äã sorted tally: [["words", 1], ["an", 1], ["array", 1], ["then", 1], ["build", 1],

‚Äã ["each", 1], ["distinct", 1], ["hash", 1], ["we", 1], ["can", 1], ["index", 1],

‚Äã ["it", 1], ["with", 1], ["and", 1], ["corresponding", 1], ["entry", 1], ["to",

‚Äã 1], ["keep", 1], ["problem", 1], ["breaks", 1], ["down", 1], ["of", 1], ["list",

‚Äã 1], ["return", 1], ["string", 1], ["into", 1], ["two", 1], ["parts", 1], ["as",

‚Äã 1], ["first", 1], ["given", 1], ["text", 1], ["some", 1], ["use", 2], ["word",

‚Äã 2], ["for", 2], ["count", 2], ["like", 2], ["sounds", 2], ["that", 2], ["the",

‚Äã 3], ["a", 6]]

‚Äã

‚Äã only the last five: [["like", 2], ["sounds", 2], ["that", 2], ["the", 3], ["a",

‚Äã 6]]

‚Äã
