We could do more testing on our Roman class, but let‚Äôs move on. We‚Äôve only scratched the surface of the set of assertions available inside the testing framework. For example, for every positive assertion (such as assert_equal) there‚Äôs a negative refutation (such as refute_equal).

The final parameter to every assertion is an optional message that will be output before any failure message. This normally isn‚Äôt needed because the failure messages are normally pretty reasonable. The one exception is the assertion refute_nil, where the default message ‚ÄúExpected nil to not be nil‚Äù doesn‚Äôt help much. In that case, you may want to add some annotation of your own. (This code assumes the existence of some kind of User class.)

‚Äã require ‚Äã'minitest/autorun'‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã ATestThatFails < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_user_created‚Äã

‚Äã user = User.‚Äãfind‚Äã(1)

‚Äã refute_nil(user, ‚Äã"User with ID=1 should exist"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Run options: --seed 16917

‚Äã # Running:

‚Äã

‚Äã F

‚Äã Finished in 0.000252s, 3968.2549 runs/s, 3968.2549 assertions/s.

‚Äã

‚Äã 1) Failure:

‚Äã ATestThatFails#test_user_created [prog.rb:11]:

‚Äã User with ID=1 should exist.

‚Äã Expected nil to not be nil.

‚Äã

‚Äã 1 runs, 1 assertions, 1 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips





Structuring Tests


Earlier we asked you to ignore the scaffolding around our tests. Now it‚Äôs time to look at it.

You include the testing framework facilities in your unit by including minitest/autorun.

‚Äã require ‚Äã"minitest/autorun"‚Äã

The minitest/autorun module includes minitest itself, which has most of the features we‚Äôve talked about so far. It also includes an alternate minitest/spec syntax that‚Äôs more like RSpec and the minitest/mock mock object package. (We‚Äôre not going to talk about minitest/spec syntax in this book. If you want that style of syntax, we recommend actually using RSpec.) Finally, it calls Minitest.autorun, which starts the test runner. This is why our test files have been executing tests when invoked just as plain Ruby files.

Unit tests are often combined into high-level groupings, called test cases. The test cases generally contain all the tests relating to a particular facility or feature‚Äîin Ruby, often each application class will have one associated test case. Within the test case, you‚Äôll typically want to organize your assertions into separate test methods, where each method contains the assertions for one type of test; one method could check regular number conversions, another could test error handling, and so on. (We‚Äôll see later that RSpec allows you to structure tests a little bit differently.) The classes that represent test cases must be subclasses of Minitest::Test. The methods that hold the assertions must have names that start with test_. This is important: the testing framework dynamically searches the test methods to find tests to run, and only methods whose names start with test_ are eligible.

Quite often you‚Äôll find that all the test methods within a test case start by setting up a particular scenario. Each test method then probes some aspect of that scenario. Finally, each method may then tidy up after itself. For example, we could be testing a class that extracts jukebox playlists from a database. (The playlist_builder file contains a DBI class that simulates a database connection for our purposes here.) unittesting/test_playlist_builder1.rb ‚Äã require ‚Äã"minitest/autorun"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"playlist_builder"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestPlaylistBuilder < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_empty_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã database = DBI.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"DBI:mysql:playlists"‚Äã)

‚Äã playlist_builder = PlaylistBuilder.‚Äãnew‚Äã(database)

‚Äã assert_empty(playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã)

‚Äã playlist_builder.‚Äãclose‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_artist_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã database = DBI.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"DBI:mysql:playlists"‚Äã)

‚Äã playlist_builder = PlaylistBuilder.‚Äãnew‚Äã(database)

‚Äã playlist_builder.‚Äãinclude_artist‚Äã(‚Äã"krauss"‚Äã)

‚Äã refute_empty(playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã, ‚Äã"Playlist shouldn't be empty"‚Äã)

‚Äã playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |entry|

‚Äã assert_match(‚Äã/krauss/i‚Äã, entry.‚Äãartist‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã playlist_builder.‚Äãclose‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_title_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã database = DBI.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"DBI:mysql:playlists"‚Äã)

‚Äã playlist_builder = PlaylistBuilder.‚Äãnew‚Äã(database)

‚Äã playlist_builder.‚Äãinclude_title‚Äã(‚Äã"midnight"‚Äã)

‚Äã refute_empty(playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã, ‚Äã"Playlist shouldn't be empty"‚Äã)

‚Äã playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |entry|

‚Äã assert_match(‚Äã/midnight/i‚Äã, entry.‚Äãtitle‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã playlist_builder.‚Äãclose‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã Run options: --seed 19023

‚Äã # Running:

‚Äã

‚Äã ...

‚Äã Finished in 0.000397s, 7556.6751 runs/s, 115869.0188 assertions/s.

‚Äã

‚Äã 3 runs, 46 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

Each test starts by connecting to a database and creating a new playlist builder. Each test ends by disconnecting from the database. The idea of using a real database in unit tests is questionable because unit tests are supposed to be fast-running, context-independent, and easy to set up, but it illustrates a point. (And that said, Ruby on Rails makes database calls in its unit tests all the time.) We can extract all this common code into setup and teardown methods. Within a Minitest::Test class, if a method called setup exists, it‚Äôll be run before each and every test method, and if a method called teardown exists, it‚Äôll be run after each test method finishes. The setup and teardown methods bracket each test rather than being run only once for the entire test case. This is shown in the code that follows: unittesting/test_playlist_builder2.rb ‚Äã require ‚Äã"minitest/autorun"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"playlist_builder"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestPlaylistBuilder < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãsetup‚Äã

‚Äã @database = DBI.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"DBI:mysql:playlists"‚Äã)

‚Äã @playlist_builder = PlaylistBuilder.‚Äãnew‚Äã(@database)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãteardown‚Äã

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãclose‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_empty_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã assert_empty(@playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_artist_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãinclude_artist‚Äã(‚Äã"krauss"‚Äã)

‚Äã refute_empty(@playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã, ‚Äã"Playlist shouldn't be empty"‚Äã)

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |entry|

‚Äã assert_match(‚Äã/krauss/i‚Äã, entry.‚Äãartist‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_title_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãinclude_title‚Äã(‚Äã"midnight"‚Äã)

‚Äã refute_empty(@playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã, ‚Äã"Playlist shouldn't be empty"‚Äã)

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |entry|

‚Äã assert_match(‚Äã/midnight/i‚Äã, entry.‚Äãtitle‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã Run options: --seed 17223

‚Äã # Running:

‚Äã

‚Äã ...

‚Äã Finished in 0.000437s, 6864.9875 runs/s, 105263.1415 assertions/s.

‚Äã

‚Äã 3 runs, 46 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

Inside the teardown method, you can detect whether the preceding test succeeded with the passed? method.





Creating Mock Objects in Minitest


Minitest allows you to create mock objects, which are objects that simulate the API of an existing object in the system, typically providing a canned response instead of a more expensive or fragile real response. A minitest mock object can be verified, meaning it‚Äôll raise a failure if the methods you expected to be called were not called during the test.

Using these mock object expectations allows for a style of testing where, instead of testing the result of a method by verifying its output, you test the behavior of the method by verifying that it makes expected calls to other methods.

In minitest, a mock object is created like any other Ruby object. Then you add the methods you wish the mock to respond to via the expect method. At the end, you can optionally test that all expected methods were called with verify.

For example, we can re-write our playlist builder test so that we don‚Äôt need to create a ‚Äúreal‚Äù DBI instance. (The word real is in scare quotes because, for this contrived example, even the DBI instance in the previous code was faked‚Ä¶.) Behind the scenes, our playlist builder calls connect and disconnect on the DBI instance.

We can instead create a mock object: unittesting/test_playlist_builder_mock.rb ‚Äã require ‚Äã"minitest/autorun"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"playlist_builder"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestPlaylistBuilder < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãsetup‚Äã

‚Äã @database = Minitest::Mock.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã @database.‚Äãexpect‚Äã(‚Äã:connect‚Äã, ‚Äãtrue‚Äã)

‚Äã @database.‚Äãexpect‚Äã(‚Äã:disconnect‚Äã, ‚Äãfalse‚Äã)

‚Äã @playlist_builder = PlaylistBuilder.‚Äãnew‚Äã(@database)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãteardown‚Äã

‚Äã @database.‚Äãdisconnect‚Äã

‚Äã @database.‚Äãverify‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_empty_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã assert_empty(@playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_artist_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãinclude_artist‚Äã(‚Äã"krauss"‚Äã)

‚Äã refute_empty(@playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã, ‚Äã"Playlist shouldn't be empty"‚Äã)

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |entry|

‚Äã assert_match(‚Äã/krauss/i‚Äã, entry.‚Äãartist‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest_title_playlist‚Äã

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãinclude_title‚Äã(‚Äã"midnight"‚Äã)

‚Äã refute_empty(@playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã, ‚Äã"Playlist shouldn't be empty"‚Äã)

‚Äã @playlist_builder.‚Äãplaylist‚Äã.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |entry|

‚Äã assert_match(‚Äã/midnight/i‚Äã, entry.‚Äãtitle‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã Run options: --seed 61004

‚Äã # Running:

‚Äã

‚Äã ...

‚Äã Finished in 0.000585s, 5128.2049 runs/s, 78632.4745 assertions/s.

‚Äã

‚Äã 3 runs, 46 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

Now, the setup method is creating the @database as a Minitest::Mock and then setting the expectation that the test will call connect and disconnect on the object (which is done behind the scenes by the PlaylistBuilder). The second argument to each method is a value returned when the mocked method is called. At the end of each test, in the teardown method, we verify the mock object, which raises a failure if both expectations aren‚Äôt met.

Minitest mock objects can get more complicated. A mock object can take an optional third argument, which is an array of arguments, and an optional block argument. If those arguments are used, then the mock object only accepts the method call if the arguments match. If not, it raises a MockExpectationError when called with arguments that don‚Äôt match. If you want to call the mock object multiple times, you need to have multiple expect calls, which are used in the order defined.

It‚Äôs common to want to override one method on an existing object rather than create an entire mock object (this isn‚Äôt necessarily recommended, but it‚Äôs common). In minitest, you can do this with the stub method, which is added to Object, so it‚Äôs available to all objects.

The first argument to stub is the name of the method you want to intercept, as a symbol. The second argument is the value you want returned, or you can pass a block argument. The return value of the stub is one of these:

The value returned by the block if there is a block.



The result of second_arg.call if the second argument responds to call, usually meaning that it‚Äôs a Proc or lambda.



The second argument itself if neither of the first two options is true.





So, we could re-write the setup of that test using stub as follows:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãsetup‚Äã

‚Äã @database = DBI.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"DBI:mysql:playlists"‚Äã)

‚Äã @database.‚Äãstub‚Äã(‚Äã:connect‚Äã, ‚Äãtrue‚Äã)

‚Äã @database.‚Äãstub‚Äã(‚Äã:disconnect‚Äã, ‚Äãtrue‚Äã)

‚Äã @playlist_builder = PlaylistBuilder.‚Äãnew‚Äã(@database)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

This version calls stub to make calls to connect or disconnect be handled by the stubbing functionality to return true rather than making the actual method call.

Stubs don‚Äôt get verified, so they are most useful for replacing an expensive or flaky method call with a canned value for use as part of some larger logic.

If you want a more complex mock object behavior, the longstanding Ruby library Mocha[19] is the next step up in using mock objects in minitest.





Organizing and Running Tests


The test cases we‚Äôve seen so far are all runnable Ruby programs. If, for example, the test case for the Roman class was in a file called test_roman.rb, we could run the tests from the command line using this:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãtest_roman.rb‚Äã

‚Äã Run options: --seed 29842

‚Äã ‚Äã# Running:‚Äã

‚Äã ..

‚Äã Finished in 0.000407s, 4914.0040 runs/s, 17199.0141 assertions/s.

‚Äã 2 runs, 7 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

Minitest is clever enough to run the tests even though there‚Äôs no main program. It collects all the test case classes and runs each in turn.

If we want, we can ask it to run a particular set of test methods based on a naming pattern:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãtest_roman.rb‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã-n‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãtest_range‚Äã

‚Äã Run options: -n test_range --seed 26287

‚Äã ‚Äã# Running:‚Äã

‚Äã .

‚Äã Finished in 0.000276s, 3623.1883 runs/s, 7246.3767 assertions/s.

‚Äã 1 runs, 2 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

In this case, minitest will run test methods whose names exactly match the text passed to -n. That‚Äôs pretty restrictive. So, if you want to run more than one test based on a naming pattern and include any regular expression punctuation in the argument, minitest will match the test name against the regular expression:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãtest_roman.rb‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã-n‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã/range/‚Äã

‚Äã Run options: -n /range/ --seed 52301

‚Äã ‚Äã# Running:‚Äã

‚Äã .

‚Äã Finished in 0.000321s, 3115.2648 runs/s, 6230.5296 assertions/s.

‚Äã 1 runs, 2 assertions, 0 failures, 0 errors, 0 skips

This last capability is a great way of grouping your tests. Use meaningful names, and you‚Äôll be able to run (for example) all the shopping cart‚Äìrelated tests by running tests with -n /cart/.





Where to Put Tests


Once you get into unit testing, you may well find yourself generating as much test code as production code. All of those tests have to live somewhere. But if you put them alongside your regular production code source files, your directories start to get bloated, and you end up with two files for every production source file.

A common solution is to have a test/ directory where you place all your test source files. This directory is then placed parallel to the directory containing the code you‚Äôre developing. For example, for our Roman numeral class, we may have this:

‚Äã roman/

‚Äã lib/

‚Äã roman.‚Äãrb‚Äã

‚Äã OTHER FILES

‚Äã

‚Äã test/

‚Äã test_roman.‚Äãrb‚Äã

‚Äã OTHER TESTS

‚Äã

‚Äã OTHER STUFF

This works well as a way of organizing files but leaves you with a small problem: how do you tell Ruby where to find the library files that are being tested? For example, if our TestRoman test code is in a test/ subdirectory, how does Ruby know where to find the roman.rb source file that we‚Äôre trying to test?

An option that doesn‚Äôt work reliably is to build the path into require statements in the test code and run the tests from the test/ subdirectory:

‚Äã require ‚Äã'test/unit'‚Äã

‚Äã require ‚Äã'../lib/roman'‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestRoman < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

This doesn‚Äôt work in general because our roman.rb file may itself require other source files in the library we‚Äôre writing. The roman.rb file will load them using require (without the leading ../lib/), and, because they aren‚Äôt in Ruby‚Äôs $LOAD_PATH, they won‚Äôt be found. Our test just won‚Äôt run. A second, less immediate problem is that we won‚Äôt be able to use these same tests to test our classes once installed on a target system because then they‚Äôll be referenced simply using require "roman".

You could do this using require_relative ‚Äô../lib/roman‚Äô, which would be more stable and doesn‚Äôt assume anything about the load path. A better solution is to assume that your Ruby program is packaged according to the conventions we‚Äôll be discussing in Chapter 15, ‚ÄãRuby Gems‚Äã. In this arrangement, the top-level lib directory of your application is assumed to be in Ruby‚Äôs load path by all other components of the application. Your test code would then be as follows:

‚Äã require ‚Äã'minitest/autorun'‚Äã

‚Äã require ‚Äã'roman'‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestRoman < Minitest::Test

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

And you‚Äôd run it using this:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã-I‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãpath/to/app/lib‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãpath/to/app/test/test_roman.rb‚Äã

The normal case, where you‚Äôre already in the application‚Äôs directory, would be as follows:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã-I‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãlib‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãtest/test_roman.rb‚Äã

This would be a good time to investigate using Rake to automate your testing (see ‚ÄãUsing the Rake Build Tool‚Äã).





Test Suites


After a while, you‚Äôll grow a decent collection of test cases for your application. You may well find that these tend to cluster: one group of cases tests a particular set of functions, and another group tests a different set of functions. If so, you can group those test cases together into test suites, letting you run them all as a group.

This is easy to do. You create a Ruby file that requires minitest/autorun and then requires each of the files holding the test cases you want to group. This way, you build a helpful hierarchy of test material.

You can run individual tests by name.

You can run all the tests in a file by running that file.

You can group a number of files into a test suite and run them as a unit.

You can group test suites into other test suites.



This gives you the ability to run your unit tests at a level of granularity that you can control, testing one method or the entire application.

Most people seem to use test_ as the test-case filename prefix. A sample test suite file might look like this:

‚Äã require ‚Äã'minitest/autorun'‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã'test_connect'‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã'test_query'‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã'test_update'‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã'test_delete'‚Äã

Now, if you run Ruby on this file, you execute the test cases in the four files you‚Äôve required.





Testing with RSpec


The minitest framework has a lot going for it. It‚Äôs simple and compatible in style with frameworks from other languages (such as JUnit for Java and pytest for Python).

RSpec has different things going for it. It‚Äôs feature-rich (or ‚Äúcomplicated,‚Äù as some would say), and it has a different vocabulary for discussing testing. It also has a different syntax. Even so, that syntax has influenced the design of other testing tools including the Jasmine and Jest JavaScript testing frameworks.

In RSpec, the focus isn‚Äôt on assertions. Instead, you write expectations. RSpec is very much concerned with driving the design side of things. As a result, the vocabulary words of RSpec (expectation and specification) are associated with ways you might reason about your code before you write it. A ‚Äúspec‚Äù is something you‚Äôd write before coding; an ‚Äúassertion‚Äù is something you use to describe code that already exists.

To be clear, you can write RSpec after you write your code, just as you can write minitest before you write your code. The design goal of RSpec is to encourage thinking about tests as a way to influence the design of code yet to be written and express those tests in a way that‚Äôs closer to natural language. Then, as you fill in the code, the specs can continue to act as tests that validate that your code meets your expectations.

Let‚Äôs start with a simple example of RSpec in action.





Starting to Score Tennis Matches


The scoring system used in lawn tennis originated in the Middle Ages. As players win successive points, their scores are shown as 15, 30, and 40. The next point is a win unless your opponent also has 40. If you‚Äôre both tied at 40, then different rules apply. The first player with a clear two-point advantage is the winner. Some say the 0, 15, 30, 40 system is a corruption of the fact that scoring used to be done using the quarters of a clock face. We just think those medieval folks enjoyed a good joke.

We want to write a class that handles this scoring system. Let‚Äôs use RSpec specifications to drive the process. We install RSpec with gem install rspec, or place it in our Gemfile (see Chapter 15, ‚ÄãRuby Gems‚Äã). We‚Äôll then create our first specification file: unittesting/bdd/1/ts_spec.rb ‚Äã RSpec.‚Äãdescribe‚Äã ‚Äã"TennisScorer"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã describe ‚Äã"basic scoring"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"starts with a score of 0-0"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



This file contains nothing more than a description of the beginning of how a tennis scoring class that we haven‚Äôt yet written should behave. Inside the declaration of the class is a grouping (describe "basic scoring") and inside that is a set of four expectations, all of which start with it. We can run this specification using the rspec command.

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãrspec‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãts_spec.rb‚Äã

‚Äã ****

‚Äã Pending: (Failures listed here are expected and do not affect your suite's status)

‚Äã 1) TennisScorer basic scoring starts with a score of 0-0

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:3‚Äã

‚Äã 2) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:4‚Äã

‚Äã 3) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:5‚Äã

‚Äã 4) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:6‚Äã

‚Äã Finished in 0.00191 seconds (files took 0.0777 seconds to load)

‚Äã 4 examples, 0 failures, 4 pending

That‚Äôs pretty cool. Executing the tests echoes our expectations back at us, telling us that each has yet to be implemented. Fixing things is just a few keystrokes away. Let‚Äôs start by meeting the first expectation‚Äîwhen a game starts, the score should be 0 to 0. We‚Äôll start by fleshing out the spec: unittesting/bdd/2/ts_spec.rb ‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"tennis_scorer"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã RSpec.‚Äãdescribe‚Äã TennisScorer ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã describe ‚Äã"basic scoring"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"starts with a score of 0-0"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts = TennisScorer.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã expect(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"0-0"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Our tests assume that we have a class TennisScorer, both in the line of code that creates an instance and also in the top line of code RSpec.describe TennisScorer. Inside that, we have a second call to describe that groups our expectations. Our first expectation now has a code block associated with it. Inside that block, we create a TennisScorer and then use RSpec‚Äôs expectation syntax to validate that the score starts out at ‚Äú0-0‚Äù. This particular aspect of RSpec syntax probably generates the most controversy‚Äîsome people love it, others find it awkward. Either way, expect(ts.score).to eq("0-0") is equivalent to assert_equal("0-0", ts.score).

We can run our tests at this point with the same command, and we‚Äôll see the test fail because the TennisScorer class doesn‚Äôt exist.

Before we create that class and pass the test, let‚Äôs take a moment to explain what RSpec is doing here. RSpec is an example of a domain-specific language (DSL), an alternate syntax built on Ruby with the goal of making it easier to express the intent of the test. Like a lot of Ruby DSLs, RSpec takes advantage of Ruby‚Äôs flexibility to result in code that doesn‚Äôt look exactly like regular Ruby.

When trying to understand RSpec, it‚Äôs helpful to reinstate full parentheses and implicit self message receivers as a guide to what‚Äôs actually happening. Here‚Äôs what that looks like for our current spec: unittesting/bdd/2/ts_spec_paren.rb ‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"tennis_scorer"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã RSpec.‚Äãdescribe‚Äã(TennisScorer) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã self.‚Äãdescribe‚Äã(‚Äã"basic scoring"‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã self.‚Äãit‚Äã(‚Äã"starts with a score of 0-0"‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts = TennisScorer.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã self.‚Äãexpect‚Äã(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã(self.‚Äãeq‚Äã(‚Äã"0-0"‚Äã))

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã self.‚Äãit‚Äã(‚Äã"makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point"‚Äã)

‚Äã self.‚Äãit‚Äã(‚Äã"makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point"‚Äã)

‚Äã self.‚Äãit‚Äã(‚Äã"makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



With all the parentheses, the structure of the code becomes more familiar. The top line shows that describe is a method of an object named RSpec, and that the inner describe and it lines are also methods with block arguments. There‚Äôs actually an important part of how this fits together that we haven‚Äôt discussed (it‚Äôs a method called instance_eval, which is discussed in Chapter 22, ‚ÄãThe Ruby Object Model and Metaprogramming‚Äã), but the basic idea is that RSpec takes the blocks that are arguments to describe and it, holds on to them, and then invokes them later in order to run the spec.

You can see that the actual expectation is also just a set of method calls. The expect method is called with an object as an argument. The result of that method is passed the to method, which itself takes an argument that‚Äôs generated by calling eq. The result of the call to eq is a matcher, and RSpec defines a series of matchers that interact with the to method (or its sibling method not_to) to determine whether the expectation is fulfilled or not.

We‚Äôll set up our TennisScorer class but only enough to let it satisfy this assertion: unittesting/bdd/2/tennis_scorer.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TennisScorer

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãscore‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"0-0"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Now we can run our spec again:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãrspec‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãts_spec.rb‚Äã

‚Äã .***

‚Äã Pending: (Failures listed here are expected and do not affect your suite's status)

‚Äã 1) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:10‚Äã

‚Äã 2) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:11‚Äã

‚Äã 3) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:12‚Äã

‚Äã Finished in 0.0016 seconds (files took 0.04504 seconds to load)

‚Äã 4 examples, 0 failures, 3 pending

Now we have only three pending specs; the first one has been satisfied.

Let‚Äôs write the next couple of specs (I‚Äôve added a new one for an error case): unittesting/bdd/3/ts_spec.rb ‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"tennis_scorer"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã RSpec.‚Äãdescribe‚Äã TennisScorer ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã describe ‚Äã"basic scoring"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"starts with a score of 0-0"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts = TennisScorer.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã expect(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"0-0"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts = TennisScorer.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:server‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"15-0"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"raises an error if it doesn't know the player"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts = TennisScorer.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã expect { ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:referee‚Äã) }.‚Äãto‚Äã raise_error(RuntimeError)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



This won‚Äôt pass yet because our TennisScorer class doesn‚Äôt implement a give_point_to method. Let‚Äôs rectify that. Our code isn‚Äôt finished, but now the existing specs will pass: unittesting/bdd/3/tennis_scorer.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TennisScorer

‚Äã PLAYERS = ‚Äã%i[server receiver]‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã

‚Äã @score = {‚Äãserver: ‚Äã0, ‚Äãreceiver: ‚Äã0}

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãscore‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@score[‚Äã:server‚Äã] * 15‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã-‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@score[‚Äã:receiver‚Äã] * 15‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(player)

‚Äã ‚Äãraise‚Äã ‚Äã"Unknown player ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãplayer‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã ‚Äãunless‚Äã PLAYERS.‚Äãinclude?‚Äã(player)

‚Äã @score[player] += 1

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Again, we‚Äôll run the file:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãrspec‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãts_spec.rb‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã...‚Äã‚Äã**‚Äã

‚Äã Pending: (Failures listed here are expected and do not affect your suite's status)

‚Äã 1) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:21‚Äã

‚Äã 2) TennisScorer basic scoring makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point

‚Äã ‚Äã # Not yet implemented‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã # ./ts_spec.rb:22‚Äã

‚Äã Finished in 0.0031 seconds (files took 0.04484 seconds to load)

‚Äã 5 examples, 0 failures, 2 pending

We‚Äôre now meeting two of the four initial expectations. But, before we move on, note there‚Äôs a bit of duplication in the specification: all of our expectations create a new TennisScorer object. We can fix that by using a before method in the specification. This works a bit like the setup method in minitest, allowing us to run code before expectations are executed. Let‚Äôs use this feature and, at the same time, build out the last two expectations: unittesting/bdd/4/ts_spec.rb ‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"tennis_scorer"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã RSpec.‚Äãdescribe‚Äã TennisScorer ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã describe ‚Äã"basic scoring"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã before(‚Äã:example‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã @ts = TennisScorer.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"starts with a score of 0-0"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã expect(@ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"0-0"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã @ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:server‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(@ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"15-0"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"raises an error if it doesn't know the player"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã expect { @ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:referee‚Äã) }.‚Äãto‚Äã raise_error(RuntimeError)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã @ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:receiver‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(@ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"0-15"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã @ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:receiver‚Äã)

‚Äã @ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:server‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(@ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"15-15"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



Let‚Äôs run it:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãrspec‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãts_spec.rb‚Äã

‚Äã .....

‚Äã Finished in 0.00193 seconds (files took 0.04763 seconds to load)

‚Äã 5 examples, 0 failures

RSpec gives us an alternative, preferred way of setting up variables that are conditions for our tests. The let method creates what looks like a variable whose value is given by evaluating a block. This lets us write the following: unittesting/bdd/5/ts_spec.rb ‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"tennis_scorer"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã RSpec.‚Äãdescribe‚Äã TennisScorer ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã describe ‚Äã"basic scoring"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã let(‚Äã:ts‚Äã) { TennisScorer.‚Äãnew‚Äã }

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"starts with a score of 0-0"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã expect(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"0-0"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-0 if the server wins a point"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:server‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"15-0"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"raises an error if it doesn't know the player"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã expect { ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:referee‚Äã) }.‚Äãto‚Äã raise_error(RuntimeError)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 0-15 if the receiver wins a point"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:receiver‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"0-15"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"makes the score 15-15 after they both win a point"‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:receiver‚Äã)

‚Äã ts.‚Äãgive_point_to‚Äã(‚Äã:server‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(ts.‚Äãscore‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã eq(‚Äã"15-15"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



The let block is only evaluated when the associated variable is used, and then the block is evaluated once, and further uses of that variable use the stored value from the first evaluation.

We‚Äôre going to stop here, but I suggest that you take this code and continue to develop it. Write expectations such as these:

‚Äã it ‚Äã"is 40-0 after the server wins three points"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"is W-L after the server wins four points"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"is L-W after the receiver wins four points"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"is Deuce after each wins three points"‚Äã

‚Äã it ‚Äã"is Advantage-server after each wins three points and the server gets one more"‚Äã





RSpec and Matchers


In the previous code, we kind of ran right past RSpec‚Äôs matchers‚Äîlines like expect(@ts.score).to eq("15-0"). RSpec has a rich syntax of matchers to cover the same ground that minitest does with different assertions.

We‚Äôve already seen eq, but that matcher is a little unusual. Many of RSpec‚Äôs matchers start with be, as with this set of matchers that cover basic logic:

‚Äã expect(value).‚Äãto‚Äã be_truthy

‚Äã expect(value).‚Äãto‚Äã be_falsey

‚Äã expect(value).‚Äãto‚Äã be_an_instance_of(Product)

‚Äã expect(value.‚Äãprice‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã be > 10

‚Äã expect(value.‚Äãprice‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã be_between(5, 15)

You can substitute any Ruby comparison operator for the greater than symbol in be >.

RSpec also provides matchers for structured data, such as objects, arrays, hashes, and strings:

‚Äã expect(array).‚Äãto‚Äã contain_exactly(‚Äã:a‚Äã, ‚Äã:b‚Äã, ‚Äã:c‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã expect(hash).‚Äãto‚Äã ‚Äãinclude‚Äã(‚Äãkey: ‚Äãvalue)

‚Äã

‚Äã expect(string).‚Äãto‚Äã start_with(‚Äã"abc"‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(string).‚Äãto‚Äã end_with(‚Äã"xyz"‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã expect(instance).‚Äãto‚Äã have_attributes(‚Äãcolor: ‚Äã‚Äã"blue"‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã expect(array_string_or_hash).‚Äãto‚Äã ‚Äãinclude‚Äã(‚Äã"value"‚Äã)

In addition, RSpec has a generic matcher match, which can be applied to arrays, strings, or hashes. Typically, the argument to match is a pattern, and the expectation passes if the expected value fits the pattern. You can use other RSpec matchers to fill part of the pattern.

Some examples:

‚Äã expect(string).‚Äãto‚Äã match(‚Äã/regex/‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(array).‚Äãto‚Äã match([3, 5])

‚Äã expect(hash).‚Äãto‚Äã match(‚Äãcolor: ‚Äãa_string_starting_with(‚Äã"b"‚Äã))

The last example shows that RSpec offers aliases for most of its matchers so they read more like natural language when used internally, so ‚Äúa_string_starting_with‚Äù is an alias for ‚Äústarts_with‚Äù. This is where people‚Äôs opinions about RSpec start to split‚Äîsome people find this kind of linguistic wordplay elegant and clever, while others find it confusing and overly complicated.

Which is a great lead into RSpec‚Äôs dynamic matcher syntax.

Often, an object has boolean methods that you want to test. In Ruby, the community standard is to end boolean methods with a ? as in: paperback?.

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Book

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãpaperback?‚Äã

‚Äã type == ‚Äã:paper‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

You can test this method in RSpec using normal RSpec syntax, which would look something like this:

‚Äã expect(book.‚Äãpaperback?‚Äã).‚Äãto‚Äã be_truthy

And that‚Äôs fine, and it works. But if you read it out loud it sounds weird‚Äîmore like how computers talk and not like how people talk. You might want to be able to write this:

‚Äã expect(book).‚Äãto‚Äã be_a_paperback

Read that out loud, and it sounds like natural language.

So, to make that work in RSpec, you need to do‚Ä¶nothing. It already works.

When RSpec sees a matcher that starts with be, be_a, or be_an, it does some parsing on the name of the matcher and looks for a method in the object under test. If the matcher has no arguments, it looks for a predicate method that ends in a question mark, so expect(book).to be_a_paperback looks for book.paperback? and failing that, book.paperbacks?. If the matcher has arguments, it looks for a regular method, so expect(book).to be_published_at(Date.today) would look for book.published_at(Date.today). RSpec will do the same thing with have and has. The matcher expect(book).to have_cover will look for book.has_cover?, while expect(book).to have_author("Dave") will look for book.has_author("Dave").

There is also a set of matchers that take a block. The general structure is expect { SOMETHING }.to MATCHER. The most common is probably expect { }.to raise_error(arg), where the argument is usually a Ruby exception class and the matcher passes if the block raises the expected error. The argument could also be a string or regular expression matching the error message.

There are also a series of matchers based on expect { BLOCK 1 }.to change { BLOCK 2 }. Here‚Äôs how this works: RSpec runs block 2 and stores the value, then it runs block 1, and then it checks block 2 again, and the matcher passes if block 2‚Äôs value has changed. You can chain additional methods to the end if you want to specify details, as in expect { book.publish! }.to change { book.publication_date }.from(nil).to(Date.today).

There‚Äôs a lot more to RSpec matchers, including the ability to create your own. For more information, see Effective Testing with RSpec 3 by Myron Marston and Erin Dees.





RSpec and Mocks


rb In addition to minitest, RSpec also allows you to create mock and stub objects and has a lot of features available by default. Here‚Äôs an overview of the most common.

In RSpec, the generic term for a fake object is test double, an object that stands in for another object, by analogy to ‚Äústunt double.‚Äù The simplest way to create one is by using the method double. You can create the double and assign it a method to respond to using the RSpec method allow:

‚Äã obj = double

‚Äã allow(obj).‚Äãto‚Äã receive(‚Äã:cost‚Äã).‚Äãand_return‚Äã(‚Äã"cheap"‚Äã)

‚Äã allow(obj).‚Äãto‚Äã receive(‚Äã:name‚Äã).‚Äãand_return‚Äã(‚Äã"banana"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãcost‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãname‚Äã

If you pass multiple arguments to and_return, you specify responses for multiple times that the method is called. You can limit the arguments under which the double is invoked by chaining in .with, as in allow(obj).to receive(:availability).with("January").and_return(true).

You can define multiple responses at once with the receive_messages method, which takes a hash. Keys become the methods to respond to, and values are the fake values returned:

‚Äã obj = double

‚Äã allow(obj).‚Äãto‚Äã receive_messages(‚Äãcost: ‚Äã‚Äã"cheap"‚Äã, ‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"banana"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãcost‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãbanana‚Äã

Or you can use a shortcut by passing keyword arguments directly to double:

‚Äã obj = double(‚Äãcost: ‚Äã‚Äã"cheap"‚Äã, ‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"banana"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãcost‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãbanana‚Äã

In minitest, we talked about validating mock objects by having the test fail if the method being faked isn‚Äôt called during the test. In RSpec, you manage this with the expect method.

You can call expect on a double before the main action of the spec. In this case, expect is an exact replacement for allow:

‚Äã obj = double

‚Äã expect(obj).‚Äãto‚Äã receive(‚Äã:cost‚Äã).‚Äãand_return‚Äã(‚Äã"cheap"‚Äã)

‚Äã expect(obj).‚Äãto‚Äã receive(‚Äã:name‚Äã).‚Äãand_return‚Äã(‚Äã"banana"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãcost‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãbanana‚Äã

The expect method behaves the same as allow except that, at the end of the spec, RSpec additionally and automatically validates whether the expected methods have been called. If not, it fails the spec.

A downside of this mechanism is that the expectation happens at the beginning of the spec, but the validation happens at the end, and only implicitly. This can make the spec hard to read. An alternative is to use a slightly different form of expect at the end of the spec:

‚Äã obj = double

‚Äã allow(obj).‚Äãto‚Äã receive(‚Äã:cost‚Äã).‚Äãand_return‚Äã(‚Äã"cheap"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãcost‚Äã

‚Äã expect(obj).‚Äãto‚Äã have_received(‚Äã:cost‚Äã)

The allow and expect constructs in RSpec are powerful. You can even use them on objects that aren‚Äôt test doubles to stub a particular method on an existing object:

‚Äã kermit = Muppet.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã allow(kermit).‚Äãto‚Äã receive(‚Äã:greeting‚Äã).‚Äãand_return‚Äã(‚Äã"Hi ho"‚Äã)

A potential problem with test doubles is that the API of the underlying object might change, but the test, with its stubbed method, blissfully continues to pass. RSpec offers some protection from that with the instance_double variation. An instance_double call takes a class as an argument:

‚Äã fake_product = instance_double(Product)

‚Äã allow(fake_product).‚Äãto‚Äã receive(‚Äã:name‚Äã).‚Äãand_return‚Äã(‚Äã"pretzel"‚Äã)

Now, when you call allow or expect with the instance double as an argument, RSpec checks to see if the class in question actually defines the method you‚Äôre stubbing. (There‚Äôs a similar RSpec creator, class_double, for class methods rather than instance methods.) If the method doesn‚Äôt exist, RSpec raises an error at the point of the declaration.

This only scratches the surface of RSpec‚Äôs mock package.





What‚Äôs Next


In this chapter, we covered Ruby‚Äôs two most commonly used test frameworks: minitest and RSpec. Which should you use? Well, if you‚Äôre working on a project that already uses one of them, we recommend sticking with that one. There‚Äôs not so much difference between the two that it‚Äôs worth re-writing all your tests.

If you‚Äôre starting a new project, consider whether you like RSpec‚Äôs syntax. RSpec is probably more widely used, but some prominent Ruby projects still use minitest, including Ruby on Rails. RSpec has a higher initial complexity but is also more flexible and has more available functionality out of the box. Ultimately, though, it‚Äôs a question of which syntax you like better and will get you to write more tests.

We‚Äôve finished our tutorial of the Ruby language, and now it‚Äôs time to widen our view and take a look at the larger Ruby tool ecosystem.

[19]

https://mocha.jamesmead.org
