Chapter 22



The Ruby Object Model and Metaprogramming




The Jacquard loom, invented more than 200 years ago, was the first device controlled by punched cards‚Äîrows of holes in each card were used to control the pattern woven into the cloth. But imagine if instead of churning out fabric, the loom could punch more cards, and those cards could be fed back into the mechanism. The machine could be used to create new programming that it could then execute. And that would be metaprogramming‚Äîwriting code that writes code.

Programming is all about building layers of abstractions. As you solve problems, you‚Äôre building bridges from the unrelenting and mechanical world of silicon to the more ambiguous and fluid world we inhabit. Some programming languages‚Äîsuch as C‚Äîare close to the machine. The distance from C code to the application domain can be large. Other languages‚ÄîRuby, perhaps‚Äîprovide higher-level abstractions and hence let you start coding closer to the target domain. For this reason, most people consider a higher-level language to be a better starting place for application development (although they‚Äôll argue about the choice of language).

But when you metaprogram, you are no longer limited to the set of abstractions built into your programming language. Instead, you create new abstractions that are integrated into the host language. In effect, you‚Äôre creating a new, domain-specific programming language‚Äîone that lets you express the concepts you need to solve your particular problem. Metaprogramming can be an excellent way to manage complex problems where the structure of the underlying data drives the structure of the code.

Ruby makes metaprogramming easy. As a result, Ruby programmers will often use metaprogramming techniques to simplify their code. This chapter shows how they do it. It isn‚Äôt intended to be an exhaustive survey of metaprogramming techniques. Instead, we‚Äôll look at the underlying Ruby object model and structures that make metaprogramming possible. From there, you‚Äôll be able to invent your own metaprogramming idioms.





Understanding Objects and Classes


Classes and objects are central to Ruby, but at first sight, they can be confusing. It seems like there are a lot of concepts: classes, objects, class objects, instance methods, class methods, singleton classes, and virtual classes. In reality, all these Ruby constructs are part of the same underlying class and object structure.

Internally, a Ruby object has three components: a set of flags, some instance variables, and an associated class. A Ruby class contains all the things an object has plus a set of method definitions and a reference to a superclass (which is itself another class). A Ruby class is itself an instance of the class Class. Let‚Äôs look at how that structure lends itself to metaprogramming in Ruby.





Method Calling and self


Ruby has a concept of the current object. The current object is referenced by the built-in, read-only variable self. The first time that Noel heard Dave Thomas speak in public, the topic was Ruby metaprogramming and he said ‚Äúunderstanding self is the key to Ruby. Also the key to life.‚Äù Words to live by.

The value self has two significant roles in a running Ruby program. First, self controls how Ruby finds instance variables. We already said that every object carries around a set of instance variables. When you access an instance variable using the @<varname> syntax, Ruby looks for that variable in the self object, as defined in the current context. In this section and the next, we‚Äôll show how the value of self changes when you call methods or define classes and modules.

Second, self plays a vital role in method calling. In Ruby, each method call is a message passed to some object. This object is called the receiver of the call. When you make a call such as items.size, the object on the left side of the dot‚Äîhere referenced by the variable items‚Äîis the receiver and size is the method to invoke.

Often, you‚Äôll see a method call with no explicit receiver, such as puts "hi". In this case, Ruby uses the current object, self, as the receiver. It goes to self‚Äôs class and looks up the method (in this case, puts). If it can‚Äôt find the method in the class, it looks in the class‚Äôs superclass and then in that class‚Äôs superclass, stopping when it runs out of superclasses (which will happen after it has looked in BasicObject). If it can‚Äôt find the method in the object‚Äôs class hierarchy, Ruby looks for a method called method_missing on the original receiver, starting back at the class of self and then looking up the superclass chain. (In the case of puts, Ruby will find the method defined in the Kernel module that‚Äôs included in Object.)

When you make a method call with an explicit receiver (for example, invoking items.size), the process is similar. The only change‚Äîbut a vitally important one‚Äîis that the value of self is changed for the duration of the call. Before starting the method lookup process, Ruby sets self to the explicit receiver (the object referenced by items in this case). Then, after the call returns, Ruby restores self to the value it had before the call.

Let‚Äôs see how this works in practice. Here‚Äôs a simple program:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãone‚Äã

‚Äã @var = 99

‚Äã two

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtwo‚Äã

‚Äã puts @var

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã t = Test.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã t.‚Äãone‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã 99

The call to Test.new on the second-to-last line creates a new object of class Test, assigning that object to the variable t. Then, on the next line, we call the method t.one. To execute this call, Ruby sets self to t and then looks in t‚Äôs class for the method one. Ruby finds the method defined in the class and calls it.

Inside the method, we set the instance variable @var to 99. This instance variable will be associated with the current object. What is that object? Since the call to t.one sets self to t, within that call of the one method, self will be that particular instance of class Test.

On the next line, the method one calls the method two. Because there‚Äôs no explicit receiver, self isn‚Äôt changed. When Ruby looks for the method two, it looks in Test, the class of t.

The method two references an instance variable @var. Again, Ruby looks for this variable in the current object and finds the same variable that was set by the method one.

The call to puts inside the two method works the same way. Again, because there‚Äôs no explicit receiver, self will be unchanged. Ruby looks for the puts method in the class of the current object but can‚Äôt find it. It then looks in Test‚Äôs superclass, class Object. Again, it doesn‚Äôt find puts. But Object mixes in the module Kernel. We‚Äôll talk more about this later. For now, we can say that mixed-in modules act as if they were superclasses. The Kernel module does define puts, so the method is found and executed.

After two and one return, Ruby resets self to the value it had before the original call to t.one. The code is at the top level. The top-level self is an object called main, which is placed there by the Ruby runtime.

This explanation may seem labored, but understanding it is an important part of mastering metaprogramming in Ruby.





Class Definitions and self


We‚Äôve seen that calling a method with an explicit receiver changes self. Perhaps surprisingly, self is also changed inside a class or module definition, but outside all the method definitions. This is a consequence of the fact that class definitions are actually executable code in Ruby‚Äîif we can execute code, we need to have a current object. A simple test shows what this object is:

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Before the class definition, self = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã\n‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Test

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"In the definition of class Test, self = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Class of self = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãclass‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã\n‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"After the class definition, self = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Before the class definition, self = main

‚Äã In the definition of class Test, self = Test

‚Äã Class of self = Class

‚Äã After the class definition, self = main

Outside the class definition, self is set to an object called main, which is what Ruby uses as the implicit top-level object. It‚Äôs also the object that holds on to method definitions that happen outside of a class definition.



Inside a class or module definition, self is set to the object of the class or module being defined. This means that instance variables set inside a class or module definition will be available to class or module methods (because self will be the same when the variables are defined and when the methods execute):

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Test

‚Äã @var = 99

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãvalue_of_var‚Äã

‚Äã @var

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã Test.‚Äãvalue_of_var‚Äã ‚Äã# => 99‚Äã

The fact that self is set to the class during a class definition turns out to be a dramatically elegant decision, but to see why, we‚Äôll first need to have a look at singleton methods.





Defining Singleton Methods


Ruby lets you define methods that are specific to a particular object. These are called singleton methods.

Here‚Äôs a simple string object and a regular, non-singleton method call:

‚Äã animal = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã puts animal.‚Äãupcase‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã CAT

This call results in the object structure shown in the following illustration:

The animal variable points to an object containing (among other things) the value of the string ("cat") and a pointer to the object‚Äôs class, String.

When we call animal.upcase, Ruby checks the object referenced by the animal variable and then looks up the method upcase in the class object referenced from the animal object. Our animal is a string and so all the methods of class String are available.

Now let‚Äôs make it more interesting. We‚Äôll define a singleton method on the string referenced by animal. We‚Äôve seen this syntax before, we use a method name that includes a reference to a specific object:

‚Äã animal = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã animal.‚Äãspeak‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"The ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã says miaow"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã animal.‚Äãspeak‚Äã

‚Äã puts animal.‚Äãupcase‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã The cat says miaow

‚Äã CAT

The call to animal.speak is handled similar to the way animal.upcase was invoked earlier. Ruby sets self to the string object "cat", which is referenced by animal, and then looks for a method called speak in that object‚Äôs class. Surprisingly, it finds it. This is initially surprising because the class of "cat" is String, and String doesn‚Äôt have a speak method. But the specific object called animal has a speak method that we‚Äôve defined for that object and that object only.

So, does Ruby have some kind of special-case magic for these methods that are defined on individual objects?

Not exactly.

When we defined animal.speak, the singleton method for the "cat" object, Ruby created a new anonymous class and placed the speak method in that class. This anonymous class goes by a couple of different names, but you‚Äôll most likely see it called a singleton class (it‚Äôs sometimes called an eigenclass). We prefer the former name because it ties in to the idea of singleton methods.

Every object in Ruby has the potential of having its own singleton class. When you define a singleton class, Ruby creates that anonymous class and makes it the singleton class of that object.

You can access that singleton class via the singleton_class method, and you can get a list of methods defined there with singleton_methods.

‚Äã animal = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã animal.‚Äãspeak‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"The ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã says miaow"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã animal.‚Äãspeak‚Äã

‚Äã puts animal.‚Äãclass‚Äã

‚Äã puts animal.‚Äãsingleton_class‚Äã

‚Äã puts animal.‚Äãsingleton_methods‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã The cat says miaow

‚Äã String

‚Äã #<Class:#<String:0x0000000103209258>>

‚Äã speak

If an object has a singleton class, that‚Äôs the first place Ruby looks for object lookup. It‚Äôs as if Ruby makes String (which was the original class of "cat") the superclass of the singleton class. The picture looks like this:

Now let‚Äôs follow the call to animal.speak. Ruby goes to the object referenced by animal and then looks in its singleton class class for the method speak. The singleton class of the animal object is the newly created singleton class, and it contains the method we need.

What happens if we instead call animal.upcase? The processing starts the same way: Ruby looks for the method upcase in the singleton class but fails to find it there. It then follows the normal processing rules and starts looking up the chain of superclasses. The superclass of the singleton is String, and Ruby finds the upcase method there. Notice that no special-case processing is here‚ÄîRuby method calls always work up the object chain in the same way.





Singletons and Classes


Earlier, we said that inside a class definition, self is set to the class object being defined. It turns out that this is the basis for one of the more elegant aspects of Ruby‚Äôs object model.

Recall that we can define class methods in Ruby using either the def self.xxx or (more rarely) def ClassName.xxx form:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Dave

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãclass_method_one‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Class method one"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã Dave.‚Äãclass_method_two‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Class method two"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã Dave.‚Äãclass_method_one‚Äã

‚Äã Dave.‚Äãclass_method_two‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Class method one

‚Äã Class method two

Now we can explain why the two forms are identical: inside the class definition, self is set to the class object Dave.

But now that we‚Äôve looked at singleton methods, we also know that, in reality, no such thing as a class method exists in Ruby. Both of the previous definitions define singleton methods on the class object. As with all other singleton methods, we can then call them via the object (in this case, the class Dave).

Before we created the two singleton methods in class Dave, the class pointer in the class object pointed to class Class. (That‚Äôs a confusing sentence. Another way of saying it is ‚ÄúDave is a class, so the class of Dave is class Class,‚Äù but that‚Äôs pretty confusing, too.) The situation looks like this:

The object diagram for class Dave after the methods are defined looks like this:

Do you see how the singleton class is created, just as it was for the animal example? The class is inserted as the singleton class of the class Dave, and the original class of Dave is made this new singleton class‚Äôs parent.

We can now tie together the two uses of self, the current object. We talked about how instance variables are looked up in self, and we talked about how singleton methods defined on self become class methods. Let‚Äôs use these facts to access instance variables for class objects:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Test

‚Äã @var = 99

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãvar‚Äã

‚Äã @var

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãvar‚Äã=(value)

‚Äã @var = value

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Original value = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãTest.‚Äãvar‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã Test.‚Äãvar‚Äã = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"New value = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãTest.‚Äãvar‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Original value = 99

‚Äã New value = cat

Newcomers to Ruby commonly try to set instance variables inline in the class definition (as we did with @var in the previous code) and then attempt to access these variables from instance methods. As the code illustrates, this won‚Äôt work because instance variables are associated with self in their current context. In the context of the instance variables defined in the class body, self is the class, and therefore instance variables defined in the class body outside of methods are associated with the class object, not with instances of the class.





Another Way to Access the Singleton Class


We‚Äôve seen how you can create methods in an object‚Äôs singleton class by adding the object reference to the method definition using something like def animal.speak.

You can also access the singleton class using Ruby‚Äôs class << an_object notation:

‚Äã animal = ‚Äã"dog"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã << animal

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãspeak‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"The ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã says WOOF!"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã animal.‚Äãspeak‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã The dog says WOOF!

Inside this kind of class definition, self is set to the singleton class for the given object (animal in this case). Because class definitions return the value of the last statement executed in the class body, we can use this fact to get the singleton class object:

‚Äã animal = ‚Äã"dog"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã animal.‚Äãspeak‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"The ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã says WOOF!"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã singleton = ‚Äãclass‚Äã << animal

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãlie‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"The ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã lies down"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã self ‚Äã# << return singleton class object‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã animal.‚Äãspeak‚Äã

‚Äã animal.‚Äãlie‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Singleton class object is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãsingleton‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"It defines methods ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãsingleton.‚Äãinstance_methods‚Äã - animal.‚Äãmethods‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"You can also access it as ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãanimal.‚Äãsingleton_class‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"And the list of methods as ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãanimal.‚Äãsingleton_methods‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã The dog says WOOF!

‚Äã The dog lies down

‚Äã Singleton class object is #<Class:#<String:0x0000000100337628>>

‚Äã It defines methods []

‚Äã You can also access it as #<Class:#<String:0x0000000100337628>>

‚Äã And the list of methods as [:lie, :speak]

Pay attention to the notation that Ruby uses to denote a singleton class: #<Class:#<String:...>>.

Ruby goes to some trouble to stop you from using singleton classes outside the context of their original object. For example, you can‚Äôt create a new instance of a singleton class:

‚Äã singleton = ‚Äãclass‚Äã << ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã; self; ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã singleton.‚Äãnew‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã from prog.rb:2:in `<main>'

‚Äã prog.rb:2:in `new': can't create instance of singleton class (TypeError)

Let‚Äôs tie together what we know about instance variables, self, and singleton classes. We could write class-level accessor methods to let us get and set the value of an instance variable defined in a class object. Ruby already has attr_accessor, which defines getter and setter methods for instances. Normally, these are defined as instance methods and hence will access values stored in instances of a class. To make them work with class-level instance variables, we have to invoke attr_accessor in the singleton class:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Test

‚Äã @var = 99

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã << self

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:var‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Original value = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãTest.‚Äãvar‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã Test.‚Äãvar‚Äã = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"New value = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãTest.‚Äãvar‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Original value = 99

‚Äã New value = cat





Inheritance and Visibility


Method definition and class inheritance have a wrinkle, but it‚Äôs fairly obscure. Within a class definition, you can change the visibility of a method in an ancestor class. For example, you can do something like this:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Base

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãa_method‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Got here"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äã:a_method‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MakeItPublic < Base

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äã:a_method‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã KeepItPrivate < Base

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

In this example, you could invoke a_method in instances of class MakeItPublic, but not via instances of Base or KeepItPrivate.

So, how does Ruby pull off this feat of having one method with two different visibilities? Simply put, it cheats.

If a subclass changes the visibility of a method in a parent, Ruby effectively inserts a hidden proxy method in the subclass that invokes the original method using super. It then sets the visibility of that proxy to whatever you requested.

For example, you might have this code:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MakeItPublic < Base

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äã:a_method‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Which is effectively the same as this:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MakeItPublic < Base

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãa_method‚Äã(*)

‚Äã ‚Äãsuper‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äã:a_method‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

The call to super can access the parent‚Äôs method regardless of its visibility, so the rewrite allows the subclass to override its parent‚Äôs visibility rules.





Modules and Mixins


As we saw in ‚ÄãMixins‚Äã, when you include a module into a Ruby class, the instance methods in that module become available as instance methods of the class, like this:

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãLogger‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãlog‚Äã(msg)

‚Äã STDERR.‚Äãputs‚Äã Time.‚Äãnow‚Äã.‚Äãstrftime‚Äã(‚Äã"%H:%M:%S: "‚Äã) + ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã (‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãmsg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã)"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Song

‚Äã ‚Äãinclude‚Äã Logger

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã s = Song.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã s.‚Äãlog‚Äã(‚Äã"created"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã 17:16:19: #<Song:0x0000000104717708> (created)

Ruby implements include very simply. The module you include is added as an ancestor of the class being defined. It‚Äôs as if the module is the parent of the class that it‚Äôs mixed into. And that would be the end of the description except for one small wrinkle. Because the module is injected into the chain of superclasses, it must itself hold a link to the original parent class. If it didn‚Äôt, there‚Äôd be no way of traversing the superclass chain to look up methods. But you can mix the same module into many different classes, and those classes could potentially have totally different superclass chains. If there were just one module object that we mixed into all these classes, there‚Äôd be no way of keeping track of the different superclasses for each.

To get around this, Ruby uses a clever trick. When you include a module in class Example, Ruby constructs a new class object, makes it the superclass of Example, and then sets the superclass of the new class to be the original superclass of Example. It then references the module‚Äôs methods from this new class object in such a way that when you look a method up in this class, it actually looks it up in the module, as shown here:



A nice side effect of this arrangement is that if you change a module after including it in a class, those changes are reflected in the class (and the class‚Äôs objects). In this way, modules behave just like classes:

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãMod‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãgreeting‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Hello"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example

‚Äã ‚Äãinclude‚Äã Mod

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Before change, greeting is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãgreeting‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãMod‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãgreeting‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Hi"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"After change, greeting is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãgreeting‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Before change, greeting is Hello

‚Äã After change, greeting is Hi

If a module itself includes other modules, a chain of proxy classes will be added to any class that includes that module‚Äîone proxy for each module that‚Äôs directly or indirectly included.

Finally, Ruby will include a particular module only once in an inheritance chain. Including a module that‚Äôs already included by one of your superclasses has no effect.





Using prepend


Ruby enables you to place a module at a different point in the method lookup chain using the prepend method. Logically, prepend behaves just like include, but the methods in the prepended module take precedence over those in the host class. Ruby pulls off this magic by inserting a dummy class in place of the original host class. Actually, it inserts the dummy class above the original class, moves the methods from the original to the copy. and then inserts the prepended module between the two.

If a method inside a prepended module has the same name as the one in the original class, it‚Äôll be invoked instead of the original. The prepended method can then call the original using super:

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãVanityPuts‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãputs‚Äã(*args)

‚Äã args.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |arg|

‚Äã ‚Äãsuper‚Äã(‚Äã"Dave says: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãarg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Object

‚Äã prepend VanityPuts

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Hello and"‚Äã, ‚Äã"goodbye"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Dave says: Hello and

‚Äã Dave says: goodbye

But there‚Äôs a problem‚Äîthe change we just made to class Object is global. We‚Äôll see how to manage that shortly when we look at refinements.





Using extend


The include method effectively adds a module as a superclass of self. This method is used inside a class definition to make the instance methods in the module available to instances of the class.

However, it‚Äôs sometimes useful to add the instance methods directly to a particular object and not to its class. For example, you‚Äôd want to do this to add methods directly to a class object that you want to be treated like class methods. You do this using Module#extend. Here‚Äôs an example:

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãHumor‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtickle‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã says hee, hee!"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = ‚Äã"Grouchy"‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãextend‚Äã(Humor)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãtickle‚Äã ‚Äã# => "Grouchy says hee, hee!"‚Äã

Stop for a second to think about how this might be implemented.

When Ruby executes obj.tickle in this code example, it does the usual trick of looking in the class of obj for a method called tickle. For extend to work, it has to add the Humor module‚Äôs instance methods into the superclass chain for the class of obj. Just as with the singleton method definitions, Ruby creates a singleton class for obj and then includes the module Humor in that class. To prove this is all that happens, here‚Äôs the C implementation of extend in the current Ruby interpreter:

‚Äã ‚Äãvoid‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãrb_extend_object‚Äã(VALUE obj, VALUE module)

‚Äã {

‚Äã rb_include_module(rb_singleton_class(obj), module);

‚Äã }

There is an interesting trick with extend. If you use it within a class definition, the module‚Äôs methods become class methods. This is because calling extend is equivalent to self.extend, so the methods are added to self, which in a class definition is the class itself.

Here‚Äôs an example of adding a module‚Äôs methods at the class level:

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãHumor‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtickle‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã says hee, hee!"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Grouchy

‚Äã ‚Äãextend‚Äã Humor

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã Grouchy.‚Äãtickle‚Äã ‚Äã# => "Grouchy says hee, hee!"‚Äã

Later, in ‚ÄãClass Methods and Modules‚Äã, we‚Äôll see how to use extend to add macro-style methods to a class.





Using Refinements


We previously looked at applying a change to a built-in class by defining the altered version of a method in a module and then prepending that module in the class. When we subsequently call the method on instances of the class, it finds the version in the module first. We‚Äôve also seen that Ruby allows you to reopen an existing class or module and add new methods to it. When we subsequently use instances of the class, the new methods exist along with the ones that were previously defined.

These techniques are time-honored, and frameworks such as Ruby on Rails rely on them. But they come with a price. Any changes we make with prepend and monkey patches are global to our running application. They apply not just to the code we wrote for our application but also to the code in all the libraries and gems we use while the application is running, too.

It‚Äôs possible that a change that made our code easier to write breaks someone else‚Äôs library code that we rely on. This is clearly a problem in theory. Does it happen in practice? Actually, surprisingly rarely. But you can never be sure that things will quite work as you expect. Even if you don‚Äôt override these classes yourself, it‚Äôs possible you‚Äôre using two separate libraries whose patches to third-party classes clash.

Ruby has a feature called refinements. They allow you to make these changes locally without affecting code outside the file you‚Äôre writing. The goal of using refinements is to continue to allow you to make changes to existing classes, but to only allow those changes to be in effect when you explicitly say that they are. Not only are the changes no longer global, but when an added method is used in a file, an explicit statement says where the added method is coming from. This means a reader of the code has a better chance of finding the source more easily.

Now, before going any further, here‚Äôs the mandatory warning. Refinements have been a part of Ruby since 2.0, but their actual uptake has been minor. It‚Äôs unusual to see refinements in the wild. Even though it seems they solve a problem in theory, not many teams seem to have found them to solve a problem in fact.

A refinement is a way of packaging a set of changes to one or more classes. These refinements are defined within a module. You can then elect to use this module with the refinements within a source file, in which case the change will apply to the source in that module past the point where the refinement is used. However, code outside this file isn‚Äôt affected. Alternatively, you can elect to use the module with refinements inside a module or class, in which case the refinement applies inside that module or class, but not outside of it.

Let‚Äôs make this concrete. Here‚Äôs a vanity version of puts rewritten using refinements:

metaprogramming/vanity_puts.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãVanityPuts‚Äã

‚Äã refine Object ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãputs‚Äã(*args)

‚Äã args.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |arg|

‚Äã Kernel.‚Äãputs‚Äã(‚Äã"Dave says: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãarg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

The refinement is contained in the module VanityPuts. The line refine Object do starts the definition. What‚Äôs happening here is we‚Äôre calling the method Module#refine, and the receiver is the VanityPuts module. The refine method takes an argument, which is a class or module and a block. Within the block, you define the methods that you‚Äôd like to update in that class. At this point, no change is made to the underlying class. You have defined a method, but haven‚Äôt yet told Ruby to use it.

That‚Äôs what the using method does:

metaprogramming/use_in_class.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"vanity_puts"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã DavesStuff

‚Äã using VanityPuts

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãgreet‚Äã(msg)

‚Äã puts msg

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã DavesStuff.‚Äãnew‚Äã.‚Äãgreet‚Äã(‚Äã"in DavesStuff"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã Dave says: in DavesStuff

You give it a module containing one or more refinements, and it marks the refined objects to say ‚Äúfor the rest of the scope of this method, when you make a call to an instance of Object, first check to see if the method can be found in the refinement. If so, invoke it, otherwise invoke the original.‚Äù

The basic scoping rule is that a refinement is activated in a source file by calling using. If using is called inside a module or class, the refinement is in effect for the duration of that definition.

You can also invoke using at the top level of a file, in which case for the rest of that source file, the methods that are defined in that refinement are active inside any classes or methods yet to be defined in the source file:

metaprogramming/use_in_file.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"vanity_puts"‚Äã

‚Äã using VanityPuts

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Hello"‚Äã, ‚Äã"world"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Dave says: Hello

‚Äã Dave says: world

Let‚Äôs step it up a notch. We‚Äôll define three source files.

Here‚Äôs one that contains a refinement definition:

metaprogramming/ref1/vanity_refinement.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãVanityPuts‚Äã

‚Äã refine Object ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãputs‚Äã(*args)

‚Äã args.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |arg|

‚Äã Kernel.‚Äãputs‚Äã(‚Äã"Dave says: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãarg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

And here‚Äôs a file that uses this refinement:

metaprogramming/ref1/file_using_refinement.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã VanityShouter

‚Äã using VanityPuts

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãshout‚Äã(msg)

‚Äã puts(msg.‚Äãupcase‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã VanityShouter.‚Äãnew‚Äã.‚Äãshout‚Äã(‚Äã"I'm here!"‚Äã)

Finally, let‚Äôs run them from a third file:

metaprogramming/ref1/main_program.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"vanity_refinement"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"About to require file using refinement"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"file_using_refinement"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Back from require"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã VanityShouter.‚Äãnew‚Äã.‚Äãshout‚Äã(‚Äã"finished"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã About to require file using refinement

‚Äã Dave says: I'M HERE!

‚Äã Back from require

‚Äã Dave says: FINISHED

Notice how the puts calls in the main program are unadorned, but the calls in the file that uses the refinement have the vanity prefix.





Designing with Refinements


In general, refinements can be used any place you‚Äôd want to extend or monkey-patch a third-party tool. There are two main use cases.

The first is the case where a developer wants to make changes to a third-party class for the developer‚Äôs own use. For example, Rake, which issues a lot of calls to run external programs using the system method, might want to modify the built-in version of system so that it logs errors differently. But it doesn‚Äôt want that logging to apply to other calls to system that aren‚Äôt part of Rake. In this case, the code could use the refinement locally within its own source files. The refinement would be an implementation detail, hidden from users of the code.

The second use case is where a library writer offers the refinement as part of the external interface. For example, the Rails Active Support gem defines methods such as hours, minutes, and ago on numbers, allowing you to write 3.days.ago. Right now, those changes to numbers are global. But, using refinements, the Rails team could code the new methods, but not add them in to any system classes. Instead, their API would document how to add them for yourself into just those source files that use them. They might tell you to write this in any source file that needs to use them:

‚Äã using Rails::Extensions::Durations

There are many more potential use cases. And the two we‚Äôve mentioned aren‚Äôt mutually exclusive. The Rails framework, for example, is likely to want to use these duration-related methods itself, as well as making them available via a documented refinement.





Metaprogramming Class-Level Macros


If you‚Äôve used Ruby for any time at all, you‚Äôre likely to have used attr_accessor, the method that defines reader and writer methods for instance variables:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Song

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:duration‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

If you‚Äôve written a Ruby on Rails application, you‚Äôve probably used has_many:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Album < ActiveRecord::Base

‚Äã has_many ‚Äã:tracks‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

These are both examples of class-level methods that generate code behind the scenes. Because of the way they expand into something bigger, folks sometimes call these kinds of methods macros.

Let‚Äôs create a trivial example and then build it up into something realistic. We‚Äôll start by implementing a simple method that adds logging capabilities to instances of a class. We previously did this using a module. This time we‚Äôll do it using a class-level method. Here‚Äôs the first iteration: metaprogramming/logging_0.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãadd_logging‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãlog‚Äã(msg)

‚Äã $stderr.‚Äãputs‚Äã(‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãTime.‚Äãnow‚Äã.‚Äãstrftime‚Äã(‚Äã"%H:%M:%S: "‚Äã)‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã (‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãmsg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã)"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã add_logging

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã ex.‚Äãlog‚Äã(‚Äã"hello"‚Äã)



Produces:

‚Äã 17:16:20: #<Example:0x0000000104e77610> (hello)

This is a silly piece of code because it includes a very general concern‚Äîlogging‚Äîin a very specific class. But bear with us‚Äîit‚Äôll get better. And we can still learn from it. First, notice that add_logging is a class method‚Äîit‚Äôs defined in the class object‚Äôs singleton class. That means we can call it later in the class definition without an explicit receiver because self is set to the class object inside a class definition.

Then, notice that the add_logging method contains a nested method definition. This inner definition will get executed only when we call the add_logging method. The result is that log will be defined as an instance method of class Example.

Let‚Äôs take one more step. We can define the add_logging method in one class and then use it in a subclass. This works because the singleton class hierarchy parallels the regular class hierarchy. As a result, class methods in a parent class are also available in the child class, as the following example shows: metaprogramming/logging_1.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Logger

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãadd_logging‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãlog‚Äã(msg)

‚Äã $stderr.‚Äãputs‚Äã(‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãTime.‚Äãnow‚Äã.‚Äãstrftime‚Äã(‚Äã"%H:%M:%S: "‚Äã)‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã (‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãmsg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã)"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example < Logger

‚Äã add_logging

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã puts ex.‚Äãlog‚Äã(‚Äã"hello"‚Äã)



Produces:

‚Äã 17:16:20: #<Example:0x00000001049b9718> (hello)

Think back to the two examples at the beginning of this section. Both work in the same way as our add_logging example. attr_accessor is an instance method defined in class Module and so is available in all module and class definitions. has_many is a class method defined in the Base class within the Rails ActiveRecord module and so is available to all classes that subclass ActiveRecord::Base.

This example is still not particularly compelling. It would still be easier to add the log method directly as an instance method of our Logger class. But what happens if we want to construct a different version of the log method for each class that uses it? For example, let‚Äôs add the capability to add a short class-specific identifying string to the start of each log message. We want to be able to say something like this:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Song < Logger

‚Äã add_logging ‚Äã"Song"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Album < Logger

‚Äã add_logging ‚Äã"CD"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

To do this, let‚Äôs define the log method on the fly. We can no longer use a straightforward def‚Ä¶end-style definition‚Äîdoing so won‚Äôt allow us to dynamically determine the method behavior on the fly. Instead, we‚Äôll use one of the cornerstones of Ruby metaprogramming, define_method. This takes the name of a method and a block, defining a method with the given name and with the block as the method body. Any arguments in the block definition become parameters to the method being defined: metaprogramming/logging_2.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Logger

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãadd_logging‚Äã(id_string)

‚Äã define_method(‚Äã:log‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |msg|

‚Äã now = Time.‚Äãnow‚Äã.‚Äãstrftime‚Äã(‚Äã"%H:%M:%S"‚Äã)

‚Äã $stderr.‚Äãputs‚Äã(‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãnow‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã-‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãid_string‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã (‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãmsg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã)"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Song < Logger

‚Äã add_logging ‚Äã"Tune"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Album < Logger

‚Äã add_logging ‚Äã"CD"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã song = Song.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã puts song.‚Äãlog‚Äã(‚Äã"rock on"‚Äã)



Produces:

‚Äã 17:16:20-Tune: #<Song:0x00000001006f8e88> (rock on)

There‚Äôs an important subtlety in this code. The body of the log method contains this line:

‚Äã $stderr.‚Äãputs‚Äã(‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãnow‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã-‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãid_string‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã (‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãmsg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã)"‚Äã)

The value now is a local variable, and msg is the parameter to the block. But id_string is the parameter to the enclosing add_logging method. It‚Äôs accessible inside the block because block definitions create closures, allowing the context in which the block is defined to be carried forward and used when the block is used. In this case, we‚Äôre taking a value from a class-level method and using it in an instance method we‚Äôre defining. This is a common pattern when creating these kinds of class-level macros.

In addition to passing parameters from the class method into the body of the method being defined, we can also use the parameter to dynamically determine the name of the method or methods to create. Here‚Äôs an example that creates a new kind of attr_accessor that logs all assignments to a given instance variable: metaprogramming/accessor_1.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã AttrLogger

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãattr_logger‚Äã(name)

‚Äã attr_reader name

‚Äã

‚Äã define_method(‚Äã:"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã="‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |val|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Assigning ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãval.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã to ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã instance_variable_set(‚Äã"@‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã, val)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example < AttrLogger

‚Äã attr_logger ‚Äã:value‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã ex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã = 123

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Value is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Value is now ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã Assigning 123 to value

‚Äã Value is 123

‚Äã Assigning "cat" to value

‚Äã Value is now cat

Again, we use the fact that the block defining the method body is a closure, accessing the name of the attribute in the log message string. Notice we also make use of the fact that attr_reader is simply a class method‚Äîwe can call it inside our class method to define the reader method for our attribute. There‚Äôs another bit of metaprogramming‚Äîwe use instance_variable_set to set the value of an instance variable. The argument to instance_variable_set is possibly unexpected‚Äîit‚Äôs a string starting with @ containing the name of the variable you want to set. There‚Äôs a corresponding instance_variable_get method that fetches the value of a named instance variable.





Class Methods and Modules


You can define class methods in one class and then use them in subclasses of that class. But it‚Äôs often inappropriate to use subclassing for the kinds of metaprogramming we have been showing because either we already have to subclass some other class or our design aesthetic rebels against making Song a subclass of Logger.

In these cases, you can use a module to hold your metaprogramming implementation. As we‚Äôve seen, using extend inside a class definition will add the methods in a module as class methods to the class being defined: metaprogramming/accessor_2.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãAttrLogger‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãattr_logger‚Äã(name)

‚Äã attr_reader name

‚Äã

‚Äã define_method(‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã="‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |val|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Assigning ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãval.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã to ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã instance_variable_set(‚Äã"@‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã, val)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example

‚Äã ‚Äãextend‚Äã AttrLogger

‚Äã attr_logger ‚Äã:value‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã ex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã = 123

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Value is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Value is now ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã Assigning 123 to value

‚Äã Value is 123

‚Äã Assigning "cat" to value

‚Äã Value is now cat

Things get a little trickier if you want to add both class methods and instance methods into the class being defined. Here‚Äôs one technique that‚Äôs used extensively in the implementation of Rails. This technique makes use of a Ruby hook method, included, which is called automatically by Ruby when you include a module into a class. It is passed the class object of the class being defined: metaprogramming/accessor_3.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãGeneralLogger‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# Instance method to be added to any class that includes us‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãlog‚Äã(msg)

‚Äã puts Time.‚Äãnow‚Äã.‚Äãstrftime‚Äã(‚Äã"%H:%M: "‚Äã) + msg

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# module containing class methods to be added‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãClassMethods‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãattr_logger‚Äã(name)

‚Äã attr_reader name

‚Äã

‚Äã define_method(‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã="‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |val|

‚Äã log ‚Äã"Assigning ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãval.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã to ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã instance_variable_set(‚Äã"@‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã, val)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# extend host class with class methods when we're included‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãincluded‚Äã(host_class)

‚Äã host_class.‚Äãextend‚Äã(ClassMethods)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example

‚Äã ‚Äãinclude‚Äã GeneralLogger

‚Äã

‚Äã attr_logger ‚Äã:value‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã ex.‚Äãlog‚Äã(‚Äã"New example created"‚Äã)

‚Äã ex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã = 123

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Value is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Value is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãex.‚Äãvalue‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã 17:16: New example created

‚Äã 17:16: Assigning 123 to value

‚Äã Value is 123

‚Äã 17:16: Assigning "cat" to value

‚Äã Value is cat

Notice how the included callback is used to extend the host class with the methods defined in the inner module ClassMethods. Ruby on Rails uses this pattern, where a module provides both class and instance methods, extensively. The Rails name for this pattern is a Concern. The Rails Concern implementation also provides shortcut methods so that you don‚Äôt need to add the included hook yourself.

Now, as an exercise, try walking through the previous example in your head. For each line of code, work out the value of self. Master this, and you‚Äôve pretty much mastered this style of metaprogramming in Ruby.





Struct and Subclassing Expressions




In Ruby, we‚Äôve seen that that you can create a superclass relationship with <:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Parent

‚Äã ...

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Child < Parent

‚Äã ...

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

What you might not know is that the thing to the right of the < needn‚Äôt be just a class name; it can be any expression that returns a class object. In this code example, we have the constant Parent. A constant is a simple form of expression, and in this case, the constant Parent holds the class object of the first class we defined.

Ruby comes with a class called Struct, which allows you to define classes that contain simple data attributes. For example, you could write this:

‚Äã Person = Struct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã:name‚Äã, ‚Äã:address‚Äã, ‚Äã:likes‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã dave = Person.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã'Dave'‚Äã, ‚Äã'TX'‚Äã)

‚Äã dave.‚Äãlikes‚Äã = ‚Äã"Programming Languages"‚Äã

‚Äã puts dave

Produces:

‚Äã #<struct Person name="Dave", address="TX", likes="Programming Languages">

The return value from Struct.new(...) is a class object. By assigning it to the constant Person, we can thereafter use Person as if it were any other class.

But say we wanted to change the to_s method of our structure. We could do it by opening up the class and writing the following method:

‚Äã Person = Struct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã:name‚Äã, ‚Äã:address‚Äã, ‚Äã:likes‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Person

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã lives in ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãaddress‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã and likes ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãlikes‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

We can do this more elegantly (although at the cost of an additional class object) by writing this:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Person < Struct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã:name‚Äã, ‚Äã:address‚Äã, ‚Äã:likes‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã lives in ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãaddress‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã and likes ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãlikes‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã dave = Person.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã'Dave'‚Äã, ‚Äã'Texas'‚Äã)

‚Äã dave.‚Äãlikes‚Äã = ‚Äã"Programming Languages"‚Äã

‚Äã puts dave

Produces:

‚Äã Dave lives in Texas and likes Programming Languages

This mechanism is useful enough that Struct provides a shortcut by taking a block in which you can define your own methods on the Struct, like this:

‚Äã Person = Struct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã:name‚Äã, ‚Äã:address‚Äã, ‚Äã:likes‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã lives in ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãaddress‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã and likes ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãlikes‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã dave = Person.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã'Dave'‚Äã, ‚Äã'Texas'‚Äã)

‚Äã dave.‚Äãlikes‚Äã = ‚Äã"Programming Languages"‚Äã

‚Äã puts dave

Produces:

‚Äã Dave lives in Texas and likes Programming Languages





Using Data for Immutable Structs


Structs are often used to create small classes that represent constrained, encapsulated values with minimal behavior, but where it‚Äôs useful to be able to treat the value as one thing, like a point with x and y coordinates. It‚Äôs also sometimes useful to have those values be immutable, meaning that their values cannot be changed. Immutable objects can be easier to reason about and manage when sharing objects across threads.

True immutability in Ruby is hard, but you can achieve it with the Data class. You use Data.define to create a new class with a particular set of attributes. Once you‚Äôve created the new class, you can construct instances using either positional or keyword arguments.

‚Äã LineItem = Data.‚Äãdefine‚Äã(‚Äã:name‚Äã, ‚Äã:price_in_cents‚Äã, ‚Äã:count‚Äã)

‚Äã li_1 = LineItem.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Apple"‚Äã, 105, 3)

‚Äã li_2 = LineItem.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"Orange"‚Äã, ‚Äãcount: ‚Äã2, ‚Äãprice_in_cents: ‚Äã75)

‚Äã

‚Äã li_1.‚Äãname‚Äã ‚Äã# => "Apple"‚Äã

‚Äã li_2.‚Äãprice_in_cents‚Äã ‚Äã# => 75‚Äã

Once you have a Data instance, you can convert it to a hash with to_h.

Since the instances are immutable, you can‚Äôt directly set the attributes, but you can create a new instance using with:

‚Äã LineItem = Data.‚Äãdefine‚Äã(‚Äã:name‚Äã, ‚Äã:price_in_cents‚Äã, ‚Äã:count‚Äã)

‚Äã li_1 = LineItem.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Apple"‚Äã, 105, 3)

‚Äã li_2 = li_1.‚Äãwith‚Äã(‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"Orange"‚Äã)

The new instance is created using any keyword arguments passed to with and the values of the original instance for the attributes that aren‚Äôt included. Including a keyword argument that doesn‚Äôt match an attribute raises an error.





Creating Classes Dynamically


Let‚Äôs look at some Ruby code:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

When we call Example.new, we‚Äôre invoking the method new on the class object Example. This is a regular method call‚ÄîRuby looks for the method new in the class of the object (and the class of Example is Class) and invokes it. So we can also invoke Class#new directly to create a new class dynamically at runtime:

‚Äã some_class = Class.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã puts some_class.‚Äãclass‚Äã





If you pass Class.new a block, that block is used as the body of the class: metaprogramming/class_new.rb ‚Äã some_class = Class.‚Äãnew‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãclass_method‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"In class method"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinstance_method‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"In instance method"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã some_class.‚Äãclass_method‚Äã

‚Äã obj = some_class.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãinstance_method‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã In class method

‚Äã In instance method

By default, these classes will be direct descendants of Object. You can give them a different parent by passing the parent‚Äôs class as a parameter:

‚Äã some_class = Class.‚Äãnew‚Äã(String) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãvowel_movement‚Äã

‚Äã tr ‚Äã'aeiou'‚Äã, ‚Äã'*'‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = some_class.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"now is the time"‚Äã)

‚Äã puts obj.‚Äãvowel_movement‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã n*w *s th* t*m*


How Classes Get Their Names You may have noticed that the classes created by Class.new have no name. However, if you assign the class object for a class with no name to a constant, Ruby automatically names the class after the constant:

‚Äã some_class = Class.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã obj = some_class.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Initial name is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãsome_class.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã SomeClass = some_class

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Then the name is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãsome_class.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"also works via the object: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãclass‚Äã.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Initial name is

‚Äã Then the name is SomeClass

‚Äã also works via the object: SomeClass





We can use these dynamically constructed classes to extend Ruby in interesting ways. For example, here‚Äôs a simple reimplementation of the Ruby Struct class:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚ÄãMyStruct‚Äã(*keys)

‚Äã Class.‚Äãnew‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã attr_accessor *keys

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(hash)

‚Äã hash.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |key, value|

‚Äã instance_variable_set(‚Äã"@‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãkey‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã, value)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã Person = MyStruct ‚Äã:name‚Äã, ‚Äã:address‚Äã, ‚Äã:likes‚Äã

‚Äã dave = Person.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"dave"‚Äã, ‚Äãaddress: ‚Äã‚Äã"TX"‚Äã, ‚Äãlikes: ‚Äã‚Äã"Stilton"‚Äã)

‚Äã chad = Person.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"chad"‚Äã, ‚Äãlikes: ‚Äã‚Äã"Jazz"‚Äã)

‚Äã chad.‚Äãaddress‚Äã = ‚Äã"Berlin"‚Äã

‚Äã
