‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Chad lives in ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãchad.‚Äãaddress‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Dave's name is dave

‚Äã Chad lives in Berlin





Using instance_eval and class_eval


No matter where you are in a Ruby program, self always has a value determined by your location in the code. Sometimes it‚Äôs useful to be able to manage that relationship and change the value of self for a while.

The methods Module#instance_eval, Module#class_eval, and Module#module_eval let you set self to be an arbitrary object, evaluate the code in a block with that object as self, and then reset self:

‚Äã ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã.‚Äãinstance_eval‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Upper case = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãupcase‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Length is ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãlength‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Upper case = CAT

‚Äã Length is 3

Inside the instance_eval block, the variable self is temporarily given the value of the object that received the instance_eval message.

All the _eval methods take a string instead of a block argument, where the string is evaluated by Ruby with the receiver as self. But the string version is considered dangerous, and you shouldn‚Äôt use it. First, it‚Äôs slow‚Äîcalling eval effectively compiles the code in the string before executing it. But, even worse, eval from a string can be dangerous. If there‚Äôs any chance that external data‚Äîstuff that comes from outside your application‚Äîcan wind up inside the string argument to eval, then you have a security hole because that external data may end up containing arbitrary code that your application will blindly execute.

class_eval and instance_eval both set self for the duration of the block. But they differ in the way they set up the environment for method definition. This can make a big difference when the receiver is a class. The method class_eval sets things up as if you were in the body of a class definition, so when the receiver is a class, method definitions will define instance methods in that class:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MyClass

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã MyClass.‚Äãclass_eval‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinstance_method‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"In an instance method"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = MyClass.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãinstance_method‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã In an instance method

In contrast, calling instance_eval acts as if you were working inside the singleton class of self.

So, if the receiver is a class, any methods you define will become singleton methods of that class, which is to say that they become class methods:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MyClass

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã MyClass.‚Äãinstance_eval‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãclass_method‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"In a class method"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã MyClass.‚Äãclass_method‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã In a class method

It might be helpful to remember that class_eval and instance_eval refer to the context in which self is being replaced‚Äîand not the kind of method that‚Äôs defined inside the block. For method definition, class_eval defines instance methods, and instance_eval defines class methods.

Ruby has variants of these methods. instance_exec, class_exec, and module_exec behave identically to their _eval counterparts but only have the block version (that is, they don‚Äôt take a string). Any arguments given to the methods are passed to the block as block parameters. This is an important feature. Using _eval, it‚Äôs impossible to pass an instance variable into a block given to one of the _eval methods‚Äîbecause self is changed by the call, these variables go out of scope. With the _exec form, you can pass them in:

‚Äã @animal = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"dog"‚Äã.‚Äãinstance_exec‚Äã(@animal) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |other|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãother‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã and ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã cat and dog





instance_eval and Constants


When using instance_eval, constants are looked up in the lexical scope in which they were referenced. This (artificial) example shows the behavior:

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãOne‚Äã

‚Äã CONST = ‚Äã"Defined in One"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãeval_block‚Äã(&block)

‚Äã instance_eval(&block)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãTwo‚Äã

‚Äã CONST = ‚Äã"Defined in Two"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãcall_eval_block‚Äã

‚Äã One.‚Äãeval_block‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã CONST

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã Two.‚Äãcall_eval_block‚Äã ‚Äã# => "Defined in Two"‚Äã





instance_eval and Domain-Specific Languages


It turns out that instance_eval has a pivotal role to play in a certain type of domain-specific language (DSL). For example, we might be writing a simple DSL for turtle graphics. In turtle graphics systems, you imagine you have a turtle you can command to move forward n squares, turn left, and turn right. You can also make the turtle raise and lower a pen. If the pen is lowered, a line will be drawn tracing the turtle‚Äôs subsequent movements. Few of these turtles exist in the wild, so we tend to simulate them inside computers. To draw a set of three 5x5 squares, we might write the following:

‚Äã 3.‚Äãtimes‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã forward(8)

‚Äã pen_down

‚Äã 4.‚Äãtimes‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã forward(4)

‚Äã left

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã pen_up

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Yes, the forward(4) is correct in this code. The initial point is always drawn.

Clearly, pen_down, forward, left, and pen_up can be implemented as Ruby methods. But this API has all the methods being called without receivers. For that to work, we have to either be within a class that defines those methods (or is a child of such a class) or have to make the methods global. Neither of those allows for the API to be as simple as we‚Äôve defined it here.

instance_eval comes to the rescue. We can define a class Turtle that defines the various methods we need as instance methods. We‚Äôll also define a walk method, which will execute our turtle DSL, and a draw method to draw the resulting picture:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Turtle

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãleft‚Äã; ... ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãright‚Äã; ... ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãforward‚Äã(n); ... ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãpen_up‚Äã; .. ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãpen_down‚Äã; ... ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãwalk‚Äã(...); ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãdraw‚Äã; ... ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

If we implement walk correctly, we can then write the code like this:

‚Äã turtle = Turtle.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã turtle.‚Äãwalk‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã 3.‚Äãtimes‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã forward(8)

‚Äã pen_down

‚Äã 4.‚Äãtimes‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã forward(4)

‚Äã left

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã pen_up

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã turtle.‚Äãdraw‚Äã

Inside the walk block we want all the methods to have turtle as the receiver, but they have self as the implicit receiver. And as written, self is based on the class or location where this whole block is defined, and that isn‚Äôt the object named turtle.

So, what is the correct implementation of walk? Well, we clearly have to use instance_eval because we want the DSL commands in the block to call the methods in the turtle object. We also have to arrange to pass the block given to the walk method to be evaluated by that instance_eval call. Our implementation looks like this:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãwalk‚Äã(&)

‚Äã instance_eval(&)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Notice how we use Ruby‚Äôs anonymous block syntax to grab the block passed to walk and pass it as-is as a block to instance_eval.

Is this a good use of instance_eval? It depends on the circumstances. The benefit is that the code inside the block looks simple‚Äîyou don‚Äôt have to make the receiver explicit:

‚Äã 4.‚Äãtimes‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã turtle.‚Äãforward‚Äã(4)

‚Äã turtle.‚Äãleft‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

There‚Äôs a drawback, though. Inside the block, scope isn‚Äôt what you might think it is, so this code wouldn‚Äôt work:

‚Äã @size = 4

‚Äã turtle.‚Äãwalk‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã 4.‚Äãtimes‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã turtle.‚Äãforward‚Äã(@size)

‚Äã turtle.‚Äãleft‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Instance variables are looked up in self, and self in the block isn‚Äôt the same as self in the code that sets the instance variable @size. Because of this kind of confusion, you may want to move away from using this style of instance_eval blocks.





Using Hook Methods


In ‚ÄãClass Methods and Modules‚Äã, we defined a method called included in our GeneralLogger module. When this module was included in a class, Ruby automatically invoked this included method, allowing our module to add class methods to the host class.

included is an example of a hook method (sometimes called a callback). A hook method is a method that you write but that Ruby calls from within the interpreter when some particular event occurs. The interpreter looks for these methods by name. If you define a method in the right context with an appropriate name, Ruby will call it when the corresponding event happens.

The methods that can be invoked from within the interpreter are:

Method-related hooks

method_added, method_missing, method_removed, method_undefined, singleton_method_added, singleton_method_removed, singleton_method_undefined



Class and module-related hooks

append_features, const_missing, extend_object, extended, included, inherited, initialize_clone, initialize_copy, initialize_dup



Object marshaling hooks

marshal_dump, marshal_load



Coercion hooks

coerce, induced_from, various to_xxx methods





We won‚Äôt discuss all of them in this chapter‚Äîinstead, we‚Äôll show just a few examples of use.





The inherited Hook


If a class defines a class method called inherited, Ruby will call that method whenever that class is subclassed (that is, whenever any class inherits from the original).

This hook is often used in situations where a base class needs to keep track of its children. For example, an online store might offer a variety of shipping options. Each might be represented by a separate class, and each of these classes could be a subclass of a single Shipping class. This parent class could keep track of all the various shipping options by recording every class that subclasses it. When it comes time to display the shipping options to the user, the application could call the base class, asking it for a list of its children: metaprogramming/shipping.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Shipping ‚Äã# Base class‚Äã

‚Äã @children = [] ‚Äã# this variable is in the class, not instances‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãinherited‚Äã(child)

‚Äã @children << child

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãshipping_options‚Äã(weight, international)

‚Äã @children.‚Äãselect‚Äã { |child| child.‚Äãcan_ship‚Äã(weight, international) }

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MediaMail < Shipping

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãcan_ship‚Äã(_weight, international)

‚Äã !international

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã FlatRatePriorityEnvelope < Shipping

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãcan_ship‚Äã(weight, international)

‚Äã weight < 64 && !international

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã InternationalFlatRateBox < Shipping

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãcan_ship‚Äã(weight, international)

‚Äã weight < 9*16 && international

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Shipping 16oz domestic"‚Äã

‚Äã puts Shipping.‚Äãshipping_options‚Äã(16, ‚Äãfalse‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã\n‚Äã‚ÄãShipping 90oz domestic"‚Äã

‚Äã puts Shipping.‚Äãshipping_options‚Äã(90, ‚Äãfalse‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã\n‚Äã‚ÄãShipping 16oz international"‚Äã

‚Äã puts Shipping.‚Äãshipping_options‚Äã(16, ‚Äãtrue‚Äã)



Produces:

‚Äã Shipping 16oz domestic

‚Äã MediaMail

‚Äã FlatRatePriorityEnvelope

‚Äã

‚Äã Shipping 90oz domestic

‚Äã MediaMail

‚Äã

‚Äã Shipping 16oz international

‚Äã InternationalFlatRateBox

Command interpreters often use this pattern: the base class keeps a track of available commands, each of which is implemented in a subclass.





The method_missing Hook


Earlier, we saw how Ruby executes a method call by looking for the method, first in the object‚Äôs class, then in its superclass, then in that class‚Äôs superclass, and so on. If the method call has an explicit receiver, then private methods are skipped in this search. If the method isn‚Äôt found by the time we run out of superclasses (because BasicObject has no superclass), then Ruby tries to invoke the hook method method_missing on the original object. Again, the same process is followed‚ÄîRuby first looks in the object‚Äôs class, then in its superclass, and so on. Ruby predefines its own version of method_missing in class BasicObject, so typically the search stops there. The built-in method_missing basically raises an exception (either a NoMethodError or a NameError depending on the circumstances).

The key here is that method_missing is simply a Ruby method. We can override it in our own classes to handle calls to otherwise undefined methods in an application-specific way.

The method_missing method has a signature that includes the name of the method being sought and the arguments:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod_missing‚Äã(name, *args, &block)

The name argument receives the name of the method that couldn‚Äôt be found. It‚Äôs passed as a symbol. The args argument is an array of the arguments that were passed in the original call. And the often forgotten block argument will receive any block passed to the original method.

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod_missing‚Äã(name, *args, &block)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Called ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãname‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã with ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãargs.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã and ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãblock‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã wibble

‚Äã wobble 1, 2

‚Äã wurble(3, 4) { stuff }

Produces:

‚Äã Called wibble with [] and

‚Äã Called wobble with [1, 2] and

‚Äã Called wurble with [3, 4] and #<Proc:0x00000001026f73d8 prog.rb:7>

Before we get too deep into the details, we‚Äôll offer a tip about etiquette. There are two main ways that people use method_missing. The first intercepts every use of an undefined method and handles it. The second is more subtle; it intercepts all calls but handles only some of them. In the latter case, it‚Äôs important to forward on the call to a superclass if you decide not to handle it in your method_missing implementation.

In this snippet, imagine there‚Äôs some method called name_handled_by_method_missing? that determines if the name matches whatever criteria we‚Äôre using for dynamic methods:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MyClass < OtherClass

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod_missing‚Äã(name, *args, &block)

‚Äã ‚Äãif‚Äã name_handled_by_method_missing?(name)

‚Äã ‚Äã# handle call‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãelse‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãsuper‚Äã ‚Äã# otherwise pass it on‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãrespond_to_missing?‚Äã(name)

‚Äã name_handled_by_method_missing?(name)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

If you fail to pass on calls that you don‚Äôt handle, your application will silently ignore calls to unknown methods in your class.

It‚Äôs also important to keep the respond_to_missing? method consistent with method_missing. As we mentioned in ‚ÄãDuck Typing‚Äã, respond_to? is used to query what messages an instance expects. Using method_missing changes the list of expected messages, so there‚Äôs a parallel to respond_to? called respond_to_missing? that‚Äôs similarly invoked if respond_to? returns false, as a final check on whether the class actually responds to the message.





Using method_missing to Simulate Accessors


The OpenStruct class is distributed with Ruby. It allows you to write objects with attributes that are created dynamically by assignment.

For example, you could write this:

‚Äã require ‚Äã'ostruct'‚Äã

‚Äã obj = OpenStruct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"Dave"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãaddress‚Äã = ‚Äã"Texas"‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãlikes‚Äã = ‚Äã"Programming"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã lives in ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãaddress‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã and likes ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãlikes‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Dave lives in Texas and likes Programming

Let‚Äôs use method_missing to write our own version of OpenStruct: metaprogramming/open_struct.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã MyOpenStruct < BasicObject

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(initial_values = {})

‚Äã @values = initial_values

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äã_singleton_class‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã << self

‚Äã self

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod_missing‚Äã(name, *args, &block)

‚Äã ‚Äãif‚Äã name[-1] == ‚Äã"="‚Äã

‚Äã base_name = name[0..-2].‚Äãintern‚Äã

‚Äã _singleton_class.‚Äãinstance_exec‚Äã(name) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |name|

‚Äã define_method(name) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |value|

‚Äã @values[base_name] = value

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã @values[base_name] = args[0]

‚Äã ‚Äãelse‚Äã

‚Äã _singleton_class.‚Äãinstance_exec‚Äã(name) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |name|

‚Äã define_method(name) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã @values[name]

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã @values[name]

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãrespond_to_missing?‚Äã(_)

‚Äã ‚Äãtrue‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = MyOpenStruct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"Dave"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãaddress‚Äã = ‚Äã"Texas"‚Äã

‚Äã obj.‚Äãlikes‚Äã = ‚Äã"Programming"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãname‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã lives in ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãaddress‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã and likes ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãlikes‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã Dave lives in Texas and likes Programming

Notice how we base our class on BasicObject. BasicObject is the root of Ruby‚Äôs object hierarchy and contains only a minimal number of methods:

‚Äã p BasicObject.‚Äãinstance_methods‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã [:equal?, :!, :__send__, :==, :!=, :instance_eval, :instance_exec, :__id__]

This is good because it means that our MyOpenStruct class will be able to have attributes such as display or class. If instead, we‚Äôd based MyOpenStruct on class Object, then these names, along with forty-nine others, would‚Äôve been predefined and hence wouldn‚Äôt trigger method_missing.

Notice also another common pattern inside method_missing. The first time we reference or assign a value to an attribute of our object, we access or update the @values hash appropriately. But we also use define_method to define the method that the caller was trying to access. This means that the next time this attribute is used, it‚Äôll use the method and not invoke method_missing. In theory, this will make subsequent calls to the same accessor perform faster. This may or may not be worth the trouble, depending on the access patterns to your object.

Also notice how we had to jump through some hoops to define the method. We want to define the method only for the current object. This means we have to put the method into the object‚Äôs singleton class. Ordinarily, we could do that via a Ruby method called define_singleton_method, but that method is defined in Object, not BasicObject, so it‚Äôs not available to us here.

So we hack it ourselves. We can do that using instance_exec and define_method. But that means we have to use the class << self trick to get the object‚Äôs singleton class. Through an interesting implementation subtlety, define_method will always define an instance method, regardless of whether it‚Äôs invoked via instance_exec or class_exec.

But this code reveals a dark underbelly of using method_missing and BasicObject: metaprogramming/open_struct_flaw.rb ‚Äã obj = MyOpenStruct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äãname: ‚Äã‚Äã"Dave"‚Äã)

‚Äã obj.‚Äãaddress‚Äã = ‚Äã"Texas"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã o1 = obj.‚Äãdup‚Äã

‚Äã o1.‚Äãname‚Äã = ‚Äã"Mike"‚Äã

‚Äã o1.‚Äãaddress‚Äã = ‚Äã"Colorado"‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã code/metaprogramming/open_struct_flaw.rb:41:in `<main>': undefined method `name='

‚Äã for nil (NoMethodError)

‚Äã

‚Äã o1.name = "Mike"

‚Äã ^^^^^^^

The dup method isn‚Äôt defined by BasicObject; it appears in class Object. So when we called dup, it was picked up by our method_missing handler, and we just returned nil (because we don‚Äôt have an attribute called dup yet). We could fix this so that it at least reports an error:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmethod_missing‚Äã(name, *args, &block)

‚Äã ‚Äãif‚Äã name[-1] == ‚Äã"="‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# as before...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãelse‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãsuper‚Äã ‚Äãunless‚Äã @values.‚Äãhas_key?‚Äã name

‚Äã ‚Äã# as before...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

This class now reports an error if we call dup (or any other method) on it. But we still can‚Äôt dup or clone it (or inspect, convert to a string, and so on). Although BasicObject seems like a natural fit for method_missing, you may find it to be more trouble than it‚Äôs worth.





Using method_missing as a Filter


As the previous example showed, method_missing has some drawbacks if you use it to intercept all calls. It‚Äôs probably better to use it to recognize certain patterns of call, passing on those it doesn‚Äôt recognize to its parent class to handle.

An example of this is the dynamic finder facility used to be found in the Ruby on Rails ActiveRecord module. ActiveRecord is the object-relational library in Rails‚Äîit allows you to access relational databases as if they were object stores. One particular feature allows you to find rows that match the criteria of having given values in certain columns. For example, if an Active Record class called Book were mapping a relational table called books and the books table included columns called title and author, you used to be able to write this (it‚Äôs since been deprecated, but we kind of miss it):

‚Äã pickaxe = Book.‚Äãfind_by_title‚Äã(‚Äã"Programming Ruby"‚Äã)

‚Äã daves_books = Book.‚Äãfind_all_by_author‚Äã(‚Äã"Dave Thomas"‚Äã)

Active Record didn‚Äôt predefine all these potential finder methods. Instead, it uses our old friend method_missing. Inside that method, it looks for calls to undefined methods that match the pattern /^find_(all_)?by_(.*)/. It also looks for /^find_or_(initialize|create)_by_(.*)/. If the method being invoked doesn‚Äôt match this pattern or if the fields in the method name don‚Äôt correspond to columns in the database table, Active Record calls super so that a genuine method_missing exception will be generated.





A Metaprogramming Example


Let‚Äôs bring together all the metaprogramming topics we‚Äôve discussed in a final example by writing a module that allows us to trace the execution of methods in any class that mixes the module in. This would let us write the following: metaprogramming/trace_calls_example.rb ‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"trace_calls"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãone‚Äã(arg)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"One called with ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãarg‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex1 = Example.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã ex1.‚Äãone‚Äã(‚Äã"Hello"‚Äã) ‚Äã# no tracing from this call‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Example

‚Äã ‚Äãinclude‚Äã TraceCalls

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtwo‚Äã(arg1, arg2)

‚Äã arg1 + arg2

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ex1.‚Äãone‚Äã(‚Äã"Goodbye"‚Äã) ‚Äã# but we see tracing from these two‚Äã

‚Äã puts ex1.‚Äãtwo‚Äã(4, 5)



Produces:

‚Äã One called with Hello

‚Äã ==> calling one with ["Goodbye"]

‚Äã One called with Goodbye

‚Äã <== one returned nil

‚Äã ==> calling two with [4, 5]

‚Äã <== two returned 9

‚Äã 9

We can see immediately that there‚Äôs a subtlety here. When we mix the TraceCalls module into a class, it has to add tracing to any existing instance methods in that class. It also has to arrange to add tracing to any methods we subsequently add.

Let‚Äôs start with the full listing of the TraceCalls module: metaprogramming/trace_calls.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãTraceCalls‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãincluded‚Äã(klass)

‚Äã klass.‚Äãinstance_methods‚Äã(‚Äãfalse‚Äã).‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |existing_method|

‚Äã wrap(klass, existing_method)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã klass.‚Äãmethod_added‚Äã(method) ‚Äã# note: nested definition‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãunless‚Äã @trace_calls_internal

‚Äã @trace_calls_internal = ‚Äãtrue‚Äã

‚Äã TraceCalls.‚Äãwrap‚Äã(self, method)

‚Äã @trace_calls_internal = ‚Äãfalse‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãwrap‚Äã(klass, method)

‚Äã klass.‚Äãinstance_eval‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã method_object = instance_method(method)

‚Äã

‚Äã define_method(method) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |*args, &block|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"==> calling ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãmethod‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã with ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãargs.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã result = method_object.‚Äãbind_call‚Äã(self, *args, &block)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"<== ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãmethod‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã returned ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãresult.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã result

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã



When we include this module in a class, the included hook method gets invoked. It first uses the instance_methods reflection method to find all the existing instance methods in the host class (the false parameter limits the list to methods in the class itself, and not in its superclasses). For each existing method, the module calls a helper method, wrap, to add some tracing code to it. We‚Äôll talk about wrap shortly.

Next, the included method uses another hook, method_added. This is called by Ruby whenever a method is defined in the receiver. Note that we define this method in the class passed to the included method. This means that the method will be called when methods are added to this host class and not to the module. This is what allows us to include TraceCalls at the top of a class and then add methods to that class‚Äîall those method definitions will be handled by method_added.

Now look at the code inside the method_added method. We have to deal with a potential problem here. As you‚Äôll see when we look at the wrap method, we add tracing to a method by creating a new version of the method that calls the old one. Inside method_added, we call the wrap function to add this tracing. But inside wrap, we‚Äôll define a new method to handle this wrapping, and that definition will invoke method_added again, and then we‚Äôd call wrap again, and so on, until the stack gets exhausted. To prevent this, we use an instance variable and do the wrapping only if we‚Äôre not already doing it.

The wrap method takes a class object and the name of a method to wrap. It finds the original definition of that method (using instance_method) and saves it. It then redefines this method. This new method outputs some tracing and then calls the original, passing in the parameters and block from the wrapper. Note how we call the method by binding the method object to the current instance and then invoking that bound method.

The key to understanding this code, and most metaprogramming code, is to follow the principles we worked out at the start of this chapter‚Äîhow self changes as methods are called and classes are defined and how methods are called by looking for them in the class of the receiver. If you get stuck, do what we do and draw little boxes and arrows. We find it useful to stick with the convention used in this chapter: class links go to the right, and superclass links go up. Given an object, a method call is then a question of finding the receiver object, going right once, and then following the superclass chain up as far as you need to go.





Top-Level Execution Environment


Finally, there‚Äôs one small detail we have to cover to complete the metaprogramming environment. Many times in this book we‚Äôve claimed that everything in Ruby is an object. But we‚Äôve used one thing time and time again that appears to contradict this‚Äîthe top-level Ruby execution environment:

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Hello, World"‚Äã

Not an object in sight. We may as well be writing some variant of Fortran or Basic. But dig deeper, and you‚Äôll come across objects and classes lurking in even the simplest code.

We know that the literal "Hello, World" generates a Ruby String, so that‚Äôs one object. We also know that the bare method call to puts is effectively the same as self.puts. But what is self?

‚Äã self ‚Äã# => main‚Äã

‚Äã self.‚Äãclass‚Äã ‚Äã# => Object‚Äã

At the top level, we‚Äôre executing code in the context of a predefined object, called main. When we define methods using def method_name at the top level, Ruby defines those as (private) instance methods for class Object. This is why methods defined at the top level are available inside all objects. In the following sample, the top-level be_polite method is visible from inside the introduce method of class Person: metaprogramming/top_level.rb ‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãbe_polite‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Why, if it isn't ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãself.‚Äãto_s‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã? So glad to see you."‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Person

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:first_name‚Äã, ‚Äã:last_name‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(first_name, last_name)

‚Äã @first_name = first_name

‚Äã @last_name = last_name

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã = ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãfirst_name‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãlast_name‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãintroduce‚Äã

‚Äã be_polite

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã clark = Person.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Clark"‚Äã, ‚Äã"Kent"‚Äã)

‚Äã p clark.‚Äãintroduce‚Äã



Produces:

‚Äã "Why, if it isn't Clark Kent? So glad to see you."

This is fairly subtle‚Äîsince they are defined in class Object, these methods are available everywhere. And because we‚Äôre in the context of Object, we can use all of Object‚Äôs methods (including those mixed in from Kernel) in function form. This explains why we can call Kernel methods such as puts at the top level (and indeed throughout Ruby); it‚Äôs because these methods are part of every object. Top-level instance variables also belong to this main, top-level object.





What‚Äôs Next


Metaprogramming is one of Ruby‚Äôs sharpest tools. Don‚Äôt be afraid to use it to raise up the level at which you program. But, at the same time, use it only when necessary‚Äîoverly metaprogrammed applications can become pretty obscure pretty quickly.

There‚Äôs one more piece of the Ruby metaprogramming puzzle: reflection, which is how Ruby knows things about the runtime environment. Let‚Äôs take a look.



Copyright ¬© 2024, The Pragmatic Bookshelf.





Chapter 23



Reflection and Object Space




One of the advantages of dynamic languages such as Ruby is the ability to introspect‚Äîto examine aspects of a program from within the program itself. This is also called reflection.

When people introspect, they think about their thoughts and feelings. This is interesting because we‚Äôre using thought to analyze thought. It‚Äôs the same when programs use introspection‚Äîa program can discover the following information about itself:

What objects it contains

Its class hierarchy

The attributes and methods of objects

Information on methods



Armed with this information, we can look at particular objects and decide which of their methods to call at runtime‚Äîeven if the class of the object didn‚Äôt exist when we first wrote the code. We can also start doing clever things, perhaps modifying the program while it‚Äôs running. We‚Äôre going to look at Ruby‚Äôs ObjectSpace, which allows us to reflect on Ruby‚Äôs internals. Later in this chapter, we‚Äôll look at distributed Ruby and marshaling, two reflection-based technologies that let us send objects around the world and through time.





Looking at Objects


Have you ever craved the ability to traverse all the living objects in your program? We have! Ruby has a global object called ObjectSpace that lets you do some fun tricks with the set of objects Ruby is tracking (this means all the objects that have been created and not yet destroyed by garbage collection).

What Is Garbage Collection?



Ruby is a dynamic language, and it doesn‚Äôt require the programmer to manage the memory that the program uses during runtime. Instead, Ruby uses a process called garbage collection. Garbage collection looks for objects that have been allocated into memory but are no longer in scope or are otherwise not accessible. Those objects are released back into the memory heap so that the space can be reused.

We‚Äôre not going to go into the details of how Ruby handles garbage collecting in this book. For one thing, the details change from version to version as newer and better algorithms for identifying discarded objects are developed.



To traverse live objects, Ruby provides the method ObjectSpace.each_object, which takes a block and applies it to each object that ObjectSpace knows about. The method takes an optional argument, which is a class or module, and filters the result to only the objects that are of that class, module, or subclass.

For example, to iterate over all objects of type Complex, you‚Äôd write the following:

‚Äã a = Complex(1, 2)

‚Äã b = Complex(99, -100)

‚Äã ObjectSpace.‚Äãeach_object‚Äã(Complex) { |x| puts x }

Produces:

‚Äã 0+1i

‚Äã 99-100i

‚Äã 1+2i

Where did that extra number (0+1i) come from? We didn‚Äôt define it in our program. Well, the Complex class defines a constant for I, which is the square root of -1. Because we‚Äôre examining all living objects in the system, this object turns up as well.

Let‚Äôs try the same example with different values. This time, they‚Äôre objects of type Integer:

‚Äã a = 102

‚Äã b = 95

‚Äã ObjectSpace.‚Äãeach_object‚Äã(Integer) { |x| p x }

Produces:

‚Äã 9223372036854775807

Neither of the Integers objects we created showed up, although another integer did. That‚Äôs because ObjectSpace doesn‚Äôt know about objects with immediate values. Immediate values are cases where the value is small enough to be stored as part of the internal chip instructions rather than being separately stored in memory. In Ruby, Symbol, true, false, and nil are immediate, and Integer and Float values are also immediate if they are close enough to zero (for machine-specific values of ‚Äúclose enough‚Äù). (Symbol is weird because if you do this exercise with Symbol, you‚Äôll see a lot of symbols with what seems to be internal-facing names, but new ones you create won‚Äôt show up.)

For what it‚Äôs worth, 9223372036854775807 = 2 ** 63 - 1 (we did have to look that up), which suggests it‚Äôs a memory-specific number being generated internally somewhere, possibly being used as a boundary for integer values.





Looking inside Objects


Once you‚Äôve found an interesting object, you may be tempted to find out what it can do. Unlike static languages, where a variable‚Äôs type determines its class, and hence the methods it supports, Ruby supports dynamic objects. You cannot tell exactly what an object can do until you look under its hood (or under its bonnet, for objects created to the east of the Atlantic). We talked about this in ‚ÄãDuck Typing‚Äã.

For instance, we can get a list of all the methods to which an object will respond (these include methods in an object‚Äôs class and that class‚Äôs ancestors) with this:

‚Äã r = 1..10

‚Äã list = r.‚Äãmethods‚Äã

‚Äã list.‚Äãlength‚Äã ‚Äã# => 123‚Äã

‚Äã list[0..3] ‚Äã# => [:last, :exclude_end?, :step, :cover?]‚Äã

We can check to see whether an object responds to a particular method:

‚Äã r = 1..10

‚Äã r.‚Äãrespond_to?‚Äã(‚Äã"frozen?"‚Äã) ‚Äã# => true‚Äã

‚Äã r.‚Äãrespond_to?‚Äã(‚Äã:key?‚Äã) ‚Äã# => false‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"me"‚Äã.‚Äãrespond_to?‚Äã(‚Äã"=="‚Äã) ‚Äã# => true‚Äã

We can get the method object for a particular method:

‚Äã r = 1..10

‚Äã method = r.‚Äãmethod‚Äã(‚Äã:frozen?‚Äã)

‚Äã method.‚Äãcall‚Äã ‚Äã# => true‚Äã

And we can ask for an object‚Äôs class and unique object ID and test its relationship to other classes:

‚Äã num = 1

‚Äã num.‚Äãobject_id‚Äã ‚Äã# => 3‚Äã

‚Äã num.‚Äãclass‚Äã ‚Äã# => Integer‚Äã

‚Äã num.‚Äãkind_of?‚Äã Integer ‚Äã# => true‚Äã

‚Äã num.‚Äãkind_of?‚Äã Numeric ‚Äã# => true‚Äã

‚Äã num.‚Äãinstance_of?‚Äã Integer ‚Äã# => true‚Äã

‚Äã num.‚Äãinstance_of?‚Äã Numeric ‚Äã# => false‚Äã





Looking at Classes


Knowing about objects is one aspect of reflection, but to get the whole picture, you also need to be able to look at classes‚Äîand the methods and constants that they contain.

Looking at the class hierarchy is easy. You can get the parent of any particular class using Class#superclass and its children using Class#subclasses. For classes and modules, the Module#ancestors method lists both superclasses and mixed-in modules:

ospace/relatives.rb

‚Äã klass = Integer

‚Äã print ‚Äã"Inheritance chain: "‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãbegin‚Äã

‚Äã print klass

‚Äã klass = klass.‚Äãsuperclass‚Äã

‚Äã print ‚Äã" < "‚Äã ‚Äãif‚Äã klass

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã ‚Äãwhile‚Äã klass

‚Äã puts

‚Äã p ‚Äã"Ancestors: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãInteger.‚Äãancestors‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã p ‚Äã"Subclasses: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚ÄãInteger.‚Äãsubclasses‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Inheritance chain: Integer < Numeric < Object < BasicObject

‚Äã "Ancestors: [Integer, Numeric, Comparable, Object, Kernel, BasicObject]"

‚Äã "Subclasses: []"

If you want to build a complete class hierarchy, simply run that code for every class in the system. We can use ObjectSpace to iterate over all Class objects like this:

‚Äã ObjectSpace.‚Äãeach_object‚Äã(Class) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |klass|

‚Äã ‚Äã# ...‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã





Looking inside Classes


We can find out a bit more about the methods and constants in a particular object. We can ask for methods by access level, and we can ask for just singleton methods. We can also take a look at the object‚Äôs constants, local, and instance variables:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Demo

‚Äã @@var = 99

‚Äã CONST = 1.23

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprivate_method‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprotected‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãprotected_method‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãpublic‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãpublic_method‚Äã

‚Äã @inst = 1

‚Äã i = 1

‚Äã j = 2

‚Äã local_variables

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã Demo.‚Äãclass_method‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã Demo.‚Äãprivate_instance_methods‚Äã(‚Äãfalse‚Äã) ‚Äã# => [:private_method]‚Äã

‚Äã Demo.‚Äãprotected_instance_methods‚Äã(‚Äãfalse‚Äã) ‚Äã# => [:protected_method]‚Äã

‚Äã Demo.‚Äãpublic_instance_methods‚Äã(‚Äãfalse‚Äã) ‚Äã# => [:public_method]‚Äã

‚Äã Demo.‚Äãsingleton_methods‚Äã(‚Äãfalse‚Äã) ‚Äã# => [:class_method]‚Äã

‚Äã Demo.‚Äãclass_variables‚Äã ‚Äã# => [:@@var]‚Äã

‚Äã Demo.‚Äãconstants‚Äã(‚Äãfalse‚Äã) ‚Äã# => [:CONST]‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã demo = Demo.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã demo.‚Äãinstance_variables‚Äã ‚Äã# => []‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# Get 'public_method' to return its local variables‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# and set an instance variable‚Äã

‚Äã demo.‚Äãpublic_method‚Äã ‚Äã# => [:i, :j]‚Äã

‚Äã demo.‚Äãinstance_variables‚Äã ‚Äã# => [:@inst]‚Äã

You may be wondering what all the false parameters were in the previous code. These reflection methods will by default continue recursively into parent classes, their parents, and so on, up the ancestor chain. Passing in false stops this kind of prying.

Given a list of method names, we may now be tempted to try calling them. Fortunately, that‚Äôs easy with Ruby.





Calling Methods Dynamically


The Object#send method lets you tell any object to invoke a method by name. The first argument is a symbol or a string representing the name, and any remaining arguments are passed along to the method of that name. Let‚Äôs have a look at the following code:

‚Äã ‚Äã"John Coltrane"‚Äã.‚Äãsend‚Äã(‚Äã:length‚Äã) ‚Äã# => 13‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Miles Davis"‚Äã.‚Äãsend‚Äã(‚Äã"sub"‚Äã, ‚Äã/iles/‚Äã, ‚Äã'.'‚Äã) ‚Äã# => "M. Davis"‚Äã

There are two twists to send. First, your class might define its own send method if it wanted to send something somewhere. Ruby provides the __send__ method, defined in BasicObject, which is identical to send and is meant to be used in cases where send might have been overwritten .

Second, the send method doesn‚Äôt enforce method access, meaning that it‚Äôll happily send to a method that is private or protected. You might reasonably think that this undermines the point of having access control in the first place. Ruby provides the related method public_send, which will only send to methods that are public. For most usages, public_send is preferred.

Another way of invoking methods dynamically uses Method objects. A Method object is like a Proc object: it represents a chunk of code and a context in which it executes. In this case, the code is the body of the method, and the context is the object that created the method. Once we have our Method object, we can execute it sometime later by sending it the message call:

‚Äã trane = ‚Äã"John Coltrane"‚Äã.‚Äãmethod‚Äã(‚Äã:length‚Äã)

‚Äã miles = ‚Äã"Miles Davis"‚Äã.‚Äãmethod‚Äã(‚Äã"sub"‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã trane.‚Äãcall‚Äã ‚Äã# => 13‚Äã

‚Äã miles.‚Äãcall‚Äã(‚Äã/iles/‚Äã, ‚Äã'.'‚Äã) ‚Äã# => "M. Davis"‚Äã

You can pass the Method object around as you would any other object, and when you invoke Method#call, the method is run as if you had invoked it on the original object. It‚Äôs like having a C-style function pointer but in a fully object-oriented style.

In languages like JavaScript and Python, mentioning a method without arguments returns the method itself, whereas in Ruby, mentioning the method without arguments calls the method with no arguments, and if you want the method itself, you need to call method.

You can use Method objects wherever you use proc objects. For example, they work with iterators:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãdouble‚Äã(a)

‚Äã 2 * a

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã method_object = method(‚Äã:double‚Äã)

‚Äã [ 1, 3, 5, 7 ].‚Äãmap‚Äã(&method_object) ‚Äã# => [2, 6, 10, 14]‚Äã

Method objects are bound to one particular object, and that object acts as the receiver when the method object is invoked. You can create unbound methods (of class UnboundMethod) by calling instance_method on a class with the name of the method. Subsequently, you can bind the method to one or more objects with bind. The binding creates a new Method object, that is bound to the argument of bind. As with aliases, unbound methods are references to the definition of the method at the time they are created and don‚Äôt reflect later changes:

‚Äã unbound_length = String.‚Äãinstance_method‚Äã(‚Äã:length‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã String

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãlength‚Äã

‚Äã 99

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã str = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã str.‚Äãlength‚Äã ‚Äã# => 99‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã bound_length = unbound_length.‚Äãbind‚Äã(str)

‚Äã bound_length.‚Äãcall‚Äã ‚Äã# => 3‚Äã

The pattern of binding a method and immediately calling it is common enough that Ruby has a shortcut, bind_call. We can replace the last two lines of the previous snippet with the single line unbound_length.bind_call(str). The first argument to bind_call is the object to bind to, and any subsequent arguments are passed to the call.

Because good things come in threes, here‚Äôs yet another way to invoke methods dynamically. The eval method (and its variations such as class_eval, module_eval, and instance_eval) will parse and execute an arbitrary string of legal Ruby source code:

‚Äã trane = ‚Äã%q{"John Coltrane".length}‚Äã

‚Äã miles = ‚Äã%q{"Miles Davis".sub(/iles/, '.')}‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã eval(trane) ‚Äã# => 13‚Äã

‚Äã eval(miles) ‚Äã# => "M. Davis"‚Äã

When using eval, it can be helpful to state explicitly the context in which the expression should be evaluated, rather than using the current context. You obtain a context using Kernel#binding at the desired point:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãget_a_binding‚Äã

‚Äã val = 123

‚Äã binding

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã val = ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã the_binding = get_a_binding

‚Äã eval(‚Äã"val"‚Äã, the_binding) ‚Äã# => 123‚Äã

‚Äã eval(‚Äã"val"‚Äã) ‚Äã# => "cat"‚Äã

The first eval evaluates val in the context of the binding as it was when the method get_a_binding was executing. In this binding, the variable val had a value of 123. The second eval evaluates val in the top-level binding, where it has the value "cat".





Performance Considerations


As we‚Äôve seen in this section, Ruby gives us several ways to invoke an arbitrary method of some object: Object#send, Method#call, and the various flavors of eval.

You may prefer to use any one of these techniques depending on your needs, but be aware that, as the following benchmark shows, eval is significantly slower than the others (or, for optimistic readers, send and call are significantly faster than eval).

‚Äã require ‚Äã"benchmark"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãinclude‚Äã Benchmark

‚Äã

‚Äã test = ‚Äã"Stormy Weather"‚Äã

‚Äã m = test.‚Äãmethod‚Äã(‚Äã:length‚Äã)

‚Äã n = 100000

‚Äã

‚Äã bm(12) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |x|

‚Äã x.‚Äãreport‚Äã(‚Äã"call"‚Äã) { n.‚Äãtimes‚Äã { m.‚Äãcall‚Äã } }

‚Äã x.‚Äãreport‚Äã(‚Äã"send"‚Äã) { n.‚Äãtimes‚Äã { test.‚Äãsend‚Äã(‚Äã:length‚Äã) } }

‚Äã x.‚Äãreport‚Äã(‚Äã"eval"‚Äã) { n.‚Äãtimes‚Äã { eval ‚Äã"test.length"‚Äã } }

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã user system total real

‚Äã call 0.004716 0.000001 0.004717 ( 0.004719)

‚Äã send 0.004930 0.000001 0.004931 ( 0.004934)

‚Äã eval 0.203683 0.001516 0.205199 ( 0.205515)





System Hooks


A hook is a technique that lets you trap some Ruby event, such as object creation. Let‚Äôs take a look at some common Ruby hook techniques.





Intercepting Method Calls


The simplest hook technique in Ruby is to intercept calls to methods in core classes. Perhaps you want to log all the operating system commands your program executes. You could simply use alias_method to rename the system method and replace it with a system method of your own that both logs the command and calls the original Kernel#system method.

For example:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Object

‚Äã ‚Äãalias_method‚Äã ‚Äã:old_system‚Äã, ‚Äã:system‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãsystem‚Äã(*args)

‚Äã old_system(*args).‚Äãtap‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |result|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"system(‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãargs.‚Äãjoin‚Äã(‚Äã', '‚Äã)‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã) returned ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãresult.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã system(‚Äã"date"‚Äã)

‚Äã system(‚Äã"kangaroo"‚Äã, ‚Äã"-hop 10"‚Äã, ‚Äã"skippy"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã Thu Nov 2 17:16:24 CDT 2023

‚Äã system(date) returned true

‚Äã system(kangaroo, -hop 10, skippy) returned nil

But the problem with this technique is that you‚Äôre relying on there not being an existing method called old_system.

A better alternative is to make use of method objects, which are effectively anonymous:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Object

‚Äã old_system_method = instance_method(‚Äã:system‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã define_method(‚Äã:system‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |*args|

‚Äã old_system_method.‚Äãbind_call‚Äã(self, *args).‚Äãtap‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |result|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"system(‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãargs.‚Äãjoin‚Äã(‚Äã', '‚Äã)‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã) returned ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãresult.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã system(‚Äã"date"‚Äã)

‚Äã system(‚Äã"kangaroo"‚Äã, ‚Äã"-hop 10"‚Äã, ‚Äã"skippy"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã Thu Nov 2 17:16:24 CDT 2023

‚Äã system(date) returned true

‚Äã system(kangaroo, -hop 10, skippy) returned nil

You can also achieve a similar behavior by using prepend to insert a module with a method of the same name earlier in the call chain. Within the module‚Äôs methods, calling super calls the host‚Äôs method of the same name. This gives us:

‚Äã ‚Äãmodule‚Äã ‚ÄãSystemHook‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãprivate‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãsystem‚Äã(*args)

‚Äã ‚Äãsuper‚Äã.‚Äãtap‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |result|

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"system(‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãargs.‚Äãjoin‚Äã(‚Äã', '‚Äã)‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã) returned ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãresult.‚Äãinspect‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Object

‚Äã prepend SystemHook

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã system(‚Äã"date"‚Äã)

‚Äã system(‚Äã"kangaroo"‚Äã, ‚Äã"-hop 10"‚Äã, ‚Äã"skippy"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã Thu Nov 2 17:16:24 CDT 2023

‚Äã system(date) returned true

‚Äã system(kangaroo, -hop 10, skippy) returned nil





Object Creation Hooks


Ruby lets you get involved when objects are created. If you can be present when every object is born, you can do all sorts of interesting things with them: you can wrap them, add methods to them, remove methods from them, and add them to containers to implement persistence‚Äîyou name it. We‚Äôll show a simple example here. We‚Äôll add a timestamp to every object as it‚Äôs created. First, we‚Äôll add a timestamp attribute to every object in the system. We can do this by hacking the class Object itself:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Object

‚Äã attr_accessor ‚Äã:timestamp‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Then, we need to hook object creation to add this timestamp. One way to do this is to do our method-renaming trick on Class#new‚Äîthe method that‚Äôs called to allocate space for a new object. The technique isn‚Äôt perfect‚Äîsome built-in objects, such as literal strings, are constructed without calling new‚Äîbut it‚Äôll work just fine for objects we write:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Class

‚Äã old_new = instance_method(‚Äã:new‚Äã)

‚Äã define_method(‚Äã:new‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |*args, **kwargs, &block|

‚Äã result = old_new.‚Äãbind_call‚Äã(self, *args, **kwargs, &block)

‚Äã result.‚Äãtimestamp‚Äã = Time.‚Äãnow‚Äã ‚Äãunless‚Äã result.‚Äãis_a?‚Äã(Time)

‚Äã result

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Finally, we can run a test. We‚Äôll create a couple of objects a few milliseconds apart and check their timestamps:

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Test

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj1 = Test.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã sleep(0.002)

‚Äã obj2 = Test.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã obj1.‚Äãtimestamp‚Äã.‚Äãto_f‚Äã ‚Äã# => 1698963384.31296‚Äã

‚Äã obj2.‚Äãtimestamp‚Äã.‚Äãto_f‚Äã ‚Äã# => 1698963384.315474‚Äã





Tracing Your Program‚Äôs Execution


While we‚Äôre having fun reflecting on all the objects and classes in our programs, let‚Äôs not forget about the humble expressions that make our code actually do things. It turns out that Ruby lets us look at these expressions, too.

First, you can watch the interpreter as it executes code, using the TracePoint class. TracePoint is used to execute a proc while adding all sorts of juicy debugging information whenever a new source line is executed, methods are called, objects are created, and so on.

Here‚Äôs a bit of what TracePoint can do (run this in your own irb window, but be aware that it‚Äôll produce a lot of output):

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Test

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãtest‚Äã

‚Äã a = 1

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã TracePoint.‚Äãtrace‚Äã(‚Äã:line‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |trace_point|

‚Äã p trace_point

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã t = Test.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã t.‚Äãtest‚Äã





How Did We Get Here?


That‚Äôs a fair question‚Ä¶one we ask ourselves regularly. Mental lapses aside, in Ruby you can find out ‚Äúhow you got there‚Äù using the method Kernel#caller, which returns an array of strings representing the current call stack:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãcat_a‚Äã

‚Äã puts caller[0..2]

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãcat_b‚Äã

‚Äã cat_a

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãcat_c‚Äã

‚Äã cat_b

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã cat_c

Produces:

‚Äã prog.rb:6:in `cat_b'

‚Äã prog.rb:10:in `cat_c'

‚Äã prog.rb:13:in `<main>'

In this case, we‚Äôre limiting printing the first three elements of the call stack because beyond that, we get into the depths of irb, which is unlikely to be useful for debugging purposes.

Ruby also provides the method __callee__, which returns the name of the current method.





Source Code


Ruby executes programs from plain old files. You can look at these files to examine the source code that makes up your program using one of a number of techniques.

The special variable __FILE__ contains the name of the current source file. This leads to a fairly short (if cheating) quine‚Äîa program that outputs its own source code:

‚Äã print File.‚Äãread‚Äã(‚Äã__FILE__‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã print File.read(__FILE__)

As we saw in the previous section, the method Kernel#caller returns the call stack as a list. Each entry in this list starts off with a filename, a colon, and a line number in that file. You can parse this information to display the source of the method call. In the following example, we have a main program, main.rb, that calls a method in a separate file, sub.rb. That method, in turn, invokes a block, where we traverse the call stack and write out the source lines involved. Notice the use of a hash of file contents, indexed by the filename.

Here‚Äôs some code that dumps out the call stack, including source information:

ospace/caller/stack_dumper.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãdump_call_stack‚Äã

‚Äã file_contents = {}

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"File Line Source Line"‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"---------------+----+------------"‚Äã

‚Äã caller.‚Äãeach‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |position|

‚Äã match_data = position.‚Äãmatch‚Äã(‚Äã/\A(.*?):(\d+)/‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãnext‚Äã ‚Äãif‚Äã match_data.‚Äãnil?‚Äã

‚Äã file = match_data[1]

‚Äã line = Integer(match_data[2])

‚Äã file_contents[file] ||= File.‚Äãreadlines‚Äã(file)

‚Äã printf(‚Äã"%-15s:%3d - %s"‚Äã,

‚Äã File.‚Äãbasename‚Äã(file),

‚Äã line,

‚Äã file_contents[file][line - 1].‚Äãlstrip‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

The file sub.rb contains a single method:

ospace/caller/sub.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãsub_method‚Äã(v1, v2)

‚Äã main_method(v1 * 3, v2 * 6)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

The following is the main program, which invokes the stack dumper after being called back by the submethod:

ospace/caller/main.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"sub"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"stack_dumper"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmain_method‚Äã(arg1, arg2)

‚Äã dump_call_stack

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã sub_method(123, ‚Äã"cat"‚Äã)

Produces:

‚Äã File Line Source Line

‚Äã ---------------+----+------------

‚Äã main.rb : 5 - dump_call_stack

‚Äã sub.rb : 2 - main_method(v1 * 3, v2 * 6)

‚Äã main.rb : 8 - sub_method(123, "cat")

The SCRIPT_LINES__ constant is closely related to this technique. If a program initializes a constant called SCRIPT_LINES__ with a hash, that hash will receive a new entry for every file subsequently loaded into the interpreter using require or load. The entry‚Äôs key is the name of the file, and the value is the source of the file as an array of strings.





Behind the Curtain: The Ruby VM


If you‚Äôd like to know what Ruby is doing with all that code you‚Äôre writing, you can ask the Ruby interpreter to show you the intermediate code that it‚Äôs executing.

You can ask it to compile the Ruby code in a string or in a file and then disassemble it and even run it. You might wonder if it can dump the opcodes out and later reload them. The answer is no‚Äîthe interpreter has the code to do this, but it‚Äôs disabled because there isn‚Äôt yet an intermediate code verifier for the Ruby interpreter.

Here‚Äôs a trivial example of disassembly:

‚Äã code = RubyVM::InstructionSequence.‚Äãcompile‚Äã(‚Äã'a = 1; puts 1 + a'‚Äã)

‚Äã puts code.‚Äãdisassemble‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã == disasm: #<ISeq:<compiled>@<compiled>:1 (1,0)-(1,17)>

‚Äã local table (size: 1, argc: 0 [opts: 0, rest: -1, post: 0, block: -1, kw: -1@-1,

‚Äã kwrest: -1])

‚Äã [ 1] a@0

‚Äã 0000 putobject_INT2FIX_1_ ( 1)[Li]

‚Äã 0001 setlocal_WC_0 a@0

‚Äã 0003 putself

‚Äã 0004 putobject_INT2FIX_1_

‚Äã 0005 getlocal_WC_0 a@0

‚Äã 0007 opt_plus <calldata!mid:+, argc:1,

‚Äã ARGS_SIMPLE>[CcCr]

‚Äã 0009 opt_send_without_block <calldata!mid:puts, argc:1,

‚Äã FCALL|ARGS_SIMPLE>

‚Äã 0011 leave

Maybe you want to know how Ruby handles #{...} substitutions in strings. Ask the VM:

‚Äã code = RubyVM::InstructionSequence.‚Äãcompile‚Äã(‚Äã'a = 1; puts "a = #{a}."'‚Äã)

‚Äã puts code.‚Äãdisassemble‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã == disasm: #<ISeq:<compiled>@<compiled>:1 (1,0)-(1,23)>

‚Äã local table (size: 1, argc: 0 [opts: 0, rest: -1, post: 0, block: -1, kw: -1@-1,

‚Äã kwrest: -1])

‚Äã [ 1] a@0

‚Äã 0000 putobject_INT2FIX_1_ ( 1)[Li]

‚Äã 0001 setlocal_WC_0 a@0

‚Äã 0003 putself

‚Äã 0004 putobject "a = "

‚Äã 0006 getlocal_WC_0 a@0

‚Äã 0008 dup

‚Äã 0009 objtostring <calldata!mid:to_s, argc:0,

‚Äã FCALL|ARGS_SIMPLE>

‚Äã 0011 anytostring

‚Äã 0012 putobject "."

‚Äã 0014 concatstrings 3

‚Äã 0016 opt_send_without_block <calldata!mid:puts, argc:1,

‚Äã FCALL|ARGS_SIMPLE>

‚Äã 0018 leave

For a full list of the opcodes, print out RubyVM::INSTRUCTION_NAMES.





Marshaling and Distributed Ruby


Ruby features the ability to serialize objects, letting you store them somewhere and reconstitute them when needed. You can use this facility, for instance, to save a tree of objects that represent some portion of the application state‚Äîa document, a CAD drawing, a piece of music, and so on.

Ruby calls this kind of serialization marshaling (think of railroad marshaling yards where individual cars are assembled in sequence into a complete train, which is then dispatched somewhere). Saving an object and some or all of its components is done using the method dump. Typically, you‚Äôll dump an entire object tree starting with some given object. Later, you can reconstitute the object using load.

Here‚Äôs a short example. We have a class Chord that holds a collection of musical notes. We‚Äôd like to save away a particularly wonderful chord so we can e-mail it to a couple hundred of our closest friends so they can load it into their copy of Ruby and savor it too. Let‚Äôs start with the classes for Note and Chord:

ospace/chord.rb

‚Äã Note = Struct.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã:value‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã value.‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Chord

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(arr)

‚Äã @arr = arr

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãplay‚Äã

‚Äã @arr.‚Äãjoin‚Äã(‚Äã"-"‚Äã)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Now we‚Äôll create our masterpiece and use dump to save a serialized version to disk:

ospace/chord.rb

‚Äã c = Chord.‚Äãnew‚Äã(

‚Äã [

‚Äã Note.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"G"‚Äã),

‚Äã Note.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Bb"‚Äã),

‚Äã Note.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Db"‚Äã),

‚Äã Note.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"E"‚Äã)

‚Äã ]

‚Äã )

‚Äã

‚Äã File.‚Äãopen‚Äã(‚Äã"posterity"‚Äã, ‚Äã"w+"‚Äã) ‚Äãdo‚Äã |f|

‚Äã Marshal.‚Äãdump‚Äã(c, f)

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

Finally, our friends read it in and are transported by our creation‚Äôs beauty:

‚Äã chord = Marshal.‚Äãload‚Äã(File.‚Äãopen‚Äã(‚Äã"posterity"‚Äã))

‚Äã chord.‚Äãplay‚Äã ‚Äã# => "G-Bb-Db-E"‚Äã





Custom Serialization Strategy


Not all objects can be dumped. Bindings, procedure objects, instances of class IO, and singleton objects cannot be saved outside the running Ruby environment (a TypeError will be raised if you try). Even if your object doesn‚Äôt contain one of these problematic objects, you may want to take control of object serialization yourself.

Marshal provides the hooks you need. In the objects that require custom serialization, simply implement two instance methods: one called marshal_dump, which writes the object out to a string, and the other called marshal_load, which reads a string that you had previously created and uses it to initialize a newly allocated object.

The instance method marshal_dump should return an object representing the state to be dumped. When the object is subsequently reconstituted using load, the method marshal_load will be called with this object and will use it to set the state of its receiver‚Äîit‚Äôll be run in the context of an allocated but not initialized object of the class being loaded.

For instance, here is a sample class that defines its own serialization. For whatever reasons, Special doesn‚Äôt want to save one of its internal data members, @volatile. The author has decided to serialize the two other instance variables in an array:

ospace/marshal_load.rb

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Special

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(valuable, volatile, precious)

‚Äã @valuable = valuable

‚Äã @volatile = volatile

‚Äã @precious = precious

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmarshal_dump‚Äã

‚Äã [@valuable, @precious]

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãmarshal_load‚Äã(variables)

‚Äã @valuable = variables[0]

‚Äã @precious = variables[1]

‚Äã @volatile = ‚Äã"unknown"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@valuable‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@volatile‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@precious‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = Special.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Hello"‚Äã, ‚Äã"there"‚Äã, ‚Äã"World"‚Äã)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Before: obj = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã data = Marshal.‚Äãdump‚Äã(obj)

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = Marshal.‚Äãload‚Äã(data)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"After: obj = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Before: obj = Hello there World

‚Äã After: obj = Hello unknown World





Using YAML and JSON for Marshaling


The Marshal module is built into the interpreter and uses a binary format to store objects externally. Although fast, this binary format has a couple of disadvantages. If the interpreter changes significantly, the marshal binary format may also change and old dumped files may no longer be loadable. Also, using Marshal assumes that the code on the other end loading the object is also a Ruby program. You might be sending an object across the web on a remote call to another server that might not be in Ruby, and in that case, you‚Äôll want a more general conversion format.

An alternative is to use a less fussy external format, preferably one using text rather than binary files. One option, supplied as a standard library, is YAML.[47] YAML stands for YAML Ain‚Äôt Markup Language, but that hardly seems important. Another common option in the standard library is JavaScript Object Notation, or JSON.[48] In Ruby, the YAML module is an alias for Psych, which is the name of the Ruby YAML parser.

We can adapt our previous marshal example to use YAML. Rather than implement specific loading and dumping methods to control the marshal process, we define the method encode_with, which explicitly sets the values to be saved into its parameter:

ospace/yaml_load.rb

‚Äã require ‚Äã"yaml"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Special

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(valuable, volatile, precious)

‚Äã @valuable = valuable

‚Äã @volatile = volatile

‚Äã @precious = precious

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãencode_with‚Äã(properties)

‚Äã properties[‚Äã"precious"‚Äã] = @precious

‚Äã properties[‚Äã"valuable"‚Äã] = @valuable

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@valuable‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@volatile‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@precious‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = Special.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Hello"‚Äã, ‚Äã"there"‚Äã, ‚Äã"World"‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Before: obj = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã data = YAML.‚Äãdump‚Äã(obj)

‚Äã obj = YAML.‚Äãload‚Äã(data, ‚Äãpermitted_classes: ‚Äã[Special])

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"After: obj = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Before: obj = Hello there World

‚Äã After: obj = Hello World

For security purposes, YAML.load takes a list of classes that you expect to find in the incoming YAML text. The expectation is that you‚Äôre willing to create instances of any of these classes based on data included in the YAML text.

We can take a look at what YAML creates as the serialized form of the object‚Äîit‚Äôs pretty simple:

‚Äã obj = Special.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Hello"‚Äã, ‚Äã"there"‚Äã, ‚Äã"World"‚Äã)

‚Äã puts YAML.‚Äãdump‚Äã(obj)

Produces:

‚Äã Before: obj = Hello there World

‚Äã After: obj = Hello World

‚Äã --- !ruby/object:Special

‚Äã precious: World

‚Äã valuable: Hello

JSON is another commonly used interchange format, and Ruby also has a JSON module that provides JSON loading and dumping. But Ruby‚Äôs JSON module doesn‚Äôt, by itself, parse Ruby objects into Ruby objects; it converts Ruby objects to hash data. If we want to restrict the attributes or convert to and from actual objects, we need to do this ourselves:

ospace/json_load.rb

‚Äã require ‚Äã"json"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã Special

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãinitialize‚Äã(valuable, volatile, precious)

‚Äã @valuable = valuable

‚Äã @volatile = volatile

‚Äã @precious = precious

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã self.‚Äãfrom_json‚Äã(json_string)

‚Äã result = JSON.‚Äãparse‚Äã(json_string)

‚Äã Special.‚Äãnew‚Äã(result[‚Äã"valuable"‚Äã], ‚Äãnil‚Äã, result[‚Äã"precious"‚Äã])

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_json‚Äã

‚Äã JSON.‚Äãdump‚Äã(

‚Äã {

‚Äã ‚Äãprecious: ‚Äã@precious,

‚Äã ‚Äãvaluable: ‚Äã@valuable

‚Äã }

‚Äã )

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãto_s‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@valuable‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@volatile‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äã@precious‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã obj = Special.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äã"Hello"‚Äã, ‚Äã"there"‚Äã, ‚Äã"World"‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Before: obj = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

‚Äã data = obj.‚Äãto_json‚Äã

‚Äã new_obj = Special.‚Äãfrom_json‚Äã(data)

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"After: obj = ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãnew_obj‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Before: obj = Hello there World

‚Äã After: obj = Hello World

Notice that the new object no longer has the volatile attribute, since that was not marshaled.





Distributed Ruby


Because we can serialize an object or a set of objects into a form suitable for out-of-process storage, we can transmit objects from one process to another. Couple this capability with the power of networking, and voil√Ý‚Äîyou have a distributed object system. To save you the trouble of having to write the code, we suggest using Masatoshi Seki‚Äôs Distributed Ruby library, often abbreviated drb or dRuby, which is available as a standard Ruby library at https://github.com/ruby/drb.

Using drb, a Ruby process may act as a server, a client, or both. A drb server acts as a source of objects, while a client is a user of those objects. To the client, it appears that the objects are local, but in reality, the code is still being executed remotely.

A server starts a service by associating an object with a given port. Threads are created internally to handle incoming requests on that port:

ospace/drb/drb_server.rb

‚Äã require ‚Äã"drb"‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãclass‚Äã TestServer

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äãadd‚Äã(*args)

‚Äã args.‚Äãinject‚Äã { |n, v| n + v }

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã server = TestServer.‚Äãnew‚Äã

‚Äã DRb.‚Äãstart_service‚Äã(‚Äã"druby://localhost:9000"‚Äã, server)

A simple drb client simply creates a local drb object and associates it with the object on the remote server; the local object is a proxy:

ospace/drb/drb_client.rb

‚Äã require ‚Äã"drb"‚Äã

‚Äã DRb.‚Äãstart_service‚Äã

‚Äã obj = DRbObject.‚Äãnew‚Äã(‚Äãnil‚Äã, ‚Äã"druby://localhost:9000"‚Äã)

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã"Sum is: ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãobj.‚Äãadd‚Äã(1, 2, 3)‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã

The client connects to the server and calls the method add, which uses the magic of inject to sum its arguments. It returns the result, which the client prints out. You can see that by writing a script that loads the server and then the client.

ospace/drb/drb_integration.rb

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"drb_server"‚Äã

‚Äã require_relative ‚Äã"drb_client"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã#sleep 1‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã Sum is: 6

The initial nil argument to DRbObject indicates that we want to attach to a new distributed object. We could also use an existing object.

Yes, this is a functional distributed object mechanism, but it‚Äôs written in a few hundred lines of Ruby code‚Äîno C, nothing fancy, just plain old Ruby code. Of course, it has no naming service, trader service, or anything like you‚Äôd see in a full-fledged distributed system, but it‚Äôs simple and faster than you might think.





What‚Äôs Next


The important thing to remember about Ruby is that there‚Äôs no big difference between ‚Äúcompile time‚Äù and ‚Äúruntime.‚Äù It‚Äôs all the same. You can add code to a running process. You can redefine methods on the fly, change their scope from public to private, and so on. You can even alter basic types, such as Class and Object. Once you get used to this flexibility, it‚Äôs hard to go back to a static language such as C++ or even to a half-static language such as Java.

But then, why would you want to do that?

Now it‚Äôs time to take a look at Ruby‚Äôs syntax in a more structured way.





Footnotes


[47]

http://www.yaml.org



[48]

https://www.json.org/json-en.html

Part 4

Ruby Language Reference



This part looks at the Ruby language from the bottom up. Most of

what appears here is the syntax and semantics of the Ruby language. The extensive library of classes and modules will mostly be covered in Part V. However, Ruby‚Äôs syntax and the library are closely entangled‚Äîliteral values are part of syntax, but they create objects in the library, so we‚Äôll cover parts of the library as needed to explain the syntax.





Chapter 24



Language Reference: Literal Types and Expressions




So far, we‚Äôve given a narrative look at how Ruby works. In this chapter, we‚Äôre going to follow more of a reference structure to discuss Ruby‚Äôs syntax as it concerns literal types and expressions. In the next chapter, we‚Äôll cover syntax relating to objects and classes.





Source Layout


Ruby is a line-oriented language. Ruby expressions and statements are terminated at the end of a line unless the parser can determine that the statement is incomplete. Some examples are when the last token on a line is an operator or comma, or when an open delimiter, such as a parenthesis, square bracket, or curly brace has not been closed. A backslash at the end of a line also tells Ruby to continue the expression onto the next line:

‚Äã ‚Äã# no backslash '\' needed -- ends with an operator‚Äã

‚Äã d = 4 + 5 +

‚Äã 6 + 7

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# no backslash '\' needed -- has an unclosed parenthesis‚Äã

‚Äã e = (4 + 5

‚Äã + 6 + 7)

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# backslash '\' needed -- ends with a number‚Äã

‚Äã f = 8 + 9 \

‚Äã + 10

A semicolon can be used to separate multiple expressions on a line:

‚Äã a = 1

‚Äã b = 2; c = 3

Comments start with # and run to the end of the physical line. Comments are ignored during syntax analysis.

The first line of a multiline comment starts with =begin, and the last line starts with =end. The lines in-between are ignored by Ruby and may be used to comment out sections of code or to embed documentation. The =begin and =end markers can‚Äôt be indented; they must start in the first column of a line, like this:

‚Äã ‚Äã=begin‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãthis is‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãall a‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãmultiline comment‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã=end‚Äã

Ruby uses comment syntax at the top of a file for directives that affect how a file is parsed. These are often called ‚Äúmagic comments,‚Äù and they must appear in the file before the first line of uncommented Ruby code. The syntax of these magic comments is # directive: value.

If you have more than one magic comment, you may put them on separate lines, as long as all of them come before the first line of Ruby code:

‚Äã ‚Äã# frozen_string_literal: true‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã# encoding: big5‚Äã

You may also put them on a single line, delimited by semicolons and surrounded by -*-, like this:

‚Äã -*- ‚Äãfrozen_string_literal: ‚Äã‚Äãtrue‚Äã; ‚Äãencoding: ‚Äãbig5 -*-

This syntax is based on the file configuration syntax used by the Emacs editor.

The following table lists the magic comment directives Ruby supports:



* * *



Table 10. Magic comment directives

CommentDefinition

coding

Synonym of encoding.



encoding

The default encoding used for string literals in the file, and the encoding of the source code as a whole. The default is utf-8.



frozen_string_literal

If true, when string literals are loaded during parsing, they are automatically frozen to make them immutable. String literals with interpolation are never frozen. The default is false.



sharable_constant_value

This directive changes the behavior of immutable constants to allow them to be shared by ractors. The default value is none, in which case constants aren‚Äôt frozen and can‚Äôt be shared with ractors. With the value literal, constants that are assigned literal values are frozen and can be shared. With the value experimental_copy, constants are automatically copied when shared with ractors, and with the value experimental_everything, all constant values are sharable. This directive is experimental and likely to change. Unlike the other magic comments, this directive may be used multiple times in a file and is applicable until the next instance of this directive or the end of the scope in which it‚Äôs declared.



warn_indent

If true, mismatched indentation in the source code triggers a warning when the file is loaded. The default is false, unless Ruby is run with the -w switch, in which case the default is true.





* * *



Ruby source files are assumed by default to be written with a UTF-8 encoding, but this can be changed with the encoding magic comment.

You can pipe programs to the Ruby interpreter‚Äôs standard input stream:

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãecho‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã'puts "Hello"'‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã|‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã

If Ruby comes across a line anywhere in the source containing just __END__, with no leading or trailing whitespace, it treats that line as the end of the program, and any subsequent lines won‚Äôt be treated as program code. Those subsequent lines are treated as data, and they can be read into the running program using the global IO object DATA. The DATA object only contains the subsequent lines from the main file of the program, and the lines after __END__ in other files will be ignored.

‚Äã DATA.‚Äãeach_line‚Äã ‚Äãdo‚Äã |line|

‚Äã p line

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã__END__‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãline one‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãline two‚Äã

Produces:

‚Äã "line one\n"

‚Äã "line two\n"





BEGIN and END Blocks


Every Ruby source file can declare blocks of code to be run as the file is being loaded (the BEGIN blocks) and after the program has finished executing (the END blocks):

BEGIN { begin code } END { end code }

A program may include multiple BEGIN and END blocks. BEGIN blocks are executed in the order they are encountered. END blocks are executed in reverse order.

These blocks are admittedly a little obscure. They seem mostly to be used on the command line for one-line commands to allow initialization or teardown around the actual one-line command.

‚Äã ‚Äã$ ‚Äã‚Äãruby‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã-ne‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã"BEGIN {result = ""}; END {p result}; result += gets.upcase"‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äãtestfile‚Äã

With the -n acting as a while loop and the blocks, this code is equivalent to the following:

‚Äã result = ‚Äã""‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãwhile‚Äã gets

‚Äã result << gets.‚Äãupcase‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã p result

Because gets is used as the while condition, this code prints every other line of the file.





Unicode in Syntax


Ruby allows you to use Unicode characters as variable and method names, as in the following example:

‚Äã ‚Äãdef‚Äã ‚Äã‚àë‚Äã(*args)

‚Äã args.‚Äãsum‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äãend‚Äã

‚Äã

‚Äã puts ‚Äã‚àë‚Äã(1, 3, 5, 9)

Produces:

‚Äã 18

This can lead to some pretty obscure and hard-to-use code. (For example, is the summation character in the previous code a real summation, \u2211, or a Greek sigma, \u03a3?) Just because we can do something doesn‚Äôt mean we necessarily should.





Ruby Literals


Ruby has special syntax for scalar values that are booleans, numbers, lambdas, ranges, regular expressions, strings, symbols, and array and hash collections. Although all types in Ruby are implemented as classes, these types have special syntax for creating literal values of them. Lambda literals will be covered in ‚ÄãProc Objects‚Äã; we‚Äôre going to discuss the rest here.





Boolean Literals


Ruby provides the literal values true and false. The true value is the instance of the singleton class TrueClass and represents a true value in logical expressions. The false value is the instance of the singleton class FalseClass and represents a false value in logical expressions. The literal value nil is the only instance of the singleton NilClass and also represents a false value in logical expressions.

The only logically false values in Ruby are false and nil. You‚Äôll sometimes see the term ‚Äúfalsey‚Äù used to cover both values. All other values, including empty strings and arrays, are logically true. These are sometimes called the ‚Äútruthy‚Äù values. Currently, Ruby doesn‚Äôt have a way to create a new value that will behave as logically false.





Integer and Floating-Point Numbers


Ruby integers are objects of class Integer. Integer objects hold integers that fit within the native machine word minus 1 bit. The internal storage of integers changes depending on the size of the number, but for nearly every purpose in Ruby, that internal detail is abstracted away from you.

Integers are written using an optional leading sign and an optional base indicator (0 or 0o for octal, 0d for decimal, 0x for hex, or 0b for binary), followed by a string of digits in the appropriate base. Underscore characters are ignored in the digit string.

‚Äã 123456 => 123456

‚Äã 0d123456 => 123456

‚Äã 123_456 => 123456 ‚Äã# - underscore ignored‚Äã

‚Äã -543 => -543 ‚Äã# - negative number‚Äã

‚Äã 0xaabb => 43707 ‚Äã# - hexadecimal‚Äã

‚Äã 0377 => 255 ‚Äã# - octal‚Äã

‚Äã 0o377 => 255 ‚Äã# - octal‚Äã

‚Äã -0b10_1010 => -42 ‚Äã# - binary (negated)‚Äã

‚Äã 123_456_789_123_456_789 => 123456789123456789 ‚Äã# Big number internal storage‚Äã

A numeric literal with a decimal point and/or an exponent is turned into a Float object, corresponding to the native architecture‚Äôs double data type. You must follow the decimal point with a digit; if you write 1.e3, Ruby tries to invoke the method e3 on the Fixnum 1. You must place at least one digit before the decimal point.

‚Äã 12.34 ‚Äã# => 12.34‚Äã

‚Äã -0.1234e2 ‚Äã# => -12.34‚Äã

‚Äã 1234e-2 ‚Äã# => 12.34‚Äã





Rational and Complex Numbers


Classes that support rational numbers (ratios of integers) and complex numbers are built into the Ruby interpreter. Rational numbers have a literal syntax with an expression followed by the letter r:

‚Äã 2r ‚Äã# => (2/1)‚Äã

‚Äã 3/4r ‚Äã# => (3/4)‚Äã

‚Äã -2/5r ‚Äã# => (-2/5)‚Äã

‚Äã 2/-5r ‚Äã# => (-2/5)‚Äã

‚Äã 1.4r ‚Äã# => (7/5)‚Äã

‚Äã 3/9r ‚Äã# => (1/3)‚Äã

Complex numbers are integers with an i suffix for the complex part:

‚Äã 2i ‚Äã# => (0+2i)‚Äã

‚Äã 4 + 3i ‚Äã# => (4+3i)‚Äã

A number can be rational and imaginary, but the r needs to come before the i in the suffix list.





Strings


Ruby provides a number of mechanisms for creating literal strings. Each generates objects of type String. The different mechanisms vary in terms of how a string is delimited and how much substitution is done on the literal‚Äôs content. Literal strings are encoded using the source encoding of the file that contains them.



Single-quoted string literals (‚Äô_stuff_‚Äô and %q/_stuff_/) undergo the least substitution. They convert the sequence \\ into a single backslash, and a backslash can be used to escape the single quote or the string delimiter. All other backslashes appear literally in the string.

‚Äã ‚Äã'hello'‚Äã ‚Äã# => hello‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã'a backslash \'\\\''‚Äã ‚Äã# => a backslash '\'‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%q/simple string/‚Äã ‚Äã# => simple string‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%q(nesting (really) works)‚Äã ‚Äã# => nesting (really) works‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%q(escape a‚Äã‚Äã\)‚Äã‚Äã with backslash)‚Äã ‚Äã# => escape a) with backslash‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%q no_blanks_here ‚Äã; ‚Äã# => no_blanks_here‚Äã



Double-quoted strings ("_stuff_", %Q/_stuff_/, and %/_stuff_/) undergo additional substitutions. Refer to the table shown.



* * *



Table 11. Substitutions in double-quoted strings

#{code}

Tab (0x09)

* * *



Here are some examples:

‚Äã a = 123

‚Äã ‚Äã"‚Äã‚Äã\123‚Äã‚Äãmile"‚Äã ‚Äã# => Smile‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Greek pi: ‚Äã‚Äã\u‚Äã‚Äã03c0"‚Äã ‚Äã# => Greek pi: œÄ‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Greek ‚Äã‚Äã\u‚Äã‚Äã{70 69 3a 20 3c0}"‚Äã ‚Äã# => Greek pi: œÄ‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Say ‚Äã‚Äã\"‚Äã‚ÄãHello‚Äã‚Äã\"‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã ‚Äã# => Say "Hello"‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%Q!"I said 'nuts'‚Äã‚Äã\!‚Äã‚Äã," I said!‚Äã ‚Äã# => "I said 'nuts'!," I said‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%Q{Try ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãa + 1‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã, not ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãa - 1‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã}‚Äã ‚Äã# => Try 124, not 122‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%<Try #{a + 1}, not #{a - 1}>‚Äã ‚Äã# => Try 124, not 122‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã"Try ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãa + 1‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã, not ‚Äã‚Äã#{‚Äãa - 1‚Äã}‚Äã‚Äã"‚Äã ‚Äã# => Try 124, not 122‚Äã

‚Äã ‚Äã%{ #{ a = 1; b = 2; a + b } }‚Äã ‚Äã# => 3‚Äã

Last, and probably least (in terms of usage), you can get the string corresponding to an ASCII character by preceding that character with a question mark, where ‚Äúa‚Äù can represent any ASCII character. Refer to the following table:



* * *



Table 12. ASCII character expressions

?a

a

ASCII character

?\n

\n

newline (0x0a)

?\C-a

\u0001

?\M-a

?\M-\C-a
